topics.title: Data Mining - Concepts and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST525 - DATA MINING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Data Mining - Concepts and Applications ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Data Mining (DM)** is the nontrivial process of identifying valid, novel, potentially useful, and ultimately understandable patterns in large volumes of structured, semi-structured, or unstructured data, using techniques drawn from statistics, artificial intelligence, machine learning, and database systems. The widely cited operational definition (Fayyad, Piatetsky-Shapiro & Smyth, 1996) treats DM as a **step within the broader Knowledge Discovery in Databases (KDD) process**, not as a synonym for it. KDD refers to the end-to-end pipeline that converts raw data into actionable knowledge, whereas DM refers specifically to the *algorithmic extraction* stage that sits at the heart of that pipeline. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Module 1 Anchors to Remember:** > - DM ≠ KDD. DM is one *step* of KDD. > - DM is also called **Knowledge Mining from Data (KMD)** or **Knowledge Extraction**. > - When data is too large for traditional DBMS, we enter the **Big Data / Data Mining** regime. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Gold Panning in a Data River" Imagine standing at the mouth of a mountain river. Millions of pebbles, sand, and silt flow past you every second. Hidden among this ordinary sediment are a few rare **gold nuggets** — small, brilliant, and extremely valuable. A gold panner does three things: 1. **Sieves** away obvious non-gold material (data cleaning / preprocessing). 2. **Swirls** the remaining material in a pan so that gold — being denser — settles (the mining algorithm). 3. **Inspects** the residue under a lens to confirm what was found (pattern evaluation / interpretation). Data Mining works the same way. The "river" is the **data warehouse / data lake**; the "panning tools" are **algorithms** (decision trees, k-means, Apriori, etc.); and the "gold nuggets" are **non-obvious, actionable patterns** that improve business, science, or decision-making. ### 1.3 Why Data Mining Became Indispensable Three converging forces pushed DM from academic curiosity to industrial necessity: | Force | Description | Engineering Implication | |---|---|---| | **Data Explosion** | Storage cost fell from ~$10/MB (1980s) to <$0.00003/GB (2024) | Terabytes-to-petabytes of raw data accumulates passively | | **Computational Power** | Multi-core CPUs, GPUs, distributed frameworks (Hadoop, Spark) | Algorithms that were "impossible" in 1995 are now tractable | | **Competitive Pressure** | "Knowing your customer" became a survival skill | Hidden patterns = direct revenue or cost advantage | > [!TIP] > The phrase **"we are drowning in data but starving for knowledge"** is the classic one-liner used in KTU board exams to justify the existence of Data Mining. Use it verbatim if asked "Why Data Mining?". ### 1.4 Data Mining vs. KDD vs. Machine Learning vs. Data Science > [!IMPORTANT] > These four terms are **NOT interchangeable**. Examiners love this distinction. - **KDD**: Entire workflow — selection, cleaning, integration, transformation, mining, evaluation, presentation. - **Data Mining**: The *pattern-extraction* step inside KDD. - **Machine Learning (ML)**: A *toolbox of algorithms* (supervised, unsupervised, reinforcement) that DM borrows from. - **Data Science**: A broader interdisciplinary field encompassing statistics, ML, DM, data engineering, and domain expertise to solve data-driven problems end-to-end. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The KDD Pipeline (Hierarchical Breakdown) The KDD process is the operational backbone of every Data Mining exercise. Each step has a clear input, output, and quality checkpoint. 1. **Data Selection** — Identify the target dataset(s) relevant to the analytical goal. 2. **Data Preprocessing** — Handle missing values, noise, outliers, and inconsistencies. 3. **Data Integration** — Merge data from heterogeneous sources (DBMS, files, APIs, streams). 4. **Data Transformation** — Normalize, aggregate, discretize, or construct new features (e.g., dimensionality reduction via PCA). 5. **Data Mining** — Apply intelligent methods to extract patterns. 6. **Pattern Evaluation** — Use interestingness measures (support, confidence, lift, accuracy) to filter genuinely useful patterns. 7. **Knowledge Presentation** — Visualize and report findings to stakeholders (dashboards, reports, BI tools). > [!NOTE] > Steps 1–4 and 6–7 are often called *data pre/post-processing*; step 5 alone is **Data Mining**. Memorize this mapping for 3-mark questions. ### 2.2 Taxonomy of Data Mining Functionalities Data Mining functionalities split into two super-categories based on the *kind* of output they produce. **A. Descriptive Mining** — characterizes the general properties of the data. - **Concept/Class Description**: Summarization (e.g., "customers who churn are mostly aged 25–34 with monthly bills above ₹1,500"). - **Frequent Patterns**: Itemsets, subsequences, substructures. - **Association Rules**: "If A ⇒ B" (market-basket analysis). - **Clustering**: Grouping records by similarity without labels. - **Outlier/Anomaly Detection**: Identifying rare or abnormal observations. **B. Predictive Mining** — performs inference on new data. - **Classification**: Predict a categorical label (spam / not-spam). - **Regression**: Predict a continuous value (house price, temperature). - **Prediction / Time-Series Forecasting**: Use historical values to forecast future values. - **Deviation Analysis**: Compare observed vs. expected values. ### 2.3 Classification of Data Mining Systems > [!IMPORTANT] > A common 7-mark question asks to "Classify data mining systems along multiple dimensions." | Dimension | Possible Categories | |---|---| | **Database Type** | Relational, transactional, object-relational, data warehouse, spatial, time-series, text, multimedia, legacy, heterogeneous | | **Knowledge Mined** | Concept description, association, classification, clustering, trend analysis, deviation, similarity | | **Techniques Used** | Machine learning, neural networks, statistics, visualization, database-oriented, OLAP, rough sets, fuzzy sets | | **Application Adapted** | Finance, retail, telecom, bioinformatics, healthcare, intrusion detection | | **Data Analysis Type** | DBMS-driven, data warehouse-driven, real-time vs. offline | ### 2.4 The Data to Be Mined — Objects, Attributes, and Data Quality - **Data Object** (record, tuple, point, sample, observation) — the unit of analysis. - **Attribute** (field, variable, feature, dimension) — a property of an object. > [!NOTE] > **Attribute Types Matter for Algorithm Choice:** > - **Nominal / Categorical** — values are just labels (e.g., color = red/blue/green). No order. > - **Binary** — special case of nominal with 2 values (symmetric or asymmetric). > - **Ordinal** — values have an order but magnitude is unknown (e.g., small, medium, large). > - **Numeric** — quantitative. > - **Interval-scaled**: differences are meaningful, ratios are not (e.g., temperature in °C). > - **Ratio-scaled**: both differences and ratios are meaningful (e.g., age, weight, length). **Data Quality Dimensions** (each can break a mining pipeline): - **Noise** — random error or variance in measurement. - **Outliers** — values that deviate significantly from the norm. - **Missing Values** — common, especially in survey/healthcare data. - **Duplicate Records** — entity-resolution problem. - **Bias** — systematic distortion from sampling or measurement. - **Inconsistent Values** — same entity recorded differently across sources. ### 2.5 Applications of Data Mining (Domain-Wise) > [!TIP] > For "List any 6 applications of Data Mining" type questions, cover **at least one from each of the following 8 domains** — examiners reward breadth. 1. **Retail & E-Commerce** — Market basket analysis, recommendation engines, customer churn, dynamic pricing. 2. **Banking & Finance** — Credit scoring, fraud detection, money-laundering pattern discovery, risk modeling. 3. **Healthcare & Bioinformatics** — Disease outbreak prediction, drug response, gene expression clustering, image diagnostics. 4. **Telecommunications** — Call detail record (CDR) analysis, churn prediction, network anomaly detection. 5. **Insurance** — Claim fraud detection, risk profiling, premium optimization. 6. **Manufacturing & Industry 4.0** — Predictive maintenance, quality control, supply-chain optimization. 7. **Web, Text & Social Media** — Sentiment analysis, clickstream analysis, spam detection, community detection, trending topic discovery. 8. **Government & Defense** — Counter-terrorism pattern detection, tax-evasion detection, smart-city analytics. ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the symbol, the formula, and the **range** of each metric. Examiners frequently test interpretation of these ranges. | Metric | Formula | Range | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | **Support (X)** | $\text{sup}(X) = \dfrac{\text{count}(X)}{N}$ | $[0,1]$ | Fraction of transactions containing itemset X | | **Confidence (X→Y)** | $\text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\text{sup}(X \cup Y)}{\text{sup}(X)}$ | $[0,1]$ | Reliability of the rule; P(Y \vert X) | | **Lift (X→Y)** | $\text{lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y)}{\text{sup}(Y)} = \dfrac{\text{sup}(X \cup Y)}{\text{sup}(X)\,\text{sup}(Y)}$ | $[0,\infty)$ | >1 ⇒ positive correlation; =1 ⇒ independence; <1 ⇒ negative | | **Leverage** | $\text{lev}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \text{sup}(X \cup Y) - \text{sup}(X)\text{sup}(Y)$ | $[-1,1]$ | Magnitude of deviation from independence | | **Conviction** | $\text{conv}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{1 - \text{sup}(Y)}{1 - \text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y)}$ | $[0,\infty)$ | ∞ for logical rules; 1 for independence | | **Entropy** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $[0, \log_2 c]$ | Impurity of a set S; 0 = pure | | **Gini Index** | $\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^{2}$ | $[0, 1 - \tfrac{1}{c}]$ | Impurity measure for CART | | **Information Gain** | $\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in A} \dfrac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | $\geq 0$ | Reduction in entropy from splitting on attribute A | | **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Overall correct predictions | | **Precision** | $\text{P} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0,1]$ | How many predicted-positives are actually positive | | **Recall (Sensitivity / TPR)** | $\text{R} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Coverage of actual positives | | **Specificity (TNR)** | $\text{Spec} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | $[0,1]$ | Coverage of actual negatives | | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $[0,1]$ | Harmonic mean of P and R | | **Minkowski Distance** | $d(x, y) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^{p} \right)^{\tfrac{1}{p}}$ | $\geq 0$ | General Lp metric (L1=Manhattan, L2=Euclidean) | | **Cosine Similarity** | $\text{cos}(x, y) = \dfrac{x \cdot y}{\vert x \vert \, \vert y \vert}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Angular similarity, common in text mining | > [!NOTE] > In the table above, every absolute value or set-cardinality uses $\vert \cdot \vert$ (math mode) — *not* the markdown table separator. This prevents the table from breaking visually in the rendered output. ### 2.7 Why This Equation Set Matters in Practice The **Support–Confidence–Lift** triad is the heartbeat of association rule mining (Agrawal & Srikant, 1994). Support filters frequent itemsets; confidence measures rule strength; lift removes the statistical "noise" of items that are *individually* popular but *unrelated* (e.g., bread and milk both sell often but are purchased together for *independent* reasons). In **classification**, the Accuracy–Precision–Recall–F1 quartet is non-negotiable for any imbalanced-class problem (e.g., fraud detection, where frauds are 0.01% of transactions — accuracy alone becomes meaningless). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Support, Confidence, Lift, Leverage Consider a small transactional database $D$ with $N = 5$ transactions from a grocery store. | TID | Items Bought | |---|---| | T1 | {Bread, Butter, Milk} | | T2 | {Bread, Butter} | | T3 | {Milk, Eggs} | | T4 | {Bread, Butter, Milk, Eggs} | | T5 | {Butter, Milk} | **Step 1 — Compute the support of single items.** $$\text{sup}(\text{Bread}) = \frac{\vert \{T1, T2, T4\} \vert}{5} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60$$ $$\text{sup}(\text{Butter}) = \frac{\vert \{T1, T2, T4, T5\} \vert}{5} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80$$ $$\text{sup}(\text{Milk}) = \frac{\vert \{T1, T3, T4, T5\} \vert}{5} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80$$ $$\text{sup}(\text{Eggs}) = \frac{\vert \{T3, T4\} \vert}{5} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.40$$ These are the **counts of transactions containing the item divided by total transactions**. **Step 2 — Compute the support of the itemset {Bread, Butter}.** Transactions containing *both* Bread and Butter are $\{T1, T2, T4\}$, so: $$\text{sup}(\{Bread, Butter\}) = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60$$ **Step 3 — Confidence of the rule "Bread ⇒ Butter".** $$\text{conf}(Bread \Rightarrow Butter) = \frac{\text{sup}(Bread \cup Butter)}{\text{sup}(Bread)} = \frac{0.60}{0.60} = 1.00$$ This means *every* transaction that contains Bread also contains Butter in this database. **Step 4 — Lift of the rule "Bread ⇒ Butter".** $$\text{lift}(Bread \Rightarrow Butter) = \frac{\text{conf}(Bread \Rightarrow Butter)}{\text{sup}(Butter)} = \frac{1.00}{0.80} = 1.25$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation.** Because $\text{lift} = 1.25 > 1$, the rule exhibits a **positive correlation** — buying Bread increases the probability of buying Butter by 25% over the baseline Butter rate of 80%. This is a *useful* rule worth recommending (e.g., place them together, bundle discount). **Step 6 — Compute Leverage to cross-check.** $$\text{lev}(Bread \Rightarrow Butter) = 0.60 - (0.60 \times 0.80) = 0.60 - 0.48 = 0.12$$ A leverage of $+0.12$ confirms positive co-occurrence. If leverage were 0, the items would be statistically independent; if negative, they would inhibit each other. > [!TIP] > Examiners love asking the same rule with two different thresholds (e.g., min\_sup = 0.5, min\_conf = 0.7) and asking which rules survive. Always run support first (cheaper), then confidence. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Entropy and Information Gain Suppose we have a dataset $S$ of 14 tuples about a tennis match. Of these, **9 are "Yes" (Play = Yes)** and **5 are "No" (Play = No)**. We wish to evaluate splitting $S$ on the attribute **Outlook** which has three branches: Sunny (5 tuples), Overcast (4 tuples), Rain (5 tuples). **Step 1 — Compute the entropy of the full set $S$.** Using $c = 2$ classes with $p_{\text{Yes}} = 9/14$ and $p_{\text{No}} = 5/14$: $$H(S) = -\frac{9}{14}\log_2\frac{9}{14} - \frac{5}{14}\log_2\frac{5}{14}$$ Substituting the decimal values $p_1 \approx 0.6429$ and $p_2 \approx 0.3571$: $$H(S) = -(0.6429)(\log_2 0.6429) - (0.3571)(\log_2 0.3571)$$ Using $\log_2 0.6429 \approx -0.6374$ and $\log_2 0.3571 \approx -1.4854$: $$H(S) = -(0.6429)(-0.6374) - (0.3571)(-1.4854)$$ $$H(S) = 0.4098 + 0.5304 = 0.9402 \text{ bits}$$ This is a *standard result*: for a 2-class problem with a 9:5 split, entropy is approximately **0.940 bits**. **Step 2 — Compute the entropy of each branch of Outlook.** - **Sunny branch (5 tuples)**: 2 Yes, 3 No. $$H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) = -\frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5} - \frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5} \approx 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$ - **Overcast branch (4 tuples)**: 4 Yes, 0 No → **pure**, entropy = 0. $$H(S_{\text{Overcast}}) = -1 \cdot \log_2 1 - 0 = 0 \text{ bits}$$ - **Rain branch (5 tuples)**: 3 Yes, 2 No. $$H(S_{\text{Rain}}) = -\frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5} - \frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5} \approx 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the weighted post-split entropy (conditional entropy).** $$H(S \vert \text{Outlook}) = \sum_{v \in \{\text{Sunny, Overcast, Rain}\}} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$ $$H(S \vert \text{Outlook}) = \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.9710)$$ $$H(S \vert \text{Outlook}) = 0.3468 + 0 + 0.3468 = 0.6936 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the Information Gain.** $$\text{IG}(S, \text{Outlook}) = H(S) - H(S \vert \text{Outlook})$$ $$\text{IG}(S, \text{Outlook}) = 0.9402 - 0.6936 = 0.2466 \text{ bits}$$ Because the information gain is positive, **Outlook is a useful splitting attribute** at the root of a decision tree. The ID3 / C4.5 algorithm would select it as the top node. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Confusion Matrix & Classification Metrics A spam classifier was tested on $N = 1000$ emails. The confusion matrix (rows = actual, columns = predicted) is: | | Predicted Spam | Predicted Ham | |---|---|---| | **Actual Spam** | TP = 90 | FN = 10 | | **Actual Ham** | FP = 50 | TN = 850 | **Step 1 — Accuracy.** $$\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{90 + 850}{1000} = 0.940$$ **Step 2 — Precision (Positive Predictive Value).** $$\text{P} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{90}{90 + 50} = \frac{90}{140} = 0.643$$ **Step 3 — Recall (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate).** $$\text{R} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{90}{90 + 10} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.900$$ **Step 4 — Specificity (True Negative Rate).** $$\text{Spec} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{850}{850 + 50} = \frac{850}{900} = 0.944$$ **Step 5 — F1-Score.** $$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.643 \cdot 0.900}{0.643 + 0.900} = \frac{1.1574}{1.543} \approx 0.750$$ **Step 6 — Interpretation.** The accuracy of 94% *looks* excellent, but the precision of only 64.3% means **36% of flagged spam is actually legitimate mail (false positives)**. In spam detection this is *worse* than missing a few spams — annoying users with false alarms is unacceptable. The F1-score of 0.75 gives a more honest single-number picture. > [!NOTE] > **Key Board-Exam Insight:** When the dataset is imbalanced (e.g., frauds are 0.1% of transactions), accuracy is **misleading** because a naive "predict everything as non-fraud" model gets 99.9% accuracy. Always report **Precision, Recall, F1, and ROC-AUC** in addition. --- ### 3.4 Summary Matrix — "Which Formula for Which Task?" | Data Mining Task | Primary Formula(s) to Know | |---|---| | **Association Rule Mining** | Support, Confidence, Lift, Leverage, Conviction | | **Classification (Decision Trees)** | Entropy, Gini Index, Information Gain, Gain Ratio | | **Classification (Evaluation)** | Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, Specificity, MCC | | **Clustering (Evaluation)** | Silhouette Coefficient, WCSS, Davies–Bouldin Index | | **Similarity / Distance** | Minkowski (L1, L2), Cosine Similarity, Jaccard Index | | **Anomaly Detection** | Z-score, IQR rule, Mahalanobis distance, isolation depth | | **Regression (Evaluation)** | MAE, MSE, RMSE, R² | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 KDD Process Flow (Block Diagram) The complete Knowledge Discovery in Databases pipeline, with feedback loops shown: ```mermaid flowchart TD startA((Raw Data Sources
DBMS / Files / APIs / Streams)) --> stepB[Data Selection
Identify target dataset] stepB --> stepC[Data Preprocessing
Handle missing, noise, outliers] stepC --> stepD[Data Integration
Merge heterogeneous sources] stepD --> stepE[Data Transformation
Normalize / Reduce / Construct] stepE --> stepF{Data Mining
Apply algorithms} stepF --> stepG[Pattern Evaluation
Interestingness measures] stepG --> stepH{Knowledge
Useful?} stepH -- Yes --> stepI[Knowledge Presentation
Visualize / Report / Deploy] stepH -- No --> stepB stepI --> endZ((Actionable Knowledge)) stepF --> endZ classDef process fill:#E8F1FF,stroke:#2A5DAA,color:#0B2545; classDef decision fill:#FFF4D6,stroke:#A66D00,color:#5C3A00; classDef terminal fill:#D6F5D6,stroke:#2C7A2C,color:#1B4D1B; class stepB,stepC,stepD,stepE,stepG,stepI process; class stepF,stepH decision; class startA,endZ terminal; ``` > [!NOTE] > The central diamond labeled `Data Mining` (with its own gold-style styling) is the *single step* that gives the broader KDD process its popular name. Everything before it is "preparation"; everything after it is "consumption". --- ### 4.2 Taxonomy of Data Mining Functionalities ```mermaid mindmap root((Data Mining
Functionalities)) Descriptive Concept Description Characterization Discrimination Frequent Patterns Itemsets Subsequences Substructures Association Rules Support Confidence Lift Clustering Partitional Hierarchical Density Based Outlier Detection Statistical Distance Based Model Based Predictive Classification Decision Tree Naive Bayes SVM Neural Net Regression Linear Polynomial Logistic Time Series ARIMA LSTM Deviation Analysis ``` --- ### 4.3 KDD Feedback / Iterative Loop Architecture The pipeline is not strictly linear. In practice, intermediate results often trigger re-execution of earlier stages: ```mermaid flowchart LR selA[Selection] --> preA[Preprocessing] preA --> intA[Integration] intA --> trnA[Transformation] trnA --> minA((Mining)) minA --> evaA[Evaluation] evaA --> preA evaA --> intA evaA --> trnA evaA --> preA2[Presentation] classDef loopback fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#A03333,color:#5C0000; classDef stage fill:#E8F1FF,stroke:#2A5DAA,color:#0B2545; class selA,preA,intA,trnA,evaA,preA2 stage; class minA loopback; ``` > [!TIP] > The red feedback arrows in the diagram are the reason mining projects are **iterative** — almost never one-shot. Examiners sometimes ask "Why is KDD iterative?" — answer with "evaluation often reveals that the data needs re-cleaning, re-integrating, or re-transforming." --- ### 4.4 Application-Domain Map ```mermaid flowchart LR coreX((Data Mining
Engine)) --> retailX[Retail and E Commerce] coreX --> bankX[Banking and Finance] coreX --> healthX[Healthcare and Bio] coreX --> telcoX[Telecommunications] coreX --> insX[Insurance] coreX --> mfgX[Manufacturing and IoT] coreX --> webX[Web Text Social Media] coreX --> govtX[Government and Defense] retailX --> taskA1[Market Basket] retailX --> taskA2[Recommendation] bankX --> taskB1[Credit Scoring] bankX --> taskB2[Fraud Detection] healthX --> taskC1[Gene Clustering] healthX --> taskC2[Disease Outbreak] telcoX --> taskD1[Churn Prediction] insX --> taskE1[Claim Fraud] mfgX --> taskF1[Predictive Maintenance] webX --> taskG1[Sentiment Analysis] govtX --> taskH1[Tax Evasion Pattern] classDef domain fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#8B6F00,color:#3D2F00; classDef task fill:#E0F7E0,stroke:#1F6B1F,color:#0B2E0B; class retailX,bankX,healthX,telcoX,insX,mfgX,webX,govtX domain; class taskA1,taskA2,taskB1,taskB2,taskC1,taskC2,taskD1,taskE1,taskF1,taskG1,taskH1 task; ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]** > Differentiate between **Data Mining** and **KDD**. Is Data Mining a synonym for KDD? Justify your answer in 5–6 lines. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | KDD | Data Mining | |---|---|---| | **Scope** | End-to-end knowledge discovery process | A single step within KDD | | **Steps Involved** | Selection → Preprocessing → Integration → Transformation → **Mining** → Evaluation → Presentation | Applies algorithms to extract patterns | | **Goal** | Convert raw data into *actionable knowledge* | Discover *valid, novel, useful patterns* | | **Duration** | Iterative, project-level | Algorithm-level, often a few hours | **Conclusion:** Data Mining is **not a synonym** for KDD. It is the *core algorithmic step* inside the broader KDD process, just as "drilling for oil" is one step inside the broader "petroleum exploration and refining" pipeline. *Mark Allocation:* (1) Definition of each — 1 mark · (2) Tabular comparison — 1 mark · (3) Concluding line with justification — 1 mark. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** > List **any six real-world applications** of Data Mining, specifying the mining task (classification / clustering / association / outlier detection) used in each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Credit Card Fraud Detection** — *Outlier/Anomaly Detection* on transactional streams. 2. **Market Basket Analysis in Retail** — *Association Rule Mining* to find "if-then" bundles. 3. **Customer Churn Prediction in Telecom** — *Classification* (decision trees, logistic regression). 4. **Spam Email Filtering** — *Classification* (Naive Bayes, SVM). 5. **Document/News Clustering** — *Clustering* (k-means, hierarchical) on TF-IDF vectors. 6. **Disease Outbreak Surveillance** — *Time-Series Forecasting / Clustering* of symptom reports. *Mark Allocation:* ½ mark per correct application-task pair × 6 = 3 marks. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > Following the KTU ESE pattern, the examiner offers a choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Each question carries 14 marks split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, May 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **complete KDD process** with a neat block diagram. How is the *Data Mining* step distinguished from the rest of the pipeline? **Model Answer (7 marks):** The KDD process is an **iterative, multi-step procedure** for converting low-level data into high-level knowledge. It consists of seven logically ordered stages: 1. **Data Selection** — Identify the subset of data relevant to the analytical objective. *Input:* heterogeneous raw data. *Output:* target dataset. 2. **Data Preprocessing** — Remove noise, handle missing values via imputation (mean / median / KNN), and resolve inconsistencies. *Output:* clean data. 3. **Data Integration** — Combine data from multiple sources (relational DBs, flat files, web APIs) and resolve schema/instance conflicts. *Output:* integrated, unified dataset. 4. **Data Transformation** — Convert data into forms suitable for mining: smoothing, aggregation, normalization (min-max, z-score), discretization, or feature construction (PCA, embeddings). *Output:* transformed dataset. 5. **Data Mining** *(the heart of KDD)* — Apply intelligent algorithms (classification, clustering, association, regression) to extract hidden patterns. This is the *only* step commonly referred to as "Data Mining" proper. 6. **Pattern Evaluation** — Quantify the interestingness of discovered patterns using objective measures (support, confidence, lift, accuracy) and subjective measures (novelty, actionability, understandability). Filter out trivial patterns. 7. **Knowledge Presentation** — Use visualization, dashboards, reports, and OLAP tools to present the validated knowledge to decision-makers. **Distinguishing the Data Mining step:** The Data Mining step is distinguished by its *algorithmic nature* — it requires applying mathematical / statistical / ML models (Apriori, k-Means, ID3, etc.) on the data. All the *preceding* steps are essentially data-engineering (no intelligence is being extracted yet), and all the *succeeding* steps are consumption (no new patterns are being generated). Only step 5 *creates* patterns. **(Block Diagram):** *(Re-draw the Mermaid flowchart from Section 4.1 in pen-and-paper form, labeling all 7 stages and the feedback loops.)* *Mark Allocation:* (1) Naming all 7 steps — 2 marks · (2) One-line description of each — 3 marks · (3) Diagram — 1 mark · (4) Distinction paragraph — 1 mark. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A supermarket transaction database has the following 6 transactions: | TID | Items | |---|---| | T1 | {Bread, Butter, Milk} | | T2 | {Bread, Diapers, Beer, Eggs} | | T3 | {Milk, Diapers, Beer, Cola} | | T4 | {Bread, Butter, Milk, Beer} | | T5 | {Butter, Milk, Diapers, Beer} | | T6 | {Bread, Milk, Diapers, Beer} | Compute **Support, Confidence, and Lift** for the rule: **Diapers ⇒ Beer**. Also compute **Leverage** and **Conviction**, and comment on whether the rule is interesting. **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Itemset frequency counts (N = 6).** - Diapers appears in T2, T3, T5, T6 → 4 times. - Beer appears in T2, T3, T4, T5, T6 → 5 times. - {Diapers, Beer} appears in T2, T3, T5, T6 → 4 times. **Step 2 — Support values.** $$\text{sup}(\text{Diapers}) = \frac{4}{6} = 0.667$$ $$\text{sup}(\text{Beer}) = \frac{5}{6} = 0.833$$ $$\text{sup}(\{\text{Diapers, Beer}\}) = \frac{4}{6} = 0.667$$ **Step 3 — Confidence.** $$\text{conf}(\text{Diapers} \Rightarrow \text{Beer}) = \frac{\text{sup}(\text{Diapers} \cup \text{Beer})}{\text{sup}(\text{Diapers})} = \frac{0.667}{0.667} = 1.000$$ **Step 4 — Lift.** $$\text{lift}(\text{Diapers} \Rightarrow \text{Beer}) = \frac{\text{conf}}{\text{sup}(\text{Beer})} = \frac{1.000}{0.833} = 1.200$$ **Step 5 — Leverage.** $$\text{lev}(\text{Diapers} \Rightarrow \text{Beer}) = 0.667 - (0.667 \times 0.833) = 0.667 - 0.556 = 0.111$$ **Step 6 — Conviction.** $$\text{conv}(\text{Diapers} \Rightarrow \text{Beer}) = \frac{1 - 0.833}{1 - 1.000} = \frac{0.167}{0} = \infty$$ **Step 7 — Interpretation.** - **Support** of 0.667 means Diapers and Beer co-occur in 67% of transactions — very frequent. - **Confidence** of 1.0 means *every* transaction that has Diapers also has Beer — perfect conditional probability. - **Lift** of 1.2 > 1 indicates a *positive correlation* — buying Diapers increases the probability of buying Beer by 20% over the baseline. - **Leverage** of 0.111 > 0 confirms positive association. - **Conviction = ∞** indicates a *logical (deterministic)* rule — there is no counterexample. **Conclusion:** The rule **"Diapers ⇒ Beer"** is **highly interesting** — high support, perfect confidence, and infinite conviction. This is a classic "beer-and-diapers" market-basket pattern, useful for **cross-merchandising, store layout, and promotional bundling**. *Mark Allocation:* (1) Itemset counts — 1 mark · (2) Support values — 1 mark · (3) Confidence — 1 mark · (4) Lift — 1 mark · (5) Leverage + Conviction — 1 mark · (6) Interpretation in 3–4 lines — 2 marks. --- ### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Dec 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Define **Data Mining**. Discuss the major **functionalities** of Data Mining in detail, classifying them into *descriptive* and *predictive* categories with **two examples** of each. **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Data Mining is the process of extracting previously unknown, valid, potentially useful, and ultimately comprehensible patterns from large stores of data, employing methods at the intersection of statistics, machine learning, AI, and database systems. **Descriptive Functionalities (3 marks):** These characterize the *general properties* of data and surface hidden structure. 1. **Concept/Class Description** — Summarizes a target class. *Example:* "Customers who buy premium memberships earn above ₹1 lakh/month and are 25–40 years old." Implemented via *attribute-oriented induction* or *data cuboids*. 2. **Association Rule Mining** — Discovers strong "if-then" co-occurrence rules. *Example:* In a Walmart dataset, {Diapers, Milk} ⇒ Beer with 70% confidence and lift 1.4. Implemented via *Apriori* or *FP-Growth*. 3. **Clustering** — Groups records by intrinsic similarity without labels. *Example:* Segmenting shoppers into 4 personas based on RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary) using k-Means. 4. **Outlier Detection** — Identifies records that deviate from the bulk. *Example:* A sudden $50,000 transaction on a credit card that usually spends $200/month. **Predictive Functionalities (3 marks):** These perform *inference* on new data using models learned from labeled historical data. 1. **Classification** — Learns a function $f: X \to C$ mapping feature vectors to discrete class labels. *Example:* Spam detection (spam / not-spam) using Naive Bayes. Other algorithms: decision trees, k-NN, SVM, neural networks. 2. **Regression** — Learns a function $f: X \to \mathbb{R}$ mapping to a continuous value. *Example:* Predicting house price from area, bedrooms, and locality using linear regression or XGBoost. 3. **Time-Series / Trend Prediction** — Forecasts future values from past values. *Example:* Forecasting next-day stock closing price using ARIMA or LSTM. 4. **Deviation Analysis** — Compares observed values against expected values. *Example:* Alerting when a server's CPU usage is 3σ above its weekly average. *Mark Allocation:* (1) Definition — 1 mark · (2) Descriptive list with 2 examples — 3 marks · (3) Predictive list with 2 examples — 3 marks. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** For a dataset of 12 records of the *PlayTennis* problem, the class distribution is **8 "Yes" and 4 "No"**. Compute the **entropy** of the dataset. If splitting on attribute **Humidity** yields two branches — *High* (6 records: 4 Yes, 2 No) and *Normal* (6 records: 4 Yes, 2 No) — compute the **Information Gain** obtained by splitting on Humidity. **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Compute the entropy of the full dataset $S$.** The class probabilities are $p_{\text{Yes}} = 8/12 = 0.667$ and $p_{\text{No}} = 4/12 = 0.333$. $$H(S) = -p_{\text{Yes}}\log_2 p_{\text{Yes}} - p_{\text{No}}\log_2 p_{\text{No}}$$ $$H(S) = -(0.667)\log_2(0.667) - (0.333)\log_2(0.333)$$ Using $\log_2(0.667) \approx -0.5850$ and $\log_2(0.333) \approx -1.5850$: $$H(S) = -(0.667)(-0.5850) - (0.333)(-1.5850)$$ $$H(S) = 0.3900 + 0.5278 = 0.9178 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 2 — Compute entropy of the *High* branch.** Proportions: $p_{\text{Yes}} = 4/6 = 0.667$, $p_{\text{No}} = 2/6 = 0.333$ (same as parent, so entropy is identical). $$H(S_{\text{High}}) = 0.9178 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 3 — Compute entropy of the *Normal* branch.** Same proportions, so $H(S_{\text{Normal}}) = 0.9178$ bits. **Step 4 — Compute the conditional (post-split) entropy.** $$H(S \vert \text{Humidity}) = \frac{6}{12} H(S_{\text{High}}) + \frac{6}{12} H(S_{\text{Normal}})$$ $$H(S \vert \text{Humidity}) = (0.5)(0.9178) + (0.5)(0.9178) = 0.9178 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the Information Gain.** $$\text{IG}(S, \text{Humidity}) = H(S) - H(S \vert \text{Humidity}) = 0.9178 - 0.9178 = 0.0000 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 6 — Interpretation.** The Information Gain is **zero** because *both branches preserve the same class distribution* (4:2 ratio) as the parent. This means **Humidity is a *useless* splitting attribute** in this dataset — it carries no discriminative information for the target class. A decision-tree algorithm (ID3 / C4.5) would **reject** this split and look for another attribute with positive information gain. *Mark Allocation:* (1) Class probabilities — ½ mark · (2) $H(S)$ — 1 mark · (3) $H(S_{\text{High}})$ and $H(S_{\text{Normal}})$ — 2 marks · (4) $H(S \vert \text{Humidity})$ — 1 mark · (5) $\text{IG}$ — 1 mark · (6) Interpretation — 1.5 marks. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Caught by KTU Board Examiners (Module 1 — Data Mining Concepts & Applications)** > > 1. **Confusing DM with KDD.** Many students use the two terms interchangeably. State explicitly that **Data Mining is a step inside KDD**, not a synonym. (Penalty: up to 1 mark per question.) > > 2. **Skipping the formula derivation for Support / Confidence.** A direct numerical answer without showing $\text{count}(X)/N$ will cost at least 1 mark. Always show the fraction and the substitution. > > 3. **Forgetting to interpret the Lift value.** Examiners explicitly reward a one-line comment on whether $\text{lift} > 1$, $= 1$, or $< 1$ implies positive, independent, or negative correlation. > > 4. **Mixing up Precision and Recall.** Precision = "of those predicted positive, how many are correct"; Recall = "of those actually positive, how many did we catch". The classic confusion is reversed. > > 5. **Using $\log$ instead of $\log_2$ in Entropy.** Decision-tree entropy is in *bits* and always uses base 2. Using $\log_e$ (or natural log) is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. > > 6. **Drawing the KDD diagram with the wrong order.** Common mistake: putting "Mining" *before* "Preprocessing" or "Integration" *after* "Transformation". Memorize the canonical order: **Selection → Preprocessing → Integration → Transformation → Mining → Evaluation → Presentation**. > > 7. **Listing "Data Mining" applications without naming the mining task.** "Amazon uses Data Mining" earns 0 marks. "Amazon uses **association-rule mining** to power *Customers Who Bought This Also Bought*" earns 1 mark per correctly paired application + task. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **One-page revision checklist — print this before the exam.** - ✅ **Data Mining** = extraction of novel, valid, useful, understandable patterns from large data. - ✅ **KDD** = end-to-end process; DM is **one step** within it. - ✅ KDD steps in order: **Selection → Preprocessing → Integration → Transformation → Mining → Evaluation → Presentation**. - ✅ Two main categories: **Descriptive** (concept description, association, clustering, outlier detection) and **Predictive** (classification, regression, time-series, deviation analysis). - ✅ **Support(X)** = count(X) / N · range [0, 1] · measures *frequency*. - ✅ **Confidence(X → Y)** = sup(X ∪ Y) / sup(X) · range [0, 1] · measures *reliability*. - ✅ **Lift(X → Y)** = conf / sup(Y) · > 1 ⇒ positive correlation, = 1 ⇒ independent, < 1 ⇒ negative. - ✅ **Leverage** measures the *absolute* deviation from independence; **Conviction** captures *directional* dependence. - ✅ **Entropy** = $-\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ · 0 = pure · increases with impurity. - ✅ **Information Gain** = $H(S) - H(S \vert A)$ · higher = better split; ID3 maximizes IG. - ✅ **Gini Index** = $1 - \sum p_i^2$ · used by CART · 0 = pure. - ✅ **Classification metrics** (imbalanced data): report **Precision, Recall, F1, ROC-AUC** — *never* rely on accuracy alone. - ✅ **Attribute types** — Nominal, Binary, Ordinal, Numeric (Interval, Ratio) — algorithm choice depends on type. - ✅ **8 application domains to remember** — Retail, Banking, Healthcare, Telecom, Insurance, Manufacturing, Web/Text/Social, Government. - ✅ **KDD is iterative** — evaluation often triggers re-cleaning, re-integration, re-transformation. - ✅ **Why is DM needed?** — "Drowning in data, starving for knowledge." - ✅ **Why is DM not a synonym for KDD?** — DM is the algorithmic extraction step; KDD is the entire workflow. ---

topics.title: Knowledge Discovery in Database vs Data Mining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST525 - DATA MINING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Knowledge Discovery in Database vs Data Mining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Knowledge Discovery in Databases (KDD)

**Knowledge Discovery in Databases (KDD)** is the **non-trivial process of identifying valid, novel, potentially useful, and ultimately understandable patterns in data**, as formally defined by **Usama Fayyad, Gregory Piatetsky-Shapiro, and Padhraic Smyth in 1996**. It represents the **entire end-to-end pipeline** that transforms raw, low-level data residing in heterogeneous data sources into high-level, actionable knowledge.

Mathematically, KDD can be expressed as a mapping function:

$$
K : \mathcal{D} \rightarrow \mathcal{K}
$$

Where $\mathcal{D}$ is the **input data domain** (raw, uncleaned, possibly noisy data) and $\mathcal{K}$ is the **knowledge domain** (validated patterns, rules, and insights). The KDD process is **iterative** and **interactive**, requiring human domain expertise at multiple stages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"KDD is the overall process of discovering useful knowledge from data, encompassing several stages from raw data selection to knowledge presentation, while Data Mining is a particular step in this process involving the application of specific algorithms for extracting patterns from data."*

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Data Mining

**Data Mining** is the **core analytical step within the KDD process** that involves the application of **computationally intensive algorithms** (such as classification, regression, clustering, association rule mining, and anomaly detection) to extract implicit, previously unknown, and potentially useful patterns from a well-prepared dataset. It is essentially the **pattern-extraction engine** of the larger KDD pipeline.

$$
\text{DataMining}(D_{prep}) = \{P_1, P_2, P_3, \ldots, P_n\}
$$

Where $D_{prep}$ is the **pre-processed, transformed data** (the output of KDD's earlier stages) and $P_i$ represents an **extracted pattern** (rules, clusters, decision trees, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonym Note for KTU Examinations:** Data Mining is often called **Knowledge Mining from Data**, **Knowledge Extraction**, or **Data/Pattern Analysis**. These terms are **interchangeable** in KTU exam contexts. The term *Data Mining* is also referred to as **Knowledge Discovery in Mining (KDM)** in certain KTU 2024 Scheme references.

### 1.3 The Gold Rush Analogy — Intuitive Real-World Understanding

Imagine you are a **gold prospector** in the 1850s California Gold Rush:

- **Raw Data (Earth & Rocks):** The entire landscape is filled with dirt, mud, gravel, and rocks. This is your **raw data warehouse** — vast, messy, and mostly useless in its current form.
- **Data Cleaning (Removing Mud):** You first wash away the mud, remove large rocks, and filter debris. This is analogous to the **Data Cleaning** stage of KDD.
- **Data Integration (Combining Streams):** You merge water from multiple streams (different rivers) to maximize yield. This is **Data Integration**.
- **Data Selection (Picking a Promising Site):** Based on geological surveys, you choose the most promising patch of land. This is **Data Selection**.
- **Data Transformation (Crushing Ore):** You crush the rock into fine powder and chemically transform it (e.g., using mercury or cyanide) to make extraction possible. This is **Data Transformation**.
- **Data Mining (The Actual Gold Panning/Smelting):** This is where you **sift the crushed ore to extract the tiny gold flakes**. This is the precise, focused, algorithm-driven step called **Data Mining** — the heart of the operation.
- **Pattern Evaluation (Verifying Gold Purity):** You test whether what you found is real gold or fool's gold. This is **Pattern Evaluation**.
- **Knowledge Presentation (Selling the Gold):** You take the refined gold to the market, polish it, and present it as jewelry. This is **Knowledge Presentation**.

> [!TIP]
> **The Golden Rule for KTU Viva/Exams:** *Data Mining is a STEP. KDD is the JOURNEY.* If the question asks for the "process" — answer KDD. If the question asks for the "algorithm/technique application" — answer Data Mining.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology

The following terminology appears repeatedly in KTU 2024 Scheme question papers:

| Term | Meaning | KTU Significance |
|------|---------|------------------|
| **Pattern** | A statement/rule that describes relationships in data | Core output of Data Mining |
| **Validity** | Pattern holds on new data with some certainty | Measured by confidence/support |
| **Novelty** | Pattern is non-obvious | Tested against prior knowledge |
| **Usefulness** | Pattern can be acted upon | Business/relevance metric |
| **Understandability** | Pattern is human-interpretable | Simplicity, interpretability |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Architecture of the KDD Process — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The KDD process is an **iterative sequence** consisting of **seven canonical stages** (as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 syllabus and aligned with the **CRISP-DM** industry standard). Each stage consumes the output of the previous stage and produces an input for the next.

**Stage 1: Data Cleaning**
- **Why:** Real-world data is dirty — it contains noise, outliers, missing values, and inconsistencies. A KTU 2024 study noted that data scientists spend approximately **60–80%** of their time on cleaning.
- **How:** Apply techniques such as **binning**, **regression** (to fill missing values), **clustering** (to detect outliers), and **manual correction**.

**Stage 2: Data Integration**
- **Why:** Data resides in heterogeneous sources (multiple databases, data warehouses, web scrapes, flat files).
- **How:** Schema integration, redundancy elimination, and conflict resolution between sources.

**Stage 3: Data Selection**
- **Why:** Not all data is relevant to the analytical goal.
- **How:** Retrieve only the **task-relevant data** from the integrated database using query operations (SQL-based or feature selection).

**Stage 4: Data Transformation**
- **Why:** Data must be in a format suitable for mining algorithms.
- **How:** Apply **smoothing**, **aggregation**, **normalization**, **discretization**, and **concept hierarchy generation**.

**Stage 5: Data Mining ⭐ (The Core Step)**
- **Why:** This is the intelligent, algorithmic extraction phase.
- **How:** Apply methods such as **classification** (decision trees, SVM, neural networks), **regression**, **clustering** (K-means, DBSCAN), **association rule mining** (Apriori, FP-Growth), or **outlier detection**.

**Stage 6: Pattern Evaluation**
- **Why:** Not every discovered pattern is useful. We need to filter by **interestingness measures**.
- **How:** Use **support**, **confidence**, **lift**, **accuracy**, and domain-specific metrics to identify genuinely interesting patterns.

**Stage 7: Knowledge Presentation**
- **Why:** End users (business managers, doctors, engineers) need to understand and act on the knowledge.
- **How:** Use **visualization** (charts, graphs, dashboards), **rule summarization**, and **report generation**.

### 2.2 Why KDD ≠ Data Mining — The Conceptual Distinction

| Aspect | KDD | Data Mining |
|--------|-----|-------------|
| **Scope** | Entire pipeline (7 stages) | Single stage (Stage 5) |
| **Goal** | Discover useful knowledge | Extract patterns from data |
| **Input** | Raw, uncleaned data | Pre-processed, transformed data |
| **Output** | Actionable knowledge | Patterns, models, rules |
| **Time Span** | Long-term, multi-stage effort | Focused, algorithmic step |
| **Tools Used** | Database management, ETL, statistics, ML | Statistical algorithms, ML models, AI |
| **Nature** | Process-oriented | Technique-oriented |
| **Dependency** | Encompasses data mining | Depends on KDD's earlier stages |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas appear in KTU exam questions involving interestingness measures of mined patterns. Memorize these.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | **Support of a Pattern $A$** | $\text{Support}(A) = \dfrac{\vert T(A) \vert}{\vert T \vert}$ | Fraction of transactions containing $A$ |
| 2 | **Confidence of Rule $A \Rightarrow B$** | $\text{Confidence}(A \Rightarrow B) = \dfrac{\text{Support}(A \cup B)}{\text{Support}(A)}$ | Conditional probability of $B$ given $A$ |
| 3 | **Lift of Rule $A \Rightarrow B$** | $\text{Lift}(A \Rightarrow B) = \dfrac{\text{Confidence}(A \Rightarrow B)}{\text{Support}(B)}$ | Correlation strength between $A$ and $B$ |
| 4 | **Information Gain** | $\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A)$ | Reduction in entropy after split on attribute $A$ |
| 5 | **Entropy** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Measure of impurity in a dataset $S$ |
| 6 | **Gini Index** | $\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i^2$ | Alternative impurity measure (used in CART) |

> **Note on Notation:** $\vert T(A) \vert$ denotes the **cardinality** (number of transactions) containing itemset $A$, and $\vert T \vert$ is the total number of transactions. **Do NOT confuse the cardinality bars with the markdown table pipe symbol** — they are conceptually different. In LaTeX, use `\vert T(A) \vert` for the cardinality notation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

KDD and Data Mining find applications across **every KTU engineering domain**:

- **Biomedical Engineering:** Mining patient records to predict disease outbreaks (KDD), classifying tumors using SVM (Data Mining).
- **Civil Engineering:** Predicting concrete strength using regression models.
- **Computer Science:** Spam detection (classification), recommendation systems (clustering + association rules).
- **Electrical Engineering:** Fault detection in power grids using anomaly detection.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Predictive maintenance using time-series clustering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: KDD vs Data Mining — A Deep Comparison

The following matrix is **frequently tested** in KTU Module 1 examinations. We derive each row by comparing the two concepts along a specific dimension.

| Comparison Dimension | KDD (Knowledge Discovery in Databases) | Data Mining | KTU Exam Cue Words |
|----------------------|---------------------------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **1. Definition** | The entire process of extracting knowledge from data | A specific step that uses algorithms to find patterns | "Define KDD", "What is data mining" |
| **2. Synonyms** | Knowledge Mining, Knowledge Extraction | Knowledge Discovery in Mining (KDM) | "Also known as..." |
| **3. Process Nature** | Multi-stage, iterative, interactive | Single-step, algorithmic, focused | "Is it a process or a step?" |
| **4. Stage Position** | Encompasses all 7 stages | Occurs at Stage 5 only | "Where in the pipeline?" |
| **5. Input Data** | Raw data (clean, integrated, selected, transformed) | Pre-processed data only | "What data is fed in?" |
| **6. Output** | Knowledge (human-understandable) | Patterns (mathematical/statistical) | "What is produced?" |
| **7. Duration** | Long-running (days to months) | Short-running (seconds to hours) | "How long does it take?" |
| **8. Human Involvement** | High (domain expert at every stage) | Low (algorithm-driven) | "Manual vs automated?" |
| **9. Techniques Used** | Data cleaning, ETL, statistics, ML, visualization | Classification, regression, clustering, association | "Which techniques?" |
| **10. Goal Orientation** | Goal-oriented (end-to-end knowledge) | Technique-oriented (pattern extraction) | "What is the focus?" |

### 3.2 Detailed Step-by-Step KDD Process Derivation

The KDD process can be **mathematically formalized** as a composition of seven functions:

$$
\text{KDD}(D_{raw}) = \text{Present}(\text{Evaluate}(\text{Mine}(\text{Transform}(\text{Select}(\text{Integrate}(\text{Clean}(D_{raw})))))))
$$

Let us **derive each step** explicitly:

**Step 1 — Data Cleaning ($f_1$):**
$$
D_1 = f_1(D_{raw}) = D_{raw} - \text{Noise} - \text{Outliers} - \text{MissingValues}
$$
This step removes inconsistencies and handles missing data. For example, if a dataset has $1000$ customer records and $50$ have missing age values, those values are imputed using mean/median/mode.

**Step 2 — Data Integration ($f_2$):**
$$
D_2 = f_2(D_1, S_2, S_3, \ldots, S_k)
$$
Multiple data sources $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_k$ are merged into a unified dataset $D_2$. Schema conflicts and redundancies are resolved.

**Step 3 — Data Selection ($f_3$):**
$$
D_3 = f_3(D_2) = \sigma_{\text{relevant}}(D_2)
$$
Only the attributes and tuples relevant to the mining goal are retained. For instance, for churn prediction, only customer demographics, usage history, and complaint records are selected.

**Step 4 — Data Transformation ($f_4$):**
$$
D_4 = f_4(D_3)
$$
Operations include normalization:
$$
x_{norm} = \frac{x - \min(x)}{\max(x) - \min(x)}
$$
Discretization, aggregation, and feature engineering are also performed.

**Step 5 — Data Mining ($f_5$):** ⭐
$$
D_5 = f_5(D_4) = \{P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n\}
$$
This is where the **intelligent algorithm** is applied. For example, applying the **Apriori algorithm** to a transactional database to find frequent itemsets.

**Step 6 — Pattern Evaluation ($f_6$):**
$$
D_6 = f_6(D_5) = \{P_i \in D_5 : \text{Interestingness}(P_i) \geq \theta\}
$$
Where $\theta$ is a user-defined threshold. Only patterns with $\text{Support} \geq \theta_s$ and $\text{Confidence} \geq \theta_c$ are retained.

**Step 7 — Knowledge Presentation ($f_7$):**
$$
\mathcal{K} = f_7(D_6)
$$
Visualization and reporting tools render the knowledge in a human-digestible form.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Computing Support and Confidence

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Exam Style Problem (2-Mark Variation)**

**Problem:** A supermarket database contains the following transactions. Compute the support and confidence of the rule: **{Bread, Butter} $\Rightarrow$ {Milk}**

| Transaction ID | Items Purchased |
|----------------|-----------------|
| T1 | Bread, Butter, Milk |
| T2 | Bread, Butter, Jam |
| T3 | Bread, Milk |
| T4 | Butter, Milk, Eggs |
| T5 | Bread, Butter, Milk, Eggs |

**Total Transactions:** $\vert T \vert = 5$

**Step 1 — Count transactions containing {Bread, Butter}:**
- T1: Yes (Bread, Butter, Milk)
- T2: Yes (Bread, Butter, Jam)
- T3: No
- T4: No
- T5: Yes (Bread, Butter, Milk, Eggs)

So $\vert T(\{\text{Bread, Butter}\}) \vert = 3$

$$
\text{Support}(\{\text{Bread, Butter}\}) = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6
$$

**Step 2 — Count transactions containing {Bread, Butter, Milk}:**
- T1: Yes
- T5: Yes
- T2: No (has Jam, not Milk)
- T3: No (no Butter)
- T4: No (no Bread)

So $\vert T(\{\text{Bread, Butter, Milk}\}) \vert = 2$

$$
\text{Support}(\{\text{Bread, Butter, Milk}\}) = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Confidence:**

$$
\text{Confidence}(\{\text{Bread, Butter}\} \Rightarrow \{\text{Milk}\}) = \frac{\text{Support}(\{\text{Bread, Butter, Milk}\})}{\text{Support}(\{\text{Bread, Butter}\})} = \frac{0.4}{0.6} = 0.6667
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Lift (assuming Support of Milk):**
Count transactions with Milk: T1, T3, T4, T5 = 4

$$
\text{Support}(\{\text{Milk}\}) = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8
$$

$$
\text{Lift} = \frac{0.6667}{0.8} = 0.8333
$$

**Interpretation:** Lift $< 1$ indicates that **{Bread, Butter} and {Milk} are negatively correlated** — customers buying bread and butter are slightly **less** likely to buy milk than the average customer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The KDD Process Flow

This diagram shows the **complete 7-stage KDD pipeline** and highlights the position of Data Mining within it.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Data Sources"] --> B["Stage 1 Data Cleaning"]
    B --> C["Stage 2 Data Integration"]

    D --> E["Stage 4 Data Transformation"]
    E --> F["Stage 5 Data Mining"]
    F --> G["Stage 6 Pattern Evaluation"]
    G --> H{"Patterns Interesting?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Stage 7 Knowledge Presentation"]
    H -- No --> B
    I --> J["Actionable Knowledge"]
    J --> K["Decision Support / Deployment"]
    K -- Feedback --> B
```
