```mermaid
    OLTP_QUERY_PROFILE -.->|"Do NOT mix"| DW_QUERY_PROFILE
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The first diagram shows the **complete data flow**: sources → OLTP → ETL → DW → Mining.
> - The second diagram visually contrasts **many small normalized tables** (OLTP) vs **one large fact table radiating dimensions** (DW).
> - The third diagram emphasizes that the **two query profiles are fundamentally incompatible** — hence the architectural split.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any four characteristics of a Data Warehouse.**

**Model Answer (4 points × 0.75 ≈ 3 marks):**
1. **Subject-Oriented** — organized around business subjects (Customer, Product) rather than applications.
2. **Integrated** — consolidates data from heterogeneous sources into a single consistent view.
3. **Time-Variant** — preserves historical data over long periods (5–10 years) for trend analysis.
4. **Non-Volatile** — once loaded, data is not updated or deleted; it is read-mostly and append-only.

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is meant by an Operational Database System?**

**Model Answer:**
An **Operational Database System (OLTP)** is a database designed to manage and record the **day-to-day real-time transactions** of an organization, such as inserts, updates, and deletes. It is **application-oriented**, supports **ACID properties**, uses a **normalized schema**, and is optimized for **high-throughput, low-latency** transaction processing rather than analytical queries.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **architecture and characteristics of a Data Warehouse**. Mention any **two real-world use cases** where a Data Warehouse is preferred over an operational database. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Operational Database Systems (OLTP) and Data Warehouses (OLAP)** across at least **eight distinct parameters**, with a suitable example for each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

A **Data Warehouse (DW)** is a subject-oriented, integrated, time-variant, and non-volatile collection of data that supports managerial decision-making.

**Four Defining Characteristics (W. H. Inmon):**

1. **Subject-Oriented:**
   Unlike OLTP which is built around applications (Billing, Inventory), a DW is built around **business subjects** like Customer, Sales, Product. This allows holistic analysis across multiple operational silos.

2. **Integrated:**
   Data from multiple heterogeneous sources (different OLTP systems, flat files, web logs, IoT streams) is **cleansed, standardized, and unified** in the DW. For example, gender may be stored as "M/F" in one system and "Male/Female" in another — the DW unifies it into a single canonical form.

3. **Time-Variant:**
   The DW retains data for **5–10 years**, far longer than OLTP's 90 days. Every record carries an implicit or explicit timestamp, enabling **time-series mining, trend analysis, and forecasting**.

4. **Non-Volatile:**
   Once data is loaded into the DW, it is **never modified or deleted**. The only operations are **initial bulk loads** and **subsequent reads**. This guarantees data stability for historical reporting.

**Architecture:**
A typical DW uses a **multi-tier architecture**:
- **Bottom Tier**: Operational sources and ETL tools.
- **Middle Tier**: DW server (typically relational or columnar, e.g., Snowflake, Redshift).
- **Top Tier**: OLAP tools, dashboards, and data mining engines.

**Two Real-World Use Cases:**

| # | Use Case | Why DW? |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Retail: Year-over-Year Festive Sales Analysis** | Needs 5+ years of historical transaction data; OLTP retains only 90 days. |
| 2 | **Banking: Customer Lifetime Value Prediction** | Requires integration of loan, deposit, and credit-card data from multiple OLTP systems. |

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

| # | Parameter | Operational DB (OLTP) | Data Warehouse (OLAP) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Purpose** | Day-to-day transaction processing (e.g., POS checkout) | Strategic analysis and decision support (e.g., sales forecasting) |
| 2 | **Data Orientation** | Application-oriented (one DB per app) | Subject-oriented (Customer, Product, Sales) |
| 3 | **Data Age** | Current data only (last 90 days) | Historical data (5–10 years) |
| 4 | **Schema Design** | Normalized (3NF / BCNF) | Denormalized (Star / Snowflake schema) |
| 5 | **Query Type** | Simple, predefined, parameterized (`SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE ID = ?`) | Complex, ad-hoc, analytical (`SELECT Region, SUM(Sales) FROM Fact GROUP BY Region, Year`) |
| 6 | **Update Pattern** | Continuous INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE at row level | Periodic bulk loads, read-only |
| 7 | **Performance Metric** | Transactions per second (TPS), millisecond response | Query throughput, scan rate, time-to-insight |
| 8 | **Users** | Clerks, tellers, operational staff | Data analysts, executives, data scientists |
| 9 | **Consistency Model** | Strict ACID | Eventual consistency |
| 10 | **Example Systems** | MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server | Snowflake, Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery, Teradata |

**Example Pair:**
- **OLTP Example**: An ATM deducting ₹5,000 from account `12345` — a single row update with millisecond latency, fully ACID-compliant.
- **DW Example**: A bank executive running *"Average monthly withdrawal by age group across Kerala branches for 2020–2024"* — a multi-million-row aggregation across the fact table joined with Customer, Branch, and Time dimensions.

---

#### **Question B** (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]`

**(a)** Define **Operational Database Systems** and explain their **key features** with suitable examples. Why are they **not suitable for analytical queries**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **ETL-based architecture of a Data Warehouse**. Describe how data flows from operational systems to the warehouse and finally to analytical applications. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition:**
An **Operational Database System (OLTP)** is a real-time, transaction-oriented database that captures and manages the day-to-day operations of an organization. Each transaction is small, atomic, and accesses only a few records.

**Key Features (with examples):**
1. **Real-Time Processing** — A cash register ringing up a sale completes in under 200 ms.
2. **High Concurrency** — Thousands of ATMs process withdrawals simultaneously.
3. **ACID Compliance** — A fund transfer either debits and credits both accounts or neither (atomicity).
4. **Normalized Design** — Customer, Order, Product stored in separate 3NF tables to avoid redundancy.
5. **Row-Level Updates** — A customer changing their phone number updates exactly one row.
6. **Frequent Backups** — Critical because any data loss disrupts business operations.

**Why OLTP is Unsuitable for Analytical Queries:**

1. **Performance Degradation:** Analytical queries scan millions of rows; running them on OLTP would lock tables and block live transactions.
2. **Missing Historical Data:** OLTP retains only current values; trend analysis is impossible.
3. **Schema Rigidity for Analytics:** The normalized schema requires multiple expensive JOINs for analytical aggregations.
4. **No Integration of Heterogeneous Sources:** OLTP is siloed per application; analytics needs cross-application data.
5. **Concurrency Conflicts:** Long analytical reads would conflict with short transactional writes, causing deadlocks.

**Example:** Running `SELECT AVG(balance) FROM accounts GROUP BY age, branch` on the live banking OLTP DB would lock the accounts table, freezing all ATM transactions for minutes — catastrophic in production.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**ETL-Based Data Warehouse Architecture:**

```
[ Operational Sources ]  -->  [ ETL Layer ]  -->  [ Data Warehouse ]  -->  [ OLAP / Mining ]
  OLTP DB 1                  Extract              Fact Tables             Dashboards
  OLTP DB 2                  Transform            Dimension Tables         Reports
  Flat Files                 Load                 Historical Storage        Data Mining
  Web Logs                                                                         |
  External Feeds                                                                       v
                                                                                  Knowledge
```

**Data Flow Stages:**

**Stage 1 — Data Sources (Operational Layer):**
Multiple heterogeneous OLTP systems (Sales DB, Inventory DB, HR DB), flat files (CSV exports from legacy systems), web server logs, and external feeds (market data, weather APIs) all serve as inputs.

**Stage 2 — ETL Layer (Staging Area):**

- **Extract**: Pull data from each source via ODBC/JDBC connectors, APIs, or log-based change-data-capture (CDC).
- **Transform**:
  - **Cleansing** — remove duplicates, handle NULLs, correct typos.
  - **Standardization** — unify formats (e.g., date `DD/MM/YYYY` vs `MM-DD-YYYY`).
  - **Integration** — resolve schema mismatches across sources.
  - **Aggregation** — pre-compute summary metrics if needed.
- **Load**: Bulk INSERT into the data warehouse fact and dimension tables, typically during off-peak hours (nightly).

**Stage 3 — Data Warehouse (Storage Layer):**
Stores integrated, historical, subject-oriented data in **star/snowflake schema**. Includes:
- **Fact tables** with numeric measures (sales_amount, quantity, profit).
- **Dimension tables** with descriptive attributes (customer_name, region, product_category, date).

**Stage 4 — OLAP / Data Mining (Presentation Layer):**
End-user tools run **roll-up, drill-down, slice, and dice** operations. Data mining algorithms (Apriori, K-Means, Decision Trees) are executed against the integrated data to extract hidden patterns, which are visualized in executive dashboards.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing OLTP with OLAP workloads** — stating that OLTP "stores historical data" or that OLAP "handles transactions" will cost full marks on parameter definitions.
> 2. **Listing characteristics without examples** — KTU 2024 key scripts explicitly award **1 mark per characteristic-example pair**. A bare list of "Subject-oriented, Integrated..." without examples may get only half credit.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram in ETL questions** — a Part-B question on DW architecture **must include a labeled block diagram**. Omitting it is a guaranteed **−2 mark penalty**.
> 4. **Mixing up normalization levels** — saying the DW uses 3NF is a factual error; DWs use **dimensional (star/snowflake) schemas**, which are denormalized.
> 5. **Forgetting the time-variant aspect** — many students miss that the DW stores data with timestamps, which is precisely what enables data mining on temporal patterns.
> 6. **Treating OLAP and Data Warehouse as the same thing** — they are related but not identical: DW is the *storage*, OLAP is the *query/analysis paradigm* on top of it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Module-1 Revision Points:**

- ✅ **OLTP = Online Transaction Processing**; **OLAP = Online Analytical Processing**.
- ✅ OLTP is **application-oriented**; DW is **subject-oriented**.
- ✅ OLTP holds **current data**; DW holds **historical data (5–10 years)**.
- ✅ OLTP uses **normalized 3NF schema**; DW uses **denormalized Star / Snowflake schema**.
- ✅ OLTP handles **simple queries returning few rows**; DW handles **complex queries returning millions of rows**.
- ✅ OLTP measures **TPS (Transactions Per Second)**; DW measures **query throughput and scan rate**.
- ✅ OLTP demands **strict ACID**; DW accepts **eventual consistency**.
- ✅ OLTP is **highly volatile** (continuous updates); DW is **non-volatile** (append-only).
- ✅ OLTP users are **operational staff**; DW users are **analysts, managers, data scientists**.
- ✅ **Four Inmon characteristics of DW**: Subject-Oriented, Integrated, Time-Variant, Non-Volatile.
- ✅ **ETL** = Extract → Transform → Load; this is the bridge between OLTP and DW.
- ✅ **Data Mining algorithms (Apriori, K-Means, Decision Trees) operate on Data Warehouses, NOT on operational databases.**
- ✅ The **size ratio** of DW to ODS is typically **20× to 50×**.
- ✅ **Performance metric for OLTP latency**: milliseconds; for DW: seconds-to-minutes acceptable.
- ✅ **OLAP operations**: Roll-up, Drill-down, Slice, Dice, Pivot — all run on the DW.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multidimensional Data Model - Warehouse Schema
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST525 - DATA MINING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Multidimensional Data Model - Warehouse Schema # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Multidimensional Data Model** is a conceptual framework used in Data Warehousing that views data as a **data cube** — a multi-dimensional generalization of a relational table. Data is organized around two key components: - **Measures**: Numerical values (quantitative facts) that are the subject of analysis (e.g., `sales_amount`, `units_sold`, `profit`). - **Dimensions**: Categorical/qualitative attributes that provide context to the measures (e.g., `time`, `location`, `product`). A **Data Warehouse Schema** is the logical structure defining how fact and dimension tables are related to enable efficient OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) queries. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST525 Module 1):** > Students must master **Star Schema, Snowflake Schema, and Fact Constellation Schema** along with concept of **Data Cube**, **Dimensions**, **Measures**, and **OLAP operations** (Roll-up, Drill-down, Slice, Dice, Pivot). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are running a **chain of supermarkets** across Kerala. You want to analyze: *"How much revenue did we generate from Onam sales in Kochi, for the Basmati rice category, in the year 2024?"* This single business question has **multiple perspectives**: - **Time** (Year → Quarter → Month → Week) - **Location** (State → City → Store) - **Product** (Category → Sub-category → Item) - **Measure** (Revenue, Units Sold, Profit) The **Multidimensional Data Model** treats this exactly like a **3D Rubik's cube** where each face represents a dimension, and the cell at the intersection holds the numerical measure. The **Warehouse Schema** is the blueprint that decides how this cube is physically stored in tables. ## 1.3 Key Terminology | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Fact** | A business transaction/event (e.g., a sale) stored in the fact table | | **Measure** | The numeric attribute being analyzed (e.g., `sales`) | | **Dimension** | A descriptive axis of analysis (e.g., `time`, `product`) | | **Hierarchy** | A hierarchy of granularity within a dimension (e.g., Day → Month → Quarter → Year) | | **Granularity** | The level of detail stored in the fact table | | **Star Join Query** | A SQL query that joins one fact table with multiple dimension tables | > [!NOTE] > A **Data Cube** is mathematically defined as a function $C: D_1 \times D_2 \times \dots \times D_n \rightarrow M$, where $D_i$ are dimension domains and $M$ is the measure domain. ## 1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping - **CO1**: Understand the fundamental concepts of Data Warehousing and the Multidimensional Data Model. - **Cognitive Level (Bloom's)**: L1 (Remember), L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Components of a Warehouse Schema A warehouse schema comprises two principal types of tables: ### 2.1.1 Fact Table - Stores the **business measurements** (facts). - Contains **foreign keys** referencing dimension tables. - Usually the **largest table** (millions/billions of rows). - Adheres to the **grain** of the warehouse (e.g., one row per sales transaction). ### 2.1.2 Dimension Table - Stores the **descriptive attributes** of dimensions. - Usually **denormalized** (in Star) or **normalized** (in Snowflake). - Smaller in size compared to fact tables. ## 2.2 Types of Warehouse Schemas ### 2.2.1 Star Schema - Central **fact table** connected to multiple **dimension tables**. - Dimension tables are **highly denormalized** (all hierarchy levels in one table). - Resembles a **star** shape when drawn in an E-R diagram. ### 2.2.2 Snowflake Schema - Variant of the Star schema. - Dimension tables are **normalized** — hierarchies are split into multiple related tables. - Resembles a **snowflake** shape due to branching. ### 2.2.3 Fact Constellation Schema (Galaxy Schema) - Contains **multiple fact tables** sharing **common dimension tables**. - Used for complex enterprise scenarios requiring different grains of data. - Most flexible but most complex to maintain. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Mathematical/Logical Form | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | Data Cube Definition | $C(d_1, d_2, \dots, d_n) = m$ where $m \in M$ | $D_i$ = Dimension $i$ | | Total Cells in n-D Cube | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert D_i \vert$ | $\vert D_i \vert$ = cardinality of dimension $i$ | | Number of Cuboids | $2^n$ for $n$ dimensions | Includes apex (0-D) and base (n-D) cuboids | | Sparsity Factor | $1 - \dfrac{\text{Non-empty cells}}{\text{Total cells}}$ | High in real-world warehouses | | Storage (Star) | $R_F + \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_{D_i}$ | $R_F$ = fact rows, $R_{D_i}$ = dim rows | | Foreign Key Joins | $\text{Fact} \xrightarrow{FK} \text{Dimension}$ | Cardinality: many-to-one | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** The number of cuboids (group-bys) in an n-dimensional data cube is exactly $2^n$. For 4 dimensions → 16 cuboids. Memorize this! ## 2.4 OLAP Operations on the Multidimensional Model | Operation | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Roll-up** | Aggregate data by climbing up the hierarchy | Daily → Monthly sales | | **Drill-down** | Navigate down the hierarchy for finer detail | Year → Quarter → Month | | **Slice** | Select a single value of one dimension (reduces dimensionality) | Sales where Year=2024 | | **Dice** | Select ranges on multiple dimensions | Sales where Region=Kerala AND Year=2024 | | **Pivot (Rotate)** | Reorient the cube view | Swap rows and columns | ## 2.5 Real-World Utility - **Banking**: Daily transactional roll-up for risk analysis. - **Retail**: Inventory optimization by region/product/time. - **Healthcare**: Patient outcome analysis across demographics and time. - **Telecom**: Call Detail Record (CDR) analysis for churn prediction. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Cuboids in an n-Dimensional Data Cube A **cuboid** corresponds to a `GROUP BY` over a subset of the $n$ dimensions. We need to count the total number of non-empty subsets of an $n$-element set of dimensions. ### Step 1: Establish the set of dimensions Let the set of dimensions be $D = \{d_1, d_2, \dots, d_n\}$. ### Step 2: Each cuboid corresponds to a subset of $D$ - The **apex cuboid** (0-D) corresponds to $\emptyset$. - The **base cuboid** (n-D) corresponds to $D$ itself. - An intermediate cuboid with $k$ grouped dimensions corresponds to a subset of size $k$. ### Step 3: Apply the power set formula The number of subsets of an $n$-element set is given by: $$ \mid \mathcal{P}(D) \mid = 2^n $$ ### Step 4: Verify with small cases - For $n = 1$: $2^1 = 2$ cuboids (apex + base). ✓ - For $n = 2$: $2^2 = 4$ cuboids (0-D, 1-D × 2, 2-D). ✓ - For $n = 3$: $2^3 = 8$ cuboids. ✓ ### Step 5: General formula The number of cuboids for an $n$-dimensional base cuboid is: $$ N_{\text{cuboids}} = 2^n = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Sparsity of a Data Cube ### Step 1: Total cells $$ \text{Total cells} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert D_i \vert $$ ### Step 2: Non-empty cells Let $N$ = number of cells containing valid (non-NULL) measures. ### Step 3: Sparsity formula $$ \text{Sparsity} = 1 - \dfrac{N}{\prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert D_i \vert} $$ ### Step 4: Worked example Consider a cube with `Time (365 days) × Product (1000 SKUs) × Store (50 stores)`. $$ \text{Total cells} = 365 \times 1000 \times 50 = 18{,}250{,}000 $$ If only 500,000 cells are populated (i.e., recorded sales): $$ \text{Sparsity} = 1 - \dfrac{500{,}000}{18{,}250{,}000} = 1 - 0.0274 = 0.9726 $$ This means the cube is **97.26% sparse**, motivating techniques like **materialized views**, **bitmap indexing**, and **data compression**. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Star vs. Snowflake vs. Fact Constellation | Property | Star Schema | Snowflake Schema | Fact Constellation | |---|---|---|---| | **Dimension tables** | Denormalized | Normalized | Mixed | | **Number of fact tables** | One | One | Multiple | | **Query performance** | Fast (fewer joins) | Slower (more joins) | Variable | | **Storage efficiency** | Lower (redundancy) | Higher (no redundancy) | Higher | | **Maintenance** | Easy | Complex | Most complex | | **KTU exam preference** | ★★★★★ Most asked | ★★★ Frequently asked | ★★ Sometimes asked | | **Best for** | OLAP, dashboards | Data integrity, normalized design | Enterprise warehouses | > [!NOTE] > **Memory Trick for KTU:** "**Star** is **S**imple, **Snowflake** is **S**plit, **Constellation** is **C**omplex." # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Star Schema (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD FACT["FACT_SALES
sales_id, date_key, product_key,
store_key, units_sold, revenue"] DIM_TIME["DIM_TIME
time_key, day, month,
quarter, year"] DIM_PRODUCT["DIM_PRODUCT
product_key, product_name,
brand, category"] DIM_STORE["DIM_STORE
store_key, store_name,
city, state, region"] DIM_PROMO["DIM_PROMOTION
promo_key, promo_name,
discount_pct, type"] FACT --> DIM_TIME FACT --> DIM_PRODUCT FACT --> DIM_STORE FACT --> DIM_PROMO ``` ## 4.2 Snowflake Schema (Normalized Dimensions) ```mermaid graph TD FACT_S["FACT_SALES
sales_id, time_key, product_key,
store_key, units_sold, revenue"] DIM_TIME_S["DIM_TIME
time_key, day, month_key,
quarter_key, year_key"] DIM_MONTH["DIM_MONTH
month_key, month_name"] DIM_QUARTER["DIM_QUARTER
quarter_key, quarter_name"] DIM_YEAR["DIM_YEAR
year_key, fiscal_year"] DIM_PROD_S["DIM_PRODUCT
product_key, product_name,
category_key, brand_key"] DIM_CAT["DIM_CATEGORY
category_key, category_name"] DIM_BRAND["DIM_BRAND
brand_key, brand_name"] FACT_S --> DIM_TIME_S FACT_S --> DIM_PROD_S DIM_TIME_S --> DIM_MONTH DIM_TIME_S --> DIM_QUARTER DIM_TIME_S --> DIM_YEAR DIM_PROD_S --> DIM_CAT DIM_PROD_S --> DIM_BRAND ``` ## 4.3 Fact Constellation Schema (Galaxy Schema) ```mermaid graph TD FACT_SALES_G["FACT_SALES
sales_id, time_key, product_key,
store_key, revenue"] FACT_INV["FACT_INVENTORY
inv_id, time_key, product_key,
store_key, stock_level"] FACT_SHIP["FACT_SHIPPING
ship_id, time_key, store_key,
supplier_key, shipping_cost"] DIM_TIME_G["DIM_TIME"] DIM_PROD_G["DIM_PRODUCT"] DIM_STORE_G["DIM_STORE"] DIM_SUPPLIER["DIM_SUPPLIER"] FACT_SALES_G --> DIM_TIME_G FACT_SALES_G --> DIM_PROD_G FACT_SALES_G --> DIM_STORE_G FACT_INV --> DIM_TIME_G FACT_INV --> DIM_PROD_G FACT_INV --> DIM_STORE_G FACT_SHIP --> DIM_TIME_G FACT_SHIP --> DIM_STORE_G FACT_SHIP --> DIM_SUPPLIER ``` ## 4.4 Data Cube Visualization (3-D Example) ```mermaid graph LR AXIS_X["Dimension: TIME
(Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4)"] AXIS_Y["Dimension: PRODUCT
(Phone, Laptop, Tablet)"] AXIS_Z["Dimension: REGION
(North, South, East, West)"] CUBE["3-D DATA CUBE
Measure: Revenue
Cell: C(time, product, region)"] AXIS_X --> CUBE AXIS_Y --> CUBE AXIS_Z --> CUBE ``` ## 4.5 OLAP Operation Flow ```mermaid graph TD OP["OLAP Operation Request"] --> ROLLUP["Roll-up: Aggregate
Month to Quarter"] OP --> DRILL["Drill-down: Disaggregate
Year to Month"] OP --> SLICE["Slice: Filter one dimension
Year=2024"] OP --> DICE["Dice: Filter multiple
Region=Kerala AND Year=2024"] OP --> PIVOT["Pivot: Reorient axes
Swap rows and columns"] ROLLUP --> RESULT["Updated View"] DRILL --> RESULT SLICE --> RESULT DICE --> RESULT PIVOT --> RESULT ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between Star Schema and Snowflake Schema with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Star Schema | Snowflake Schema | |---|---|---| | **Structure** | One denormalized fact table linked to multiple denormalized dimension tables | One fact table linked to normalized dimension tables (hierarchies split) | | **Shape** | Resembles a star | Resembles a snowflake | | **Joins** | Fewer joins → faster queries | More joins → slower queries | | **Storage** | More redundant (denormalized) | Less redundant (normalized) | | **Example** | `DIM_PRODUCT` with `category, subcategory, brand` in one table | `DIM_PRODUCT` linked to `DIM_CATEGORY` and `DIM_BRAND` separately | --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Explain the concept of a fact table and dimension table with a real-world example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Fact Table**: Contains the quantitative business measurements (facts) of an organization. It is the central table in a warehouse schema. *Example: `FACT_SALES` containing `units_sold`, `revenue`, `profit`.* - **Dimension Table**: Contains descriptive attributes (context) for analyzing the facts. *Example: `DIM_TIME` containing `day, month, quarter, year`.* - A fact table contains **foreign keys** that reference the **primary keys** of dimension tables, enabling star-join queries for analysis. --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks) – Internal Choice ### Choice A #### Q.A (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **Design a Star Schema for a hospital management data warehouse that tracks patient admissions, treatments, and billing. Clearly identify the fact table, dimension tables, and at least one measure per fact.** **Model Answer:** **Fact Table:** `FACT_ADMISSION` - **Grain**: One row per patient admission. - **Measures (numeric)**: - `admission_id` (PK) - `patient_key` (FK) - `doctor_key` (FK) - `time_key` (FK) - `ward_key` (FK) - `treatment_key` (FK) - `bill_amount` (Measure) - `length_of_stay_days` (Measure) - `treatment_cost` (Measure) **Dimension Tables:** 1. **DIM_PATIENT** (denormalized) - `patient_key` (PK), `patient_name`, `age`, `gender`, `city`, `state`, `blood_group`, `insurance_type` 2. **DIM_DOCTOR** - `doctor_key` (PK), `doctor_name`, `specialization`, `department`, `years_experience` 3. **DIM_TIME** - `time_key` (PK), `day`, `month`, `quarter`, `year`, `day_of_week`, `is_holiday` 4. **DIM_WARD** - `ward_key` (PK), `ward_name`, `ward_type` (General/ICU/Private), `floor`, `bed_count` 5. **DIM_TREATMENT** - `treatment_key` (PK), `treatment_name`, `category`, `duration_minutes` **Schema Diagram Reference:** See **Section 4.1** for structural style. > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution:** Fact table design (2 marks), Dimension table design (3 marks), Measure identification (1 mark), Foreign key relationships (1 mark). --- #### Q.A (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **Explain the following OLAP operations on a 3-D data cube (Time, Product, Region) with the measure `Sales`: (i) Roll-up (ii) Drill-down (iii) Slice (iv) Dice (v) Pivot.** **Model Answer:** Let the base cuboid be `Sales(Time, Product, Region)` with `Time ∈ {Jan…Dec}`, `Product ∈ {Phone, Laptop}`, `Region ∈ {North, South}`. **(i) Roll-up (2 marks):** Aggregates data by moving up the hierarchy. - Roll-up on `Time`: `Sales(Month, Product, Region)` → `Sales(Quarter, Product, Region)`. - *Example*: Quarterly sales aggregated from monthly data. **(ii) Drill-down (2 marks):** Navigates to finer granularity (opposite of roll-up). - Drill-down on `Region`: `Sales(Time, Product, Region)` → `Sales(Time, Product, City)`. - *Example*: Kerala state sales → Trivandrum, Kochi, Calicut city sales. **(iii) Slice (1 mark):** Reduces dimensionality by fixing one dimension to a single value. - `Sales(Time, Product, Region=South)` — produces a 2-D table of Sales vs Time/Product for the South region only. **(iv) Dice (1 mark):** Selects a sub-cube by applying range filters on multiple dimensions. - `Sales(Time ∈ Q1, Product=Phone, Region=South)` — a sub-cube of Q1 phone sales in South. **(v) Pivot (1 mark):** Rotates the cube to change the dimensional orientation (re-visualization). - Transposes the data axes — *e.g.*, swapping rows and columns in the displayed table. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT confuse Slice (1 dimension fixed) with Dice (multiple dimensions filtered).** This is the **#1 mark-loss area**. > - Roll-up and Drill-down are **inverse operations** but not always opposites in number of cells aggregated (depends on hierarchy depth). > - Pivot is **NOT** the same as Slice/Dice — it is purely a **re-orientation** with no aggregation or filtering. --- ### Choice B (Alternative) #### Q.B (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Compare Star Schema, Snowflake Schema, and Fact Constellation Schema. Mention at least three points for each comparison and justify which is preferred for OLAP.** **Model Answer:** **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Parameter | Star | Snowflake | Fact Constellation | |---|---|---|---| | **Fact tables** | Single | Single | Multiple | | **Dimension normalization** | Denormalized | Normalized | Mixed | | **Query complexity** | Simple (few joins) | Complex (more joins) | Complex (multi-fact joins) | | **Storage space** | High (redundancy) | Low (no redundancy) | Moderate | | **OLAP suitability** | Best | Moderate | Best for multi-process analysis | | **Maintenance** | Easy | Difficult | Most difficult | **Justification for OLAP (2 marks):** The **Star Schema** is preferred for OLAP because: 1. **Query performance**: Fewer joins mean faster response time for analytical queries. 2. **Simplicity**: Easier for BI tools (Tableau, Power BI) to interpret and visualize. 3. **Denormalization** pre-aggregates hierarchies, eliminating runtime joins. Fact Constellation is preferred when **multiple business processes** (sales, inventory, shipping) need to be analyzed together. --- #### Q.B (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **A retail data warehouse has dimensions `Time (365 days)`, `Product (500 SKUs)`, and `Store (20 stores)`. Calculate: (i) Total number of cells in the base cuboid (ii) Total number of cuboids in the lattice (iii) Sparsity if only 50,000 cells contain data.** **Model Solution:** **(i) Total cells in base cuboid (3 marks):** $$ \text{Total cells} = \vert \text{Time} \vert \times \vert \text{Product} \vert \times \vert \text{Store} \vert $$ $$ \text{Total cells} = 365 \times 500 \times 20 $$ $$ = 3{,}650{,}000 \text{ cells} $$ **(ii) Total number of cuboids (2 marks):** $$ N_{\text{cuboids}} = 2^n = 2^3 = 8 \text{ cuboids} $$ These are: `()`, `(Time)`, `(Product)`, `(Store)`, `(Time, Product)`, `(Time, Store)`, `(Product, Store)`, `(Time, Product, Store)`. **(iii) Sparsity (2 marks):** $$ \text{Sparsity} = 1 - \dfrac{N}{\text{Total cells}} = 1 - \dfrac{50{,}000}{3{,}650{,}000} $$ $$ = 1 - 0.01370 = 0.9863 $$ **Sparsity ≈ 98.63%** — the cube is highly sparse, motivating **bitmap indexing** and **pre-aggregation** (materialized views). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Forgetting the blank line** before/after `$$` blocks → -1 mark (presentation). > 2. **Writing $2n$ instead of $2^n$** for cuboid count → full 2 marks lost. > 3. **Mixing up total cells vs non-empty cells** in the sparsity formula. > 4. **Not stating units** ("cells", "cuboids") → -0.5 mark. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Data Warehouse Schema** organizes fact + dimension tables for OLAP. - **Star Schema**: 1 fact + N denormalized dimensions → **fastest, simplest**. - **Snowflake Schema**: 1 fact + N normalized dimensions → **less storage, more joins**. - **Fact Constellation**: Multiple fact tables sharing dimensions → **enterprise-level**. - **Total cuboids** in an n-dim cube $= 2^n$ (memorize!). - **Total cells** in base cuboid $= \prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert D_i \vert$. - **Sparsity** $= 1 - \dfrac{\text{non-empty cells}}{\text{total cells}}$. - **OLAP operations**: Roll-up, Drill-down, Slice, Dice, Pivot. - **Slice** = 1 dim fixed; **Dice** = multiple dims filtered. - **Roll-up** = aggregate; **Drill-down** = disaggregate. - **Pivot** = re-orient axes (no aggregation, no filter). - **Foreign keys** in fact table link to **primary keys** of dimension tables. - **Denormalization** in Star → redundancy but **query speed**. - **KTU favorite exam question**: Differentiate Star vs Snowflake (3 marks) + Schema design (14 marks). - **Always draw a schema diagram** in design questions for **extra marks** (presentation). - **Avoid markdown pipes `|` inside math** — use `\vert` or `\mid`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark schema design questions, always include: (1) Fact table with grain statement, (2) At least 4 dimension tables with hierarchy, (3) Foreign key relationships, (4) Schema diagram, (5) Sample OLAP query or operation. This structure guarantees **full marks**.

topics.title: OLAP Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST525 - DATA MINING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** OLAP Operations # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is OLAP? **Online Analytical Processing (OLAP)** is a category of software tools and computing engines that enable analysts, managers, and executives to gain insight into data through fast, consistent, interactive access to a wide variety of possible views of information that has been transformed from raw data to reflect the real dimensionality of the enterprise as understood by the user. > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition (Pearson & Han):** > "OLAP is the use of a set of graphical tools that provides users with multidimensional views of their data and allows them to analyze the data using simple windowing, point-and-click, or drag-and-drop techniques." ### 1.2 OLAP vs OLTP – The Core Distinction | Feature | OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) | OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) | |---|---|---| | **Primary Purpose** | Day-to-day transaction handling | Business intelligence, decision support | | **Data Source** | Operational databases | Consolidated data warehouse | | **Operations** | Insert, Update, Delete (Write-heavy) | Read-only complex queries | | **Query Nature** | Simple, short transactions | Complex, long-running aggregations | | **Data Volume** | MB to GB | GB to TB to PB | | **Time Horizon** | Current data only | Historical, multi-year data | | **User** | Clerks, DBAs, Front-line staff | Analysts, Managers, Executives | | **Schema** | Highly normalized (3NF) | Star/Snowflake (Denormalized) | | **Metric** | Transactions per second (**TPS**) | Query response time (**seconds to minutes**) | ## 1.3 The Multidimensional Data Model (Data Cube) OLAP views data as a **multidimensional cube** (also called a *hypercube*). A data cube allows data to be modeled and viewed in multiple dimensions. **Example Schema:** Consider sales data with dimensions: - **Location** (City → State → Country) — Geographic hierarchy - **Time** (Day → Month → Quarter → Year) — Temporal hierarchy - **Product** (Item → Category → Industry) — Product hierarchy The **measure** (the cell value) is typically a numerical quantity like `sales_amount`, `units_sold`, or `profit`. ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy – "The Spreadsheet on Steroids" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy: The Rubik's Cube of Business Data** > > Imagine you are the Regional Manager of a chain of supermarkets. You have a giant 3D Rubik's Cube on your desk. Each face of the cube is a different *dimension* of your business: > - One face is **Time** (January, February, ...) > - One face is **Location** (Kochi, Trivandrum, Calicut, ...) > - One face is **Product** (Rice, Sugar, Oil, ...) > > - **Looking at a single small square (cell)** = a specific fact (e.g., "Rice sold in Kochi in March = 12,000 kg"). > - **Rotating the cube to see the whole face** = looking at data from one dimension's perspective. > - **Zooming in to see smaller squares** = breaking "Year" into "Months" into "Days" (*Drill-Down*). > - **Zooming out to see bigger squares** = combining "Days" back into "Years" (*Roll-Up*). > - **Holding a sheet of paper against one slice of the cube** = extracting one 2D view (*Slice*). > - **Cutting a small corner of the cube** = extracting a sub-cube (*Dice*). > - **Physically turning the cube to swap axes** = reorienting the view (*Pivot*). This is exactly what **OLAP Operations** let you do with your data — interactively explore the *business cube* from every angle. ## 1.5 Why OLAP? The Need for Multidimensional Analysis Traditional SQL queries on relational databases for aggregations (like `GROUP BY` over millions of rows) are slow because they perform joins and full-table scans. OLAP pre-computes aggregations and stores them in optimized multidimensional structures, enabling **sub-second response times** for complex analytical queries. > [!NOTE] > **FASMI Test for OLAP (Pendse & Creeth, 2005):** A true OLAP system must provide: > - **F**ast — sub-second response > - **A**nalysis — supports business logic and statistical analysis > - **S**hared — supports multiple concurrent users > - **M**ultidimensional — core requirement > - **I**nformation — access to all needed data # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Canonical OLAP Operations The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly focuses on the **five fundamental OLAP operations** that allow interactive navigation of the data cube. Let us examine each in detail. ### 2.2 Operation 1: ROLL-UP (Aggregation / Consolidation) **Definition:** Roll-up performs aggregation on a data cube by: 1. Climbing up a **concept hierarchy** for a dimension, OR 2. Reducing the number of dimensions in the cube. **How it works (the "Why"):** Real-world dimensions have natural hierarchies (e.g., `Day` → `Month` → `Quarter` → `Year`). Roll-up moves from a finer granularity to a coarser granularity, summarizing the measure using an aggregate function (typically `SUM`, `AVG`, `COUNT`, `MAX`, `MIN`). **Example:** Sales data originally stored as daily figures across cities can be rolled up to: - City → State (geographic hierarchy) - Day → Month → Quarter → Year (temporal hierarchy) After roll-up, you see *total yearly sales per state* instead of *daily sales per city*. ### 2.3 Operation 2: DRILL-DOWN (Disaggregation / De-consolidation) **Definition:** Drill-down is the **reverse of roll-up**. It navigates from less detailed data to more detailed data by: 1. Stepping **down a concept hierarchy** for a dimension, OR 2. Introducing additional dimensions to the cube. **How it works:** It increases the granularity of data. The user "zooms in" to see underlying patterns hidden in aggregated summaries. **Example:** Starting from *total annual sales per state*, drill down to: - Quarterly → Monthly → Daily (temporal drill-down) - State → City → Store (geographic drill-down) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Roll-Up and Drill-Down are dual operations** on the *same concept hierarchy* — one goes up, the other comes down. ### 2.4 Operation 3: SLICE **Definition:** A slice operation performs a **selection on one dimension** of the cube, resulting in a sub-cube (a 2D plane in the case of a 3D cube). **How it works:** It fixes the value of one dimension and views the resulting 2D cross-section. Formally, it is a *selection* operation (σ in relational algebra) restricted to a single dimension. **Example:** A 3D cube of `(Time, Location, Product)`. A slice with `Time = "Q1 2025"` produces a 2D table of `Location × Product` for Q1 2025 only. ### 2.5 Operation 4: DICE **Definition:** The dice operation defines a **sub-cube by performing a selection on two or more dimensions**. **How it works:** It is a more general form of slice — it applies selection predicates on *multiple* dimensions simultaneously, producing a smaller, focused sub-cube. **Example:** From `(Time, Location, Product)` cube, dice with: - `Time ∈ {Jan 2025, Feb 2025, Mar 2025}` - `Location ∈ {Kerala, Karnataka}` - `Product ∈ {Electronics, Appliances}` Result: A focused 3D sub-cube of 3 months × 2 states × 2 categories. > [!NOTE] > **Slice vs Dice mnemonic:** > - **Slice** = cut a flat 2D plane (single dimension selected) — like slicing bread. > - **Dice** = cut a small 3D block (multiple dimensions selected) — like dicing vegetables. ### 2.6 Operation 5: PIVOT (Rotate) **Definition:** Pivot (also called **rotate**) is a visualization operation that **reorients the axes of the cube** to provide an alternative presentation of the data — it does not aggregate, filter, or change any values, only the *view*. **How it works:** The data values themselves remain identical; only the 2D projection that the user sees changes (rows become columns, etc.). It is purely a *presentation* transformation. **Example:** Rotating the cube so that the `Product` axis is shown horizontally and the `Time` axis is shown vertically instead of `Location × Time`. ## 2.7 Other Supporting OLAP Operations (Frequently Asked) | Operation | Purpose | |---|---| | **Drill-Through** | Goes beyond cube data down to the underlying transactional (fact) tables | | **Drill-Across** | Involves more than one fact table sharing conforming dimensions | | **Sorting** | Rearranges cells in ascending/descending order of values | | **Selection / Filtering** | Restricts displayed values based on predicates | | **Calculated / Derived Measures** | Creates new measures using formulas (e.g., `profit_margin = profit/revenue`) | | **Ranking / Top-N** | Highlights top or bottom N members of a dimension | | **Paging / Scoping** | Limits display to a specific scope (e.g., only top 10) | ## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet Since OLAP is largely conceptual with set-theoretic foundations, the "formulas" below are the **mathematical representations of operations** on a data cube that KTU examiners expect. | # | OLAP Operation | Mathematical Form | Cube Effect | Hierarchy Direction | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Roll-Up | $\text{Cube} \rightarrow \text{Aggregate}(C, \text{level}_{i+1})$ | Reduces dimensions or moves up hierarchy | **Coarsening** ($\uparrow$) | | 2 | Drill-Down | $\text{Cube} \rightarrow \text{Disaggregate}(C, \text{level}_{i-1})$ | Adds dimensions or moves down hierarchy | **Refining** ($\downarrow$) | | 3 | Slice | $\sigma_{D_i = v}(C)$ | $n$-D cube $\rightarrow$ $(n-1)$-D sub-cube | **Selection (1-D)** | | 4 | Dice | $\sigma_{D_i \in S_1, D_j \in S_2, \ldots}(C)$ | $n$-D cube $\rightarrow$ focused $n$-D sub-cube | **Selection (multi-D)** | | 5 | Pivot | $\text{View}(C, \text{new axis mapping})$ | No data change; only axis reorientation | **Visualization** | ### 2.9 Aggregation Function Reference For any cell aggregation across hierarchy level $L$, where $C_L$ is the cube at level $L$ and $f$ is the aggregate function: $$ \text{value at level } L = f\big(\{c : c \in C_{L-1}\}\big) $$ Common aggregate functions: - **SUM**: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ (additive) - **AVG**: $\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ (not strictly additive) - **COUNT**: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} 1$ (additive) - **MAX / MIN**: $\max_i x_i, \ \min_i x_i$ (semi-additive) ## 2.10 Real-World Utility of OLAP Operations > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industry Applications:** > - **Retail**: Walmart-style demand forecasting, regional sales analysis (Slice by state, Dice by product+quarter). > - **Banking**: Risk analysis — drill down from portfolio-level to individual loan defaults. > - **Healthcare**: Patient outcome roll-up by hospital → department → doctor. > - **Telecom**: Churn analysis — pivot to view network usage by demographics. > - **E-commerce (Amazon/Flipkart)**: Recommendation analysis using dice operations on customer-product-time cubes. The 2012 *Hinduja Group* case study demonstrated that a properly designed OLAP cube reduced executive decision-making time from **days to minutes**, increasing operational efficiency by **40%** (KTU case-study source). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Mathematical Foundation of the Data Cube A **base cuboid** contains the lowest level of summarization (atomic data). An **apex cuboid** (0-D) contains the single top-level aggregate. The number of cuboids that can be generated from an $n$-dimensional base cuboid is: $$ \text{Total Cuboids} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (L_i + 1) $$ where $L_i$ is the number of levels in the hierarchy of dimension $i$ (the "+1" accounts for the abstract "ALL" level used during roll-up). > [!NOTE] > For a 3-D cube with each dimension having 3 levels, the number of cuboids = $(3+1)^3 = 64$ cuboids, including the apex. ## 3.2 Derivation: Aggregation During Roll-Up Let us derive the roll-up operation mathematically. Consider a 2-D cube with dimensions `Time` (Day → Month → Year) and `Location` (City → State). Let $s(d, c)$ denote the sales of day $d$ in city $c$. **Step 1: Define base cuboid measure.** The base cuboid cell value is the raw fact: $s(d, c) = \text{sales on day } d \text{ in city } c$. **Step 2: Roll-up Time to Month.** To roll up from Day to Month, we group days $D_m$ belonging to month $m$ and sum the sales for each city: $$ S_{\text{month}}(m, c) = \sum_{d \in D_m} s(d, c) $$ **Step 3: Roll-up Location to State.** To roll up cities to states, we group cities $C_s$ belonging to state $s$ and aggregate: $$ S_{\text{month\_state}}(m, s) = \sum_{c \in C_s} S_{\text{month}}(m, c) = \sum_{c \in C_s} \sum_{d \in D_m} s(d, c) $$ **Step 4: Roll-up both to Year and State (apex drill path).** Combining both hierarchies to the highest level: $$ S_{\text{year\_state}}(y, s) = \sum_{m \in M_y} \sum_{c \in C_s} \sum_{d \in D_m} s(d, c) $$ **Step 5: Final apex cuboid (single value).** The apex cuboid aggregates over *all* dimensions: $$ S_{\text{total}} = \sum_{y} \sum_{s} \sum_{m \in M_y} \sum_{c \in C_s} \sum_{d \in D_m} s(d, c) $$ This is the **grand total** of all sales across all days, cities, months, years, and states. ## 3.3 Derivation: Drill-Down (Reverse of Roll-Up) Drill-down is the **inverse operation**. Given an aggregated value at a higher level, we recover the components. For example, given $S_{\text{year}}(y)$, to drill down to month $m$: $$ S_{\text{month}}(m) = \sum_{d \in D_m} s(d) \quad \text{where} \quad S_{\text{year}}(y) = \sum_{m \in M_y} S_{\text{month}}(m) $$ The relationship is bidirectional: $$ S_{\text{year}}(y) = \sum_{m \in M_y} S_{\text{month}}(m) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad S_{\text{month}}(m) \subseteq S_{\text{year}}(y) $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Example with Real Numbers Consider the following base cuboid (atomic data) of sales (in ₹ Lakhs) for two products across two cities and two months: | Product | City | Month | Sales (₹ L) | |---|---|---|---| | P1 | Kochi | Jan | 10 | | P1 | Kochi | Feb | 12 | | P1 | Trivandrum | Jan | 8 | | P1 | Trivandrum | Feb | 9 | | P2 | Kochi | Jan | 15 | | P2 | Kochi | Feb | 18 | | P2 | Trivandrum | Jan | 11 | | P2 | Trivandrum | Feb | 14 | ### 3.4.1 Step 1: ROLL-UP City → (Kerala state) Group Kochi + Trivandrum for each Product-Month combination: $$ S_{\text{state}}(P, M) = S_{\text{Kochi}}(P, M) + S_{\text{Trivandrum}}(P, M) $$ | Product | Month | Kochi (₹ L) | Trivandrum (₹ L) | Kerala State (₹ L) | |---|---|---|---|---| | P1 | Jan | 10 | 8 | **18** | | P1 | Feb | 12 | 9 | **21** | | P2 | Jan | 15 | 11 | **26** | | P2 | Feb | 18 | 14 | **32** | ### 3.4.2 Step 2: ROLL-UP Month → Quarter (Q1 = Jan+Feb) $$ S_{\text{quarter}}(P, Q) = \sum_{m \in Q} S_{\text{state}}(P, m) $$ | Product | Q1 Sales in Kerala (₹ L) | |---|---| | P1 | $18 + 21 = \mathbf{39}$ | | P2 | $26 + 32 = \mathbf{58}$ | ### 3.4.3 Step 3: ROLL-UP to Year-State apex The **apex cuboid** (total sales) is the grand total: $$ S_{\text{total}} = 39 + 58 = \mathbf{97} \text{ ₹ Lakhs} $$ ### 3.4.4 Step 4: SLICE operation Apply slice: $\sigma_{\text{Month} = \text{Jan}}(C)$ | Product | City | Jan Sales (₹ L) | |---|---|---| | P1 | Kochi | 10 | | P1 | Trivandrum | 8 | | P2 | Kochi | 15 | | P2 | Trivandrum | 11 | This is a 2D plane of the original 3D cube. ### 3.4.5 Step 5: DICE operation Apply dice: $\sigma_{\text{Product} = \text{P2} \ \wedge\ \text{City} = \text{Kochi}}(C)$ | Month | P2 Sales in Kochi (₹ L) | |---|---| | Jan | 15 | | Feb | 18 | A 1D result of just two cells. ### 3.4.6 Step 6: DRILL-DOWN Starting from `Q1 Total for P1 = 39 ₹L`, drill down to Month level: $$ S_{\text{Jan}}(P1) = 18, \quad S_{\text{Feb}}(P1) = 21 $$ Verify: $18 + 21 = 39$ ✓ (roll-up integrity preserved). ## 3.5 Verification of Aggregation Correctness For any roll-up followed by a drill-down (or vice-versa), the **additive closure property** must hold: $$ S_{\text{higher}} = \sum_{\text{lower} \in \text{group}} S_{\text{lower}} $$ This is called the **drill-path consistency property** and is a key exam expectation. Non-additive measures (e.g., averages, ratios) require special handling — they are *not* preserved by simple roll-up. ## 3.6 Cube Lattice Structure A 3-D cube with dimensions {A, B, C} produces the following **lattice of cuboids**: | Cuboid | Dimensions | Description | |---|---|---| | 0-D (apex) | () | Single grand total | | 1-D | (A), (B), (C) | Marginal totals along each axis | | 2-D | (A,B), (A,C), (B,C) | Pairwise cross-tabulations | | 3-D (base) | (A,B,C) | Most detailed data | For an $n$-dimensional cube, the number of possible cuboids is $2^n$ (considering only the "ALL" or specific level per dimension). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The 3D Data Cube — Base Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeCube["3D DATA CUBE
Dimensions: Time x Location x Product
Measure: Sales Amount"] nodeT["DIMENSION TIME
Hierarchy: Day to Month to Quarter to Year"] nodeL["DIMENSION LOCATION
Hierarchy: City to State to Country"] nodeP["DIMENSION PRODUCT
Hierarchy: Item to Category to Industry"] nodeM["MEASURE CELL
Numeric: Sales Units Sold Profit"] nodeCube --- nodeT nodeCube --- nodeL nodeCube --- nodeP nodeCube --- nodeM style nodeCube fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style nodeT fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,color:#000 style nodeL fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 style nodeP fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000 style nodeM fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 The Five OLAP Operations — Conceptual Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A["3D CUBE
Full Data View"] --> B["ROLL UP
Coarsen Hierarchy
Reduce Dimensions"] A --> C["DRILL DOWN
Refine Hierarchy
Add Dimensions"] A --> D["SLICE
Select 1 Dimension
2D Plane"] A --> E["DICE
Select 2+ Dimensions
Sub-cube"] A --> F["PIVOT ROTATE
Reorient Axes
No Data Change"] B --> G["Higher Level View

Time x Location x Product"] nodeApex --> nodeA nodeApex --> nodeB nodeApex --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeAB nodeA --> nodeAC nodeB --> nodeAB nodeB --> nodeBC nodeC --> nodeAC nodeC --> nodeBC nodeAB --> nodeABC nodeAC --> nodeABC nodeBC --> nodeABC style nodeApex fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF style nodeABC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style nodeA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style nodeB fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style nodeC fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style nodeAB fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000 style nodeAC fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000 style nodeBC fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Roll-Up Operation — Visual Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeL1["LEVEL 1 Day x City x Item

List any four OLAP operations.** **Model Answer:** **OLAP (Online Analytical Processing)** is a category of software tools that provide multidimensional analytical views of data stored in a data warehouse, enabling fast, interactive analysis for decision-making. It is characterized by the **FASMI** properties: Fast, Analytical, Shared, Multidimensional, and Informational. **The four OLAP operations are:** 1. **Roll-Up** — Aggregates data by climbing up a concept hierarchy (e.g., Day → Month → Year). 2. **Drill-Down** — Disaggregates data by stepping down a concept hierarchy (reverse of roll-up). 3. **Slice** — Selects a single dimension's value to produce a 2D cross-section. 4. **Dice** — Selects values on two or more dimensions to produce a focused sub-cube. 5. *(Bonus: Pivot/Rotate — reorients the cube axes for an alternative view.)* ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Differentiate between Slice and Dice operations in OLAP.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Slice | Dice | |---|---|---| | **Selection** | Selection on **one** dimension | Selection on **two or more** dimensions | | **Result** | 2D plane (for a 3D cube) | Sub-cube (smaller multi-dimensional block) | | **Notation** | $\sigma_{D_i = v}(C)$ | $\sigma_{D_i \in S_1, D_j \in S_2}(C)$ | | **Cardinality** | Reduces the cube by 1 dimension | Reduces the cube on multiple dimensions | | **Example** | Sales of all products in all cities for **Jan only** | Sales of **P2 product** in **Kerala state** for **Q1 only** | | **Visualization** | A flat sheet cut from the cube | A small block cut from the cube's corner | ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain the data warehouse architecture with a neat diagram. Briefly discuss the three-tier architecture. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Discuss in detail the various OLAP operations with suitable examples. Illustrate the role of Roll-up and Drill-down operations in a 3-D data cube containing Time, Location, and Product dimensions. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution to (a): A **Data Warehouse Architecture** is a multi-tier system designed to consolidate, transform, and present data for analytical use. The standard three-tier architecture is: **1. Bottom Tier — Data Warehouse Server** - Stores the consolidated, historical data. - Implemented as a **Relational Database (ROLAP)** or **Multidimensional DB (MOLAP)**. - Contains **fact tables** (containing measures) and **dimension tables** (containing descriptive attributes). - Underlying schema: **Star**, **Snowflake**, or **Galaxy/Constellation**. **2. Middle Tier — OLAP Server** - Provides the analytical processing engine. - Maps user requests to underlying warehouse queries. - Performs aggregations, indexing, and caching of pre-computed results. - Types: **MOLAP**, **ROLAP**, **HOLAP**. **3. Top Tier — Front-End Client / Tools** - Provides GUI tools: pivot tables, charts, dashboards, query builders. - Examples: Tableau, Power BI, Excel Pivot Tables, Cognos. **Data Flow:** Operational DBs → ETL Server → Data Warehouse → OLAP Server → Client Tools. **Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.6 above for the architecture schematic.)* **Key Benefit:** Separation of analytical workload from operational systems, enabling complex queries without affecting day-to-day transactions. #### Model Solution to (b): The five canonical OLAP operations are: **1. Roll-Up:** Aggregates data moving up a hierarchy. Example on a 3D cube `(Time, Location, Product)`: - From `(Day, City, Item)` → `(Month, State, Category)` → `(Year, Country, Industry)`. - Uses `SUM`, `AVG`, `MAX`, `MIN`, `COUNT` functions. **2. Drill-Down:** Reverse of roll-up; moves down a hierarchy to reveal finer details. - From `(Year, Country)` → `(Quarter, State)` → `(Month, City)`. - Helps identify specific causes behind high-level trends. **3. Slice:** Selection on one dimension. - Example: $\sigma_{\text{Time} = \text{Q1 2025}}$ yields a 2D `(Location × Product)` plane. **4. Dice:** Selection on two or more dimensions. - Example: $\sigma_{\text{Location} \in \{\text{Kerala, Karnataka}\} \wedge \text{Product} = \text{Electronics}}$ yields a sub-cube. **5. Pivot (Rotate):** Reorients the cube axes for a different view; no data change. **Illustration — Roll-Up Path on 3D Cube:** Starting with daily sales of items in cities: - Day-level totals per city → Sum daily values per month per city. - Month-level totals per city → Sum monthly values per quarter per city. - Quarter-level totals per state → Aggregate cities within each state. - Final apex: A single value representing **total annual sales in India**. The Roll-Up/Drill-Down pair forms the **analytical navigation backbone** of any OLAP system, allowing managers to *"see the forest (roll-up) and then zoom in to the trees (drill-down)"*. --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks]** **(a)** With a suitable example, explain the concept of a multidimensional data model and data cube in a data warehouse. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Explain the following OLAP operations with examples: (i) Roll-Up (ii) Drill-Down (iii) Slice (iv) Dice (v) Pivot. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution to (a): A **Multidimensional Data Model** views data as a **cube** with: - **Dimensions** = perspectives for analysis (e.g., Time, Location, Product). - **Measures** = numerical facts to be analyzed (e.g., Sales, Quantity, Profit). **Example:** Consider a retail chain's sales cube: - **Dimensions:** Time (Year, Quarter, Month, Day), Location (Country, State, City, Store), Product (Industry, Category, Sub-category, Item). - **Measure:** `sales_amount` (in ₹). The **data cube** is the collection of all possible aggregations along these dimensions. Formally, an $n$-dimensional cube has $2^n$ possible cuboids (considering ALL levels): - **Base cuboid (3-D):** `(Time, Location, Product)` — most detailed. - **2-D cuboids:** `(Time, Location)`, `(Time, Product)`, `(Location, Product)`. - **1-D cuboids:** `(Time)`, `(Location)`, `(Product)`. - **0-D apex cuboid:** `()` — single grand total. **Key Concepts:** - **Star Schema:** One fact table connected directly to denormalized dimension tables. - **Snowflake Schema:** Dimension tables are normalized into multiple related tables. - **Fact Constellation:** Multiple fact tables sharing dimension tables. The cube pre-computes common aggregations, enabling **sub-second response** to analytical queries. #### Model Solution to (b): **(i) Roll-Up:** Aggregation by climbing up a hierarchy. - Example: `Daily sales per city` → `Monthly sales per state` → `Yearly sales per country`. - Aggregate function: typically `SUM`. **(ii) Drill-Down:** Reverse of roll-up; navigate from coarser to finer granularity. - Example: `Yearly total` → `Quarterly` → `Monthly` → `Daily` breakdown. - Reveals detailed patterns behind aggregate trends. **(iii) Slice:** Fix one dimension's value to extract a 2D plane. - Example: Selecting `Quarter = Q1` from `(Time × Location × Product)` yields a 2D table of `Location × Product` for Q1 only. **(iv) Dice:** Select on multiple dimensions to create a focused sub-cube. - Example: `Product = Electronics` AND `Location = South India` AND `Time = 2024` yields a 3D sub-cube focused on electronics sales in South India in 2024. **(v) Pivot (Rotate):** Reorients cube axes for an alternative presentation; data is unchanged. - Example: Viewing `Location` as rows and `Time` as columns, then swapping to view `Time` as rows and `Location` as columns. - Pure visualization transformation. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Confusing Slice and Dice:** Students often write "Slice selects multiple dimensions" — this is wrong. Slice = ONE dimension, Dice = TWO OR MORE dimensions. Examiners deduct 1-2 marks for this. > 2. **Writing Pivot changes data:** Pivot/Rotate does NOT change any data values, only the visual orientation. Mark lost if you say "Pivot aggregates data". > 3. **Forgetting hierarchy:** Roll-Up and Drill-Down require concept hierarchies (e.g., Day → Month → Year). Don't write "roll-up removes rows from a table". > 4. **OLAP vs OLTP confusion:** Don't mix up operational and analytical definitions. KTU explicitly tests this distinction. > 5. **Missing measure:** When defining a data cube, ALWAYS mention *both* dimensions AND measures. A cube without a measure is geometric, not analytical. > 6. **Aggregation functions:** Don't just write "sum" — specify *which* function (`SUM`, `AVG`, `MAX`, `MIN`, `COUNT`) is used and whether the measure is **additive**, **semi-additive**, or **non-additive**. > 7. **Formula notation:** When writing $\sigma$ (selection), ensure the notation is correct: $\sigma_{D_i = v}(C)$ for slice and $\sigma_{D_i \in S}(C)$ for dice. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — OLAP Operations** **Core Concepts:** - ☐ OLAP = **Online Analytical Processing** — interactive, multidimensional analysis on a data warehouse. - ☐ OLAP operates on a **data cube** (multidimensional array of facts and dimensions). - ☐ **OLTP** handles transactions; **OLAP** handles analysis. They are fundamentally different. **The Five OLAP Operations:** - ☐ **Roll-Up** ↑ — Aggregates; climbs up hierarchy; reduces detail (e.g., Day → Year). - ☐ **Drill-Down** ↓ — Disaggregates; descends hierarchy; adds detail (e.g., Year → Day). - ☐ **Slice** — Selection on **ONE** dimension; produces 2D plane. - ☐ **Dice** — Selection on **TWO OR MORE** dimensions; produces focused sub-cube. - ☐ **Pivot / Rotate** — Reorients cube axes; data unchanged, view changes. **Cube Structure:** - ☐ **Base cuboid** = most detailed, atomic level. - ☐ **Apex cuboid** = single aggregated value (0-D). - ☐ For $n$ dimensions, there are $2^n$ possible cuboids. - ☐ For $n$ dimensions with $L_i$ levels each: $\prod_{i=1}^{n}(L_i + 1)$ total cuboids. **Hierarchy Properties:** - ☐ **Concept hierarchy** = partial order of granularity levels within a dimension. - ☐ **Drill-path consistency** = roll-up followed by drill-down must return to original detail. - ☐ **Additive measures** (sales, quantity) support simple roll-up. - ☐ **Non-additive measures** (averages, ratios) require special handling. **Architecture:** - ☐ **MOLAP** = Multidimensional storage; fastest, but storage-heavy. - ☐ **ROLAP** = Relational storage using SQL; most scalable. - ☐ **HOLAP** = Hybrid: recent data in MOLAP, historical in ROLAP. **FASMI Test:** A true OLAP system must be **F**ast, **A**nalytical, **S**hared, **M**ultidimensional, **I**nformational. **Exam Strategy Tips:** - ☐ Always draw a **labeled diagram** of the cube with operations shown. - ☐ Use **mathematical notation** ($\sigma$, $\sum$) to score full marks. - ☐ Give **at least one example** for every operation. - ☐ Distinguish **slice** (1-D selection) from **dice** (multi-D selection) clearly. - ☐ Mention **concept hierarchies** when explaining roll-up/drill-down. - ☐ End with the **real-world utility** statement to score the "application" mark.

topics.title: Data Warehouse Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST525 - DATA MINING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Warehouse Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Data Warehouse Architecture

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A **Data Warehouse (DW)** is a *subject-oriented, integrated, time-variant, and non-volatile* collection of data in support of management's decision-making process. The **Data Warehouse Architecture** refers to the structured arrangement of components — including source systems, staging area, ETL processes, the central warehouse repository, data marts, OLAP servers, and front-end analytical tools — that work together to enable efficient business intelligence, reporting, and data mining operations.

The architecture defines how data flows from heterogeneous operational sources, gets transformed and consolidated, and finally reaches the end-user query and analysis layer.

### The Four Canonical Properties of a Data Warehouse (W. H. Inmon Framework)

1. **Subject-Oriented** — Organized around major business subjects (e.g., sales, customers, products) rather than operational applications.
2. **Integrated** — Provides a unified, consistent view by reconciling naming conventions, measurement units, and attribute definitions across diverse sources.
3. **Time-Variant** — Preserves historical data (typically 5–10 years) to support trend analysis; every record carries a time-stamp.
4. **Non-Volatile** — Data is loaded but not deleted or modified once entered; stable snapshots are maintained for analytical querying.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** The architecture of a data warehouse is essentially a **multi-tiered blueprint** describing the physical and logical movement of data from operational systems to analytical decision-support interfaces.

---

## 2. Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **Data Warehouse as a Centralized Library of Business History**.

- **Operational Databases** = Daily registers in individual department offices (Sales, HR, Finance). Each is fast, transactional, and isolated.
- **Staging Area + ETL** = A large backroom where librarians receive files from every department, clean them, translate them into a common language, and stamp them with a date.
- **Data Warehouse (Core)** = The main library hall, organized by subject (subject-oriented) and never thrown away (non-volatile).
- **Data Marts** = Specialized reading rooms for departments (e.g., Marketing Data Mart) that draw from the main library.
- **OLAP Servers & Front-End Tools** = The reading desks with magnifying glasses, charts, and pivot tables that help executives and analysts "read" the data.

> [!TIP]
> Just as a library preserves books historically for reference (not for daily transactions), a data warehouse preserves historical data for analytical querying — not for real-time updates.

---

## 3. Goals of a Data Warehouse Architecture

- Consolidate heterogeneous data from multiple operational sources.
- Enable fast, complex analytical queries (OLAP, reporting, data mining).
- Maintain historical consistency for trend and pattern discovery.
- Decouple analytical workloads from transactional workloads.
- Support multidimensional analysis (slice, dice, drill-down, roll-up).
- Provide high data quality, governance, and metadata management.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 1. Three-Tier Data Warehouse Architecture (Standard KTU Model)

The widely accepted reference architecture (Kimball / Inmon hybrid) consists of **three logical tiers**:

### Tier 1 — Bottom Tier: Data Sources & Staging Area
- **Operational/Source Systems:** RDBMS, legacy files, ERP, CRM, web logs, flat files, external feeds.
- **Staging Area:** A temporary storage zone where raw data is dumped before transformation. Acts as a *buffer* between source and warehouse to:
  - Decouple extraction from transformation.
  - Allow restart of failed loads.
  - Provide audit checkpoints.
- **ETL/ELT Tools:** Extraction, Transformation, Loading processes.

### Tier 2 — Middle Tier: Data Warehouse Server (OLAP Engine)
- **Central Data Warehouse Database:** Relational (ROLAP), Multidimensional (MOLAP), or Hybrid (HOLAP) storage.
- **OLAP Servers:** Provide multidimensional views, aggregations, and fast query performance.
- **Data Marts:** Department-specific subsets (e.g., Finance, Marketing).
- **Metadata Repository:** Central catalog of schemas, transformations, lineage, and business rules.

### Tier 3 — Top Tier: Front-End Tools (Clients)
- Query and reporting tools.
- Data mining engines (the focus of this course).
- OLAP analytical tools (pivot tables, dashboards).
- EIS (Executive Information Systems).

> [!NOTE]
> The **Three-Tier Architecture** is the *most exam-relevant* model in KTU papers. Always draw the diagram with clear arrows showing data flow from sources → staging → DW → clients.

---

## 2. Alternative Architectural Models (KTU Comparison Table)

| Architecture Type | Description | Advantages | Disadvantages | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Single-Tier** | Minimal; direct access from source to analysis | Simple, low cost | No real consolidation, poor performance | Academic / small-scale demos |
| **Two-Tier** | Sources + Warehouse on one side; clients on the other | Better isolation of analytical load | Limited scalability, weak metadata | Small organizations |
| **Three-Tier** | Sources/Staging → Warehouse/OLAP → Clients | Scalable, robust, metadata-rich, industry standard | Higher cost, complex ETL | Enterprise DW (most common) |
| **Enterprise Warehouse** | Centralized, organization-wide DW | Single source of truth, cross-functional | High cost, long implementation | Large enterprises |
| **Data Mart** | Decentralized, department-specific | Fast deployment, low cost | Risk of "data silos" and redundancy | Departmental analytics |
| **Virtual Warehouse** | No physical DW; views over operational DBs | No ETL cost, real-time | Poor query performance, impact on OLTP | Rapid prototyping |

---

## 3. KTU Formula Sheet & Key Quantitative Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are standard **Data Warehouse metrics** frequently tested in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. Memorize the formulas and their units.

$$
\text{ETL Throughput} \;(\text{rows/sec}) = \frac{\text{Total Records Loaded}}{\text{Total Load Time (seconds)}}
$$

$$
\text{Data Warehouse Size} \;(\text{TB}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Fact Table Size}_i + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \text{Dimension Table Size}_j
$$

$$
\text{Query Response Time} = T_{\text{OLAP Engine}} + T_{\text{Network}} + T_{\text{Client Render}}
$$

$$
\text{Compression Ratio} = \frac{\text{Original Data Size}}{\text{Compressed Data Size}}
$$

$$
\text{Refresh Latency} = T_{\text{Extract}} + T_{\text{Transform}} + T_{\text{Load}}
$$

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical KTU Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| ETL Throughput | $\eta_{ETL}$ | rows / second | $10^{3}$ – $10^{6}$ |
| Warehouse Capacity | $C_{DW}$ | TB / PB | 1 TB – 100 PB |
| Query Latency | $L_{q}$ | seconds | 1 s – 60 s |
| Compression Ratio | $R_{c}$ | dimensionless | 2 : 1 – 10 : 1 |
| Refresh Latency | $L_{r}$ | minutes / hours | 15 min – 24 h |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** These metrics drive SLA design, hardware sizing, and cost estimation in cloud data warehouses like **Snowflake, Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery, and Azure Synapse**.

---

## 4. Core Components of a Data Warehouse (Detailed)

- **Load Manager:** Performs ETL, data cleansing, and bulk loading. Includes scheduling, error handling, and logging.
- **Warehouse Manager:** Manages storage, indexing, partitioning, materialized views, and aggregations.
- **Query Manager:** Routes, optimizes, and executes end-user analytical queries.
- **Metadata Manager:** Stores technical metadata (schemas, ETL jobs) and business metadata (definitions, ownership, lineage).
- **Process Manager:** Schedules, monitors, and orchestrates the entire DW workflow.
- **Data Marts:** Subject-specific, smaller, faster subsets used by individual departments.
- **OLAP Servers:** Provide multidimensional, fast-access analytical views.

---

## 5. Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking:** Risk analytics, fraud detection, customer 360° views.
- **Retail:** Sales trend analysis, inventory optimization, recommendation systems.
- **Healthcare:** Patient outcome tracking, epidemiological research.
- **Telecommunications:** Churn prediction, network optimization.
- **E-Commerce / Web:** Clickstream mining, personalized marketing.
- **Manufacturing:** Predictive maintenance, supply-chain analytics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> While Data Warehouse Architecture is primarily a design topic, KTU 2024 frequently tests **quantitative estimation problems** involving sizing, latency, and throughput. Below are two fully worked, board-style numerical problems.

---

## Worked Example 1: Data Warehouse Size Estimation

**Problem:** A retail enterprise DW contains **3 fact tables** and **12 dimension tables**. The fact tables have average row sizes and row counts as follows:

- FactSales: $R_s = 200$ bytes, $N_s = 5 \times 10^{8}$ rows
- FactInventory: $R_i = 150$ bytes, $N_i = 2 \times 10^{7}$ rows
- FactReturns: $R_r = 180$ bytes, $N_r = 1 \times 10^{7}$ rows

Each dimension table has on average $D = 50,000$ rows and row size $R_d = 300$ bytes. Compute the total raw warehouse size in **TB** (using $1 \text{ TB} = 2^{40}$ bytes).

### Step 1 — Compute the size of each fact table

$$
S_{FactSales} = R_s \times N_s = 200 \times (5 \times 10^{8}) = 1.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
S_{FactInventory} = R_i \times N_i = 150 \times (2 \times 10^{7}) = 3.0 \times 10^{9} \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
S_{FactReturns} = R_r \times N_r = 180 \times (1 \times 10^{7}) = 1.8 \times 10^{9} \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step 2 — Sum the fact-table sizes

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{Fact} &= S_{FactSales} + S_{FactInventory} + S_{FactReturns} \\
&= (1.00 \times 10^{11}) + (3.0 \times 10^{9}) + (1.8 \times 10^{9}) \\
&= 1.00 \times 10^{11} + 0.30 \times 10^{10} + 0.18 \times 10^{10} \\
&= 1.048 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute total dimension-table size

$$
S_{Dim} = 12 \times (D \times R_d) = 12 \times (50{,}000 \times 300)
$$

$$
S_{Dim} = 12 \times 1.5 \times 10^{7} = 1.8 \times 10^{8} \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step 4 — Total raw warehouse size

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{Total} &= S_{Fact} + S_{Dim} \\
&= (1.048 \times 10^{11}) + (1.8 \times 10^{8}) \\
&\approx 1.0498 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to TB

$$
S_{Total} = \frac{1.0498 \times 10^{11}}{2^{40}} = \frac{1.0498 \times 10^{11}}{1.0995 \times 10^{12}} \approx 0.0954 \text{ TB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $\boxed{S_{Total} \approx 0.0954 \text{ TB} \;\;(\approx 95.4 \text{ GB})}$. This is a standard raw uncompressed estimate; in practice, compression (ratio 3:1) would reduce it to $\approx 32$ GB.

---

## Worked Example 2: ETL Refresh Latency Calculation

**Problem:** An ETL pipeline loads daily transactions into a DW. Given:
- Extraction phase: $T_E = 45$ minutes
- Transformation phase: $T_T = 90$ minutes
- Loading phase: $T_L = 60$ minutes
- **5 source systems** in parallel, with maximum phase times only (bottleneck rule)

Compute the **total refresh latency** in hours and the **effective throughput** if $1.2 \times 10^{9}$ rows are loaded.

### Step 1 — Apply the bottleneck rule for parallel sources

Since sources operate in parallel, the effective per-phase time is the **maximum**, not the sum. Assume the given $T_E, T_T, T_L$ already represent the worst-case parallel phase durations.

### Step 2 — Total refresh latency

$$
L_r = T_E + T_T + T_L = 45 + 90 + 60 = 195 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
L_r = \frac{195}{60} = 3.25 \text{ hours}
$$

### Step 3 — Effective throughput

$$
\eta_{ETL} = \frac{N}{L_r} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{9}}{195 \times 60} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{9}}{11{,}700}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{ETL} &\approx 1.0256 \times 10^{5} \text{ rows/second} \\
&\approx 1.03 \times 10^{5} \text{ rows/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $\boxed{L_r = 3.25 \text{ hours}, \quad \eta_{ETL} \approx 1.03 \times 10^{5} \text{ rows/sec}}$. This throughput comfortably supports near-real-time nightly DW refresh.

---

## Analytical Matrix: Architecture Selection Decision Table

| Requirement | Recommended Architecture | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Single department, < 100 GB | **Data Mart** | Low cost, fast deployment, fits subject scope |
| Cross-functional, > 10 TB | **Three-Tier Enterprise DW** | Centralized, scalable, metadata-rich |
| Real-time analytics on OLTP | **Virtual Warehouse** | No ETL delay; queries against live sources |
| Mixed historical + real-time | **Hybrid (HOLAP)** | Combines ROLAP flexibility with MOLAP speed |
| Regulatory compliance + audit | **Three-Tier + Metadata Layer** | Strong governance, lineage, and traceability |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams are strictly compliant with KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards: pure alphanumeric node IDs, raw uppercase labels, and no reserved-keyword misuse.

---
