## 2.3 The Information Gain Algorithm (Step-by-Step Logic)

The **ID3 decision tree algorithm** uses Information Gain as its splitting criterion. Here is the operational logic:

1. Compute the **Entropy** $H(S)$ of the entire dataset $S$ with respect to the class label.
2. For each candidate attribute $A$, compute the **Conditional Entropy** $H(S \vert A)$ — the weighted average entropy after partitioning $S$ by values of $A$.
3. Compute the **Information Gain** as $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A)$.
4. Choose the attribute $A^*$ that **maximizes** $IG(S, A)$.
5. Make $A^*$ the splitting attribute of the current node.
6. Recurse on each partitioned subset.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula Name** | **Equation** | **Variables & Units** | **Used In** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shannon Entropy | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $p_i$ = probability of class $i$; $c$ = number of classes; unit = **bits** | ID3, C4.5 |
| Conditional Entropy | $H(S \vert A) = \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | $S_v$ = subset where $A=v$ | ID3 |
| Information Gain | $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A)$ | $IG \ge 0$; unit = **bits** | ID3 |
| Split Information | $SplitInfo(S, A) = -\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | Penalizes multi-valued attributes | C4.5 |
| Gain Ratio | $GR(S, A) = \frac{IG(S, A)}{SplitInfo(S, A)}$ | $GR \in [0, 1]$; dimensionless | C4.5 |
| Gini Index | $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2$ | $Gini \in [0, 1 - 1/c]$; dimensionless | CART |
| Gini Gain | $\Delta Gini(A) = Gini(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} Gini(S_v)$ | Maximized for split | CART |
| Chi-Square | $\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ | $O_i$ = observed; $E_i$ = expected frequency | Statistical Filter |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common KTU Mistake**: Students often compute $H(S)$ using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\log_2$. The unit of entropy in Information Theory is **bits**, which requires base 2. Using $\log_e$ gives units of **nats**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Field** | **Application** |
|---|---|
| Bioinformatics | Selecting relevant genes from thousands of DNA microarrays |
| Image Processing | Picking the most discriminative pixel/HOG features |
| Cybersecurity | Identifying key network traffic attributes for intrusion detection |
| IoT Analytics | Reducing sensor feature dimensionality in edge devices |
| NLP | Selecting meaningful n-grams or TF-IDF terms |
| Healthcare AI | Selecting critical patient indicators for diagnosis |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Exam), the 14-mark question on this topic **frequently appears in Part B Module 2**, with a typical structure: "Compute Information Gain / Gini Index for the given dataset and identify the best splitting attribute."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Information Gain Calculation

Let us consider the canonical KTU training dataset $D$ that contains **14 records** of weather conditions and a class label `Play` (Yes/No). This is the famous *Play-Tennis* dataset pattern.

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | Play |
|-----|---------|-------------|----------|------|------|
| 1 | Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | No |
| 2 | Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | No |
| 3 | Overcast | Hot | High | Weak | Yes |
| 4 | Rain | Mild | High | Weak | Yes |
| 5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | No |
| 7 | Overcast | Cool | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 8 | Sunny | Mild | High | Weak | No |
| 9 | Sunny | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 10 | Rain | Mild | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 11 | Sunny | Mild | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 12 | Overcast | Mild | High | Strong | Yes |
| 13 | Overcast | Hot | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 14 | Rain | Mild | High | Strong | No |

**Class Distribution:** Yes = 9, No = 5, Total = 14.

### Step 1: Compute Overall Entropy $H(S)$

We first identify the probability of each class:
$$
p_{\text{Yes}} = \frac{9}{14}, \quad p_{\text{No}} = \frac{5}{14}
$$

Now we substitute into Shannon's Entropy formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i \\
&= -\left[ p_{\text{Yes}} \log_2 p_{\text{Yes}} + p_{\text{No}} \log_2 p_{\text{No}} \right] \\
&= -\left[ \frac{9}{14} \log_2 \left(\frac{9}{14}\right) + \frac{5}{14} \log_2 \left(\frac{5}{14}\right) \right] \\
&= -\left[ \frac{9}{14} \cdot (-0.6374) + \frac{5}{14} \cdot (-1.4854) \right] \\
&= -\left[ -0.4097 + -0.5305 \right] \\
&= 0.9402 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

> So, the **initial entropy of the dataset is $H(S) = 0.940$ bits**.

### Step 2: Compute Conditional Entropy $H(S \vert \text{Outlook})$

The attribute *Outlook* takes three values: *Sunny*, *Overcast*, *Rain*.

**Sunny Subset (5 records):** Yes = 2, No = 3

$$
H(\text{Sunny}) = -\left[ \frac{2}{5} \log_2 \frac{2}{5} + \frac{3}{5} \log_2 \frac{3}{5} \right] = -\left[ 0.4 \cdot (-1.3219) + 0.6 \cdot (-0.7370) \right] = 0.9710 \text{ bits}
$$

**Overcast Subset (4 records):** Yes = 4, No = 0

$$
H(\text{Overcast}) = -\left[ \frac{4}{4} \log_2 \frac{4}{4} + \frac{0}{4} \log_2 \frac{0}{4} \right] = -\left[ 1 \cdot 0 + 0 \right] = 0 \text{ bits}
$$

**Rain Subset (5 records):** Yes = 3, No = 2

$$
H(\text{Rain}) = -\left[ \frac{3}{5} \log_2 \frac{3}{5} + \frac{2}{5} \log_2 \frac{2}{5} \right] = -\left[ 0.6 \cdot (-0.7370) + 0.4 \cdot (-1.3219) \right] = 0.9710 \text{ bits}
$$

**Weighted Conditional Entropy** (weighted by subset size):

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \vert \text{Outlook}) &= \frac{5}{14} \cdot H(\text{Sunny}) + \frac{4}{14} \cdot H(\text{Overcast}) + \frac{5}{14} \cdot H(\text{Rain}) \\
&= \frac{5}{14} \cdot 0.9710 + \frac{4}{14} \cdot 0 + \frac{5}{14} \cdot 0.9710 \\
&= 0.3468 + 0 + 0.3468 \\
&= 0.6936 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Information Gain for Outlook

$$
\begin{aligned}
IG(S, \text{Outlook}) &= H(S) - H(S \vert \text{Outlook}) \\
&= 0.9402 - 0.6936 \\
&= 0.2466 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Repeat for Other Attributes (Humidity, Wind, Temperature)

After performing similar calculations:

| **Attribute** | $H(S \vert A)$ | $IG(S, A)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Outlook | 0.6936 | **0.2466** |
| Humidity | 0.7885 | 0.1517 |
| Wind | 0.8922 | 0.0480 |
| Temperature | 0.9088 | 0.0314 |

### Step 5: Select the Best Splitting Attribute

Since **Outlook** maximizes the Information Gain:

$$
A^* = \arg\max_{A} IG(S, A) = \text{Outlook}
$$

**Decision:** The root node of the ID3 decision tree is split on the **Outlook** attribute. This is the heart of Attribute Subset Selection via Information Gain.

## 3.2 Gini Index Worked Mini-Example

Consider a binary classification dataset $S$ with 10 records: 6 "Yes" and 4 "No".

$$
Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2 = 1 - \left[ \left(\frac{6}{10}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{4}{10}\right)^2 \right] = 1 - [0.36 + 0.16] = 0.48
$$

Now suppose an attribute $A$ partitions $S$ into $S_1$ (4 Yes, 1 No) and $S_2$ (2 Yes, 3 No).

$$
Gini(S_1) = 1 - \left[\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^2\right] = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20
$$

$$
Gini(S_2) = 1 - \left[\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{3}{5}\right)^2\right] = 1 - 0.52 = 0.48
$$

$$
Gini_{\text{split}}(A) = \frac{5}{10} \cdot 0.20 + \frac{5}{10} \cdot 0.48 = 0.10 + 0.24 = 0.34
$$

**Gini Gain (reduction in impurity):**

$$
\Delta Gini(A) = Gini(S) - Gini_{\text{split}}(A) = 0.48 - 0.34 = 0.14
$$

Higher $\Delta Gini(A)$ indicates a better splitting attribute.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Attribute Subset Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawData[Raw Dataset with N Attributes] --> preprocess[Step 1: Data Cleaning and Encoding]
    preprocess --> candidateGen[Step 2: Candidate Subset Generator]
    candidateGen --> eval[Step 3: Evaluation Function]
    eval --> metric{Step 4: Quality Metric}
    metric --> infoGain[Information Gain]
    metric --> giniIdx[Gini Index]
    metric --> chiSq[Chi-Square Test]
    metric --> gainRatio[Gain Ratio]
    infoGain --> rank[Step 5: Rank Attributes]
    giniIdx --> rank
    chiSq --> rank
    gainRatio --> rank
    rank --> threshold{rank exceeds threshold K}
    threshold --> yesPath[Yes: Retain Attribute]
    threshold --> noPath[No: Discard Attribute]
    yesPath --> reducedSet[Reduced Attribute Subset of size K]
    noPath --> reducedSet
    reducedSet --> output[Mining Algorithm with K Dimensions]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: The pipeline begins with a raw $N$-dimensional dataset and ends with a *reduced $K$-dimensional* dataset ($K \ll N$). The central decision point is the threshold gate — only the **top-$K$ ranked attributes** survive the filter.

## 4.2 Comparative Schematic — Filter vs Wrapper vs Embedded

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FILTER[Filter Approach]
        F1[Statistical Test on Data] --> F2[Score Each Attribute]
        F2 --> F3[Select Top K]
    end
    subgraph WRAPPER[Wrapper Approach]
        W1[Generate Subset] --> W2[Train Model]
        W2 --> W3[Evaluate Accuracy]
        W3 --> W4{Stop Criterion}
        W4 --> W1
    end
    subgraph EMBEDDED[Embedded Approach]
        E1[Algorithm Training] --> E2[Internal Feature Ranking]
        E2 --> E3[Final Model with Subset]
    end
    F3 --> finalAttr[Final Attribute Subset]
    W4 --> finalAttr
    E3 --> finalAttr
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree Splitting Flow (ID3 Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Root: Entire Dataset S] --> computeH[Compute H of S]
    computeH --> loopAttr[For Each Attribute A in S]
    loopAttr --> computeIG[Compute IG of S given A]
    computeIG --> compareIG{IG max greater than current max}
    compareIG --> yesUpdate[Update best attribute]
    compareGIS --> noNext[Continue loop]
    yesUpdate --> noNext
    noNext --> moreAttr{More attributes to test}
    moreAttr --> yesAttr[Yes: continue iteration]
    moreAttr --> noAttr[No: pick overall best]
    yesAttr --> loopAttr
    noAttr --> split[Split node on best attribute]
    split --> child1[Child Subset 1]
    split --> child2[Child Subset 2]
    split --> child3[Child Subset 3]
```

## 4.4 Forward Selection vs Backward Elimination Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FWD[Forward Selection]
        F0[Empty Set] --> F1[Add best single attribute]
        F1 --> F2[Add next best attribute]
        F2 --> F3[Repeat until no improvement]
    end
    subgraph BWD[Backward Elimination]
        B0[Full Set of all attributes] --> B1[Remove worst attribute]
        B1 --> B2[Remove next worst attribute]
        B2 --> B3[Repeat until accuracy drops]
    end
    F3 --> finalRed[Reduced Subset]
    B3 --> finalRed
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Shortcut**: When asked to "name the attribute selection strategy used in ID3" in a 3-mark question, answer: *"Information Gain-based greedy top-down induction (Embedded approach)."*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Define Information Gain. Why is it preferred over simple accuracy for attribute selection?**

> **Model Answer:**
> Information Gain (IG) is the expected reduction in entropy (impurity) of a dataset $S$ achieved by partitioning it on attribute $A$. Mathematically,
> $$ IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A) $$
> where $H(S) = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i$ is the Shannon entropy. It is preferred over simple accuracy because:
> 1. It captures the **reduction in disorder** at the class level, not just the count of correct predictions.
> 2. It naturally handles **multi-class** problems without bias toward the majority class.
> 3. It quantifies the **information-theoretic value** of an attribute in bits, enabling principled comparison across attributes.
> 4. It is **invariant to attribute scaling** and is sensitive to **information asymmetry**.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**Distinguish between Filter and Wrapper approaches of attribute subset selection. State one advantage of each.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | **Aspect** | **Filter Approach** | **Wrapper Approach** |
> |---|---|---|
> | Evaluation | Uses intrinsic data statistics (e.g., IG, $\chi^2$) | Uses a target mining model as black-box evaluator |
> | Speed | Very fast (no model retraining) | Slow (model is retrained for every candidate subset) |
> | Feature Interactions | Ignored | Captured |
> | Overfitting Risk | Low | High (subset tailored to one model) |
> | **Advantage** | Scalable to high-dimensional data | Higher prediction accuracy |
>
> **Filter Advantage Example:** Computational efficiency — runs in $O(N \cdot d)$ time.
> **Wrapper Advantage Example:** Captures non-linear feature interactions that filters miss.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Information Gain Computation

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Pattern]**
**(a)** Define the entropy of a dataset. Compute the entropy of a dataset $D$ with 12 positive and 8 negative examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For the following training data, calculate the Information Gain for the attribute *Weather*. Identify the best splitting attribute among {Weather, Humidity, Wind}. *(7 marks)*

| Day | Weather | Humidity | Wind | Play |
|-----|---------|----------|------|------|
| 1 | Sunny | High | Weak | No |
| 2 | Sunny | High | Strong | No |
| 3 | Cloudy | High | Weak | Yes |
| 4 | Rainy | High | Weak | Yes |
| 5 | Rainy | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 6 | Rainy | Normal | Strong | No |
| 7 | Cloudy | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 8 | Sunny | High | Weak | No |
| 9 | Sunny | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 10 | Rainy | Normal | Weak | Yes |

#### Model Solution

**(a) Definition and Entropy Computation**

**Definition:** Entropy is a measure of impurity or randomness in a dataset. For a dataset $D$ with $c$ classes having probabilities $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_c$:

$$
H(D) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i
$$

**Computation:** Total = 20, Yes = 10, No = 10.

$$
p_{\text{Yes}} = \frac{10}{20} = 0.5, \quad p_{\text{No}} = \frac{10}{20} = 0.5
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(D) &= -\left[ 0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.5 \log_2 0.5 \right] \\
&= -\left[ 0.5 \cdot (-1) + 0.5 \cdot (-1) \right] \\
&= 1.000 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Information Gain for Weather**

**Step 1 — Partition by Weather:**

| Weather | Yes | No | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sunny | 1 | 3 | 4 |
| Cloudy | 2 | 0 | 2 |
| Rainy | 3 | 1 | 4 |

**Step 2 — Compute Subset Entropies:**

$$
H(\text{Sunny}) = -\left[\frac{1}{4}\log_2\frac{1}{4} + \frac{3}{4}\log_2\frac{3}{4}\right] = -\left[0.25(-2) + 0.75(-0.4150)\right] = 0.8113
$$

$$
H(\text{Cloudy}) = -\left[\frac{2}{2}\log_2 1 + 0\right] = 0
$$

$$
H(\text{Rainy}) = -\left[\frac{3}{4}\log_2\frac{3}{4} + \frac{1}{4}\log_2\frac{1}{4}\right] = 0.8113
$$

**Step 3 — Weighted Conditional Entropy:**

$$
H(D \vert \text{Weather}) = \frac{4}{10}(0.8113) + \frac{2}{10}(0) + \frac{4}{10}(0.8113) = 0.3245 + 0 + 0.3245 = 0.6490
$$

**Step 4 — Information Gain:**

$$
IG(D, \text{Weather}) = 1.000 - 0.6490 = 0.3510 \text{ bits}
$$

Assuming analogous computations yield $IG(D, \text{Humidity}) = 0.2810$ and $IG(D, \text{Wind}) = 0.1240$, the **best splitting attribute is Weather** since it has the highest information gain.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Gini Index and Chi-Square

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Pattern]**
**(a)** Compute the Gini Index for the entire dataset given in the table below. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Using the same dataset, calculate the Chi-Square statistic for the attribute *Outlook* and comment on its significance at a 5% confidence level. *(7 marks)*

> *Tables and full numeric solutions for Chi-Square will follow the same exhaustive derivation pattern as Information Gain, with degrees of freedom $df = (r-1)(c-1) = (3-1)(2-1) = 2$ and critical value $\chi^2_{0.05, 2} = 5.991$.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Base of Logarithm**: Always use $\log_2$ unless the question explicitly states otherwise. Marks are deducted for using $\log_{10}$ or $\ln$ without conversion.
> 2. **Zero Frequencies**: When a subset has 0 instances of a class, the term $0 \cdot \log_2 0$ is defined as **0** (not undefined). Use L'Hôpital's limit convention: $\lim_{x \to 0^+} x \log_2 x = 0$.
> 3. **Arithmetic Precision**: KTU typically accepts answers correct to **3 decimal places**. Do not over-round intermediate steps.
> 4. **Forgetting Split Information**: If the question asks for **Gain Ratio** (C4.5), you MUST divide IG by SplitInfo. Forgetting the denominator is a classic 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Confusing Gini Gain with Gini Index**: $Gini(S)$ measures impurity of a node; $\Delta Gini(A)$ measures the *reduction* in impurity after splitting on $A$. Examiners differentiate these carefully.
> 6. **Subset Search vs Subset Selection**: Subset **Search** is the algorithmic process of exploring subsets; Subset **Selection** is the final choice. Both terms may appear in 2-mark questions.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Attribute Subset Selection** = dimensionality reduction by picking the *best K* of *N* original attributes.
- **Three approaches**: Filter (fast, model-free), Wrapper (slow, model-coupled, accurate), Embedded (algorithm-internal, e.g., ID3).
- **Information Gain** = $H(S) - H(S \vert A)$; pick the attribute that **maximizes** IG.
- **Entropy** uses $\log_2$ → unit is **bits**; entropy of a pure node is **0**; entropy of a 50-50 binary node is **1 bit**.
- **Gini Index** ranges from **0** (pure) to **$1 - 1/c$** (most impure for $c$ classes).
- **Gain Ratio** = IG / SplitInfo; normalizes IG to handle multi-valued attributes (C4.5 fix over ID3).
- **Chi-Square** tests independence between attribute and class; reject null hypothesis if $\chi^2_{\text{computed}} > \chi^2_{\text{critical}}$.
- **Forward Selection** starts empty and adds; **Backward Elimination** starts full and removes; both are **greedy hill-climbing** strategies.
- **Strongly relevant vs weakly relevant vs irrelevant** attributes — the foundational taxonomy of Liu & Motoda (1998).
- **Best Splitting Attribute** in ID3 = attribute with **highest IG**; in CART = attribute with **highest Gini Gain**; in C4.5 = attribute with **highest Gain Ratio**.
- **Practical Tip**: In KTU answer sheets, always draw a small table showing the IG of every candidate attribute to make your selection logic visually obvious to the examiner.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner's Note:** A 14-mark Attribute Subset Selection question in KTU typically awards **2 marks for the formula statement**, **3 marks for entropy computation**, **3 marks for conditional entropy computation**, **3 marks for IG/Gini calculation**, and **3 marks for the final attribute selection and conclusion**. Structure your answer accordingly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensionality Reduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST525 - DATA MINING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dimensionality Reduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dimensionality Reduction** is the process of transforming high-dimensional data into a meaningful representation of reduced dimensionality such that the intrinsic structure and the salient properties of the original data are preserved as much as possible.

In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus context, dimensionality reduction is classified into two principal paradigms:

- **Feature Selection (Variable Selection):** Selecting a subset of the original $d$ dimensions (features) without transforming them. The original feature semantics are retained.
- **Feature Extraction (Feature Projection):** Constructing new low-dimensional features as linear or non-linear combinations of the original features. The original axes are projected onto a new subspace.

Mathematically, given a data matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ with $n$ observations and $d$ original features, dimensionality reduction seeks a mapping:

$$
f: \mathbb{R}^{d} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{k}, \quad \text{where } k \ll d
$$

such that the projected data $Y = f(X) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ retains maximum information content from $X$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Shadow Analogy — Plain English Explanation**

Imagine a 3D object, like a **rubber duck**, held under a desk lamp. The lamp casts a 2D shadow on the floor. That shadow is a **dimensionality reduction** of the duck: from 3 dimensions down to 2.

A good shadow preserves the *silhouette* — you can still tell it is a duck. A bad shadow (say, from the side instead of the top) loses important information. **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** is exactly the mathematical equivalent of rotating the lamp until the shadow captures the most *spread*, the most *variance*, of the duck's shape.

In data terms:
- **High dimensions** = hundreds of features per customer (age, income, clicks, time-on-page, search history, device type, location …)
- **Low dimensions** = a 2D scatter plot where customers with similar behavior cluster together visually.

### 1.3 The Curse of Dimensionality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As the number of dimensions $d$ grows, data becomes exponentially sparse. This phenomenon, termed the **Curse of Dimensionality** by Bellman (1961), leads to overfitting, computational blow-up, and distance metrics becoming uninformative.

Key consequences include:

- **Distance concentration:** In high dimensions, the ratio of the farthest to nearest neighbor distance approaches **1**, rendering $k$-NN, clustering, and similarity search unreliable.
- **Sample sparsity:** The volume of a unit hypercube grows as $1^d = 1$, but the data needed to sample it densely grows exponentially.
- **Redundancy:** Real-world features are highly correlated (e.g., height in cm and height in inches), wasting representation capacity.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Highlighted

> [!NOTE]
> The two principal **variability preservation metrics** used throughout this module are:
> - **Variance** — measures spread along a single axis. Unit = squared units of original feature.
> - **Covariance** — measures joint variability between two features. Unit = product of feature units.
> - **Eigenvalue** $\lambda_i$ — magnitude of variance captured along principal component $i$.
> - **Explained Variance Ratio (EVR)** — fraction of total dataset variance retained: $EVR_i = \lambda_i / \sum_{j=1}^{d}\lambda_j$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Dimensionality Reduction Techniques

- **Linear Methods**
  - Principal Component Analysis (**PCA**)
  - Linear Discriminant Analysis (**LDA**)
  - Singular Value Decomposition (**SVD**)
  - Independent Component Analysis (**ICA**)

- **Non-Linear Methods**
  - Kernel PCA (KPCA)
  - t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (**t-SNE**)
  - Isomap
  - Locally Linear Embedding (**LLE**)
  - Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (**UMAP**)

### 2.2 Principal Component Analysis (PCA) — Conceptual Logic

PCA finds an **orthogonal linear transformation** that reorients the data along axes of maximum variance. It performs a **change of basis** from the original feature space to a new orthonormal basis formed by the **eigenvectors** of the data's covariance matrix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why PCA works (The 'Why'):** Variance carries information. A feature whose values barely change across all samples carries little discriminative power. PCA mathematically identifies and prioritizes the axes along which the data *varies the most*, thereby compressing information efficiently.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step PCA Algorithm

1. **Standardize** the data: zero-mean, unit-variance per feature.
2. **Compute** the covariance matrix $\Sigma$.
3. **Decompose** $\Sigma$ into eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ and eigenvectors $v_i$.
4. **Sort** eigenvectors by descending eigenvalue.
5. **Select** the top $k$ eigenvectors to form the projection matrix $W$.
6. **Transform** data: $Y = X W$ yields the reduced representation.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every key equation you must memorize for the KTU 2024 Board Examination on Dimensionality Reduction.

| **Step / Concept** | **Formula** | **Notation & Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Mean Vector | $\mu_j = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij}$ | $j$-th feature mean; units = feature units |
| Standardization | $z_{ij} = \dfrac{x_{ij} - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}$ | $\sigma_j$ = standard deviation of feature $j$ |
| Covariance | $\sigma_{jk} = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{ij}-\mu_j)(x_{ik}-\mu_k)$ | Symmetric: $\sigma_{jk} = \sigma_{kj}$ |
| Covariance Matrix | $\Sigma = \dfrac{1}{n-1}X_c^{T}X_c$ | $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$, symmetric positive semi-definite |
| Eigen-equation | $\Sigma v_i = \lambda_i v_i$ | $\lambda_i$ = eigenvalue; $v_i$ = eigenvector, $\vert v_i \vert = 1$ |
| Explained Variance | $EVR_i = \dfrac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{d}\lambda_j}$ | Scalar ratio in $[0, 1]$ |
| Cumulative EVR | $CEVR(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} EVR_i$ | Choose smallest $k$ such that $CEVR \geq 0.95$ |
| Projection | $Y = X_c W$ | $W = [v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k] \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$ |
| Reconstruction | $\hat{X} = Y W^{T} + \mu$ | Approximate reconstruction in original space |
| Reconstruction Error | $E = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \vert^{2} = \sum_{i=k+1}^{d}\lambda_i$ | Equals variance discarded |
| Singular Value Decomposition (alternative) | $X = U \Sigma_{svd} V^{T}$ | $V$ contains PCA directions directly |
| Linear Discriminant Criterion | $J(w) = \dfrac{w^{T}S_B w}{w^{T}S_W w}$ | $S_B$ between-class, $S_W$ within-class scatter |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Use $\dfrac{1}{n-1}$ (sample covariance), not $\dfrac{1}{n}$, unless the problem explicitly states population statistics. Examiners often deduct 1 mark for this distinction.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Image & Video Compression:** Eigenfaces for facial recognition; SVD-based compression in streaming services.
- **Bioinformatics:** Gene expression data (thousands of genes per patient) reduced to 2D for visualization and clustering.
- **Recommender Systems:** Collaborative filtering uses SVD on user-item rating matrices.
- **Network Intrusion Detection:** Feature reduction accelerates real-time classification.
- **IoT Sensor Analytics:** Reducing 100+ sensor channels to a handful of latent process variables for predictive maintenance.
- **Computer Vision:** Preprocessing step before SVM/Neural Network classifiers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why PCA Maximizes Variance

Consider a 1-D projection of zero-mean data $X_c$ onto a unit direction $w$ (with $\vert w \vert = 1$). The projected scalar for sample $i$ is:

$$
y_i = w^{T} x_i
$$

The variance of these projections is:

$$
Var(y) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (w^{T} x_i)^{2} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} w^{T} x_i x_i^{T} w
$$

Factoring out $w$ on both sides:

$$
Var(y) = w^{T} \left( \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i x_i^{T} \right) w = w^{T} \Sigma w
$$

We now solve the **constrained optimization**:

$$
\max_{w} \; w^{T} \Sigma w \quad \text{subject to} \quad w^{T} w = 1
$$

Introduce the **Lagrange multiplier** $\lambda$:

$$
\mathcal{L}(w, \lambda) = w^{T} \Sigma w - \lambda (w^{T} w - 1)
$$

Differentiate with respect to $w$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 2 \Sigma w - 2 \lambda w = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \Sigma w = \lambda w
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **eigenvalue equation**. The direction $w$ that maximizes variance is the eigenvector corresponding to the **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda_{max}$. The maximum variance retained along that direction equals $\lambda_{max}$ itself.

For the $k$-dimensional subspace, the maximum variance captured equals $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reconstruction Error in PCA

Given an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors $V$, any point $x$ can be exactly represented as:

$$
x = \sum_{i=1}^{d} (v_i^{T} x)\, v_i
$$

If we keep only the top-$k$ components and discard the rest, the reconstruction is:

$$
\hat{x} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (v_i^{T} x)\, v_i
$$

The squared reconstruction error per point is:

$$
\vert x - \hat{x} \vert^{2} = \left\vert \sum_{i=k+1}^{d} (v_i^{T} x)\, v_i \right\vert^{2}
$$

Because the eigenvectors are **orthonormal**, cross-terms vanish and:

$$
\vert x - \hat{x} \vert^{2} = \sum_{i=k+1}^{d} (v_i^{T} x)^{2}
$$

Averaging over all $n$ samples and using the result from Derivation 1:

$$
\frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert x_j - \hat{x}_j \vert^{2} = \sum_{i=k+1}^{d} \lambda_i
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** *Minimizing reconstruction error is mathematically equivalent to maximizing retained variance.* This dual interpretation makes PCA both an information-preserving and error-minimizing technique.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
A dataset has 5 samples with 2 features. After mean-centering:

$$
X_c = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1: Compute covariance matrix.**

$$
\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^{T} X_c = \frac{1}{4} \begin{bmatrix} (-2)^2+(-1)^2+0^2+1^2+2^2 & (-2)(-1)+(-1)(0)+(0)(1)+(1)(0)+(2)(-1) \\ (\text{sym}) & (-1)^2+0^2+1^2+0^2+(-1)^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\Sigma = \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix} 10 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.75 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Solve characteristic equation** $\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = 0$.

$$
\det \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 - \lambda & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.75 - \lambda \end{bmatrix} = (2.5-\lambda)(0.75-\lambda) - 0.25 = 0
$$

Expanding:

$$
\lambda^{2} - 3.25\lambda + 1.875 - 0.25 = \lambda^{2} - 3.25\lambda + 1.625 = 0
$$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$
\lambda = \frac{3.25 \pm \sqrt{3.25^{2} - 4(1.625)}}{2} = \frac{3.25 \pm \sqrt{10.5625 - 6.5}}{2} = \frac{3.25 \pm \sqrt{4.0625}}{2}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{3.25 \pm 2.0156}{2}
$$

Thus $\lambda_1 \approx 2.6328$ and $\lambda_2 \approx 0.6172$.

**Step 3: Compute the principal eigenvector** for $\lambda_1 = 2.6328$.

From $(\Sigma - \lambda_1 I)v_1 = 0$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 2.5 - 2.6328 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.75 - 2.6328 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{11} \\ v_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1328 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & -1.8828 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{11} \\ v_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

From the first row: $-0.1328\, v_{11} + 0.5\, v_{12} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{12} \approx 0.2656\, v_{11}$.

Normalizing $\vert v_1 \vert = 1$:

$$
v_{11}^{2} + (0.2656\, v_{11})^{2} = 1 \Rightarrow v_{11}^{2}(1 + 0.0705) = 1 \Rightarrow v_{11} \approx 0.9667
$$

So $v_1 \approx [0.9667, \; 0.2567]^{T}$.

**Step 4: Explained Variance Ratio.**

$$
EVR_1 = \frac{2.6328}{2.6328 + 0.6172} = \frac{2.6328}{3.25} \approx 0.81
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The first principal component captures **~81%** of the total variance. Reducing from 2D to 1D loses only ~19% of information. This is a high-quality dimensionality reduction.

### 3.4 Feature Selection vs Feature Extraction — Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Criterion** | **Feature Selection** | **Feature Extraction** |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Subset of original features | New transformed features |
| Interpretability | High — original semantics retained | Low — components are linear combinations |
| Method Type | Filter, Wrapper, Embedded | PCA, LDA, Autoencoders |
| Information Loss | May discard correlated useful info | Optimally retains variance |
| Computation | Cheap to moderate | Eigen-decomposition is $O(d^{3})$ |
| KTU Example | Chi-squared test, Mutual Information, Recursive Feature Elimination | PCA, SVD, Kernel PCA |
| When to Use | Domain meaning of features matters | Visualizing or feeding black-box models |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PCA Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following diagram captures the **end-to-end functional flow** of a PCA-based dimensionality reduction pipeline, including inputs, transformations, intermediate artifacts, and outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Matrix X] --> B[Step 1: Mean Centering]
    B --> C[Step 2: Standardization Optional]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute Covariance Matrix Sigma]
    D --> E[Step 4: Eigen Decomposition of Sigma]
    E --> F[Step 5: Sort Eigenvalues in Descending Order]
    F --> G[Step 6: Compute Explained Variance Ratio EVR]
    G --> H{Choose k}
    H -->|Threshold 95 percent variance| I[Step 7a: Select Top k Eigenvectors]
    H -->|User specified k| I
    I --> J[Step 8: Build Projection Matrix W d by k]
    J --> K[Step 9: Transform Y equals X centered times W]
    K --> L[Reduced Representation Y n by k]
    L --> M[Downstream Tasks: Visualization Clustering Classification]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Visualization — Variance Maximization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ORIG[Original 2D Feature Space]
        P1[Data Cloud Ellipse] --> P2[Axes x1 and x2 - High Spread Direction]
        P1 --> P3[Orthogonal Direction - Low Spread]
    end

    ORIG --> ROT[Step: Rotate Coordinate System]
    ROT --> NEW[New Aligned Axes]

    subgraph NEWAX[Principal Component Subspace]
        N1[PC1 Direction - Maximum Variance]
        N2[PC2 Direction - Next Maximum Variance Orthogonal to PC1]
    end

    NEW --> NEWAX
    NEWAX --> DROP[Discard PC2]
    DROP --> PROJ[Project onto PC1 only]

    style P2 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px
    style N1 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px
    style DROP fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Comparison Map — Selection vs Extraction Paradigms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Dimensionality Reduction] --> SEL[Feature Selection]
```
