| Point | Coordinates | $d(P_i, \mu_1^{(1)})$ | $d(P_i, \mu_2^{(1)})$ | Assigned To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | $(2, 10)$ | $\sqrt{(2-3)^2 + (10-7.67)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 5.43} = 2.54$ | $\sqrt{(2-7)^2 + (10-4.33)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 32.15} = 7.56$ | **Cluster 1** |
| $P_2$ | $(2, 5)$ | $\sqrt{(2-3)^2 + (5-7.67)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 7.13} = 2.85$ | $\sqrt{(2-7)^2 + (5-4.33)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 0.45} = 5.05$ | **Cluster 1** |
| $P_3$ | $(8, 4)$ | $\sqrt{(8-3)^2 + (4-7.67)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 13.47} = 6.20$ | $\sqrt{(8-7)^2 + (4-4.33)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0.11} = 1.05$ | **Cluster 2** |
| $P_4$ | $(5, 8)$ | $\sqrt{(5-3)^2 + (8-7.67)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 0.11} = 2.03$ | $\sqrt{(5-7)^2 + (8-4.33)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 13.47} = 4.18$ | **Cluster 1** |
| $P_5$ | $(7, 5)$ | $\sqrt{(7-3)^2 + (5-7.67)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 7.13} = 4.81$ | $\sqrt{(7-7)^2 + (5-4.33)^2} = \sqrt{0 + 0.45} = 0.67$ | **Cluster 2** |
| $P_6$ | $(6, 4)$ | $\sqrt{(6-3)^2 + (4-7.67)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 13.47} = 4.74$ | $\sqrt{(6-7)^2 + (4-4.33)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0.11} = 1.05$ | **Cluster 2** |

**Clusters formed after Iteration 2:**

$$C_1^{(2)} = \{P_1, P_2, P_4\} \quad \text{(SAME AS PREVIOUS)}$$
$$C_2^{(2)} = \{P_3, P_5, P_6\} \quad \text{(SAME AS PREVIOUS)}$$

### **Step 2.2 — Recompute centroids**

Since the cluster membership is unchanged, the new centroids will be identical:

$$\mu_1^{(2)} = (3.00, \; 7.67) = \mu_1^{(1)}$$
$$\mu_2^{(2)} = (7.00, \; 4.33) = \mu_2^{(1)}$$

**Convergence Check:** $\mu_1^{(2)} = \mu_1^{(1)}$ and $\mu_2^{(2)} = \mu_2^{(1)}$ → **ALGORITHM CONVERGED! ✓**

---

## 3.4 Final Result

| Cluster | Members | Final Centroid |
|---|---|---|
| **Cluster 1** | $P_1(2,10), P_2(2,5), P_4(5,8)$ | $\mu_1 = (3.00, \; 7.67)$ |
| **Cluster 2** | $P_3(8,4), P_5(7,5), P_6(6,4)$ | $\mu_2 = (7.00, \; 4.33)$ |

### **Final Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**

$$J_{\text{final}} = \sum_{x \in C_1} \Vert x - \mu_1 \Vert^2 + \sum_{x \in C_2} \Vert x - \mu_2 \Vert^2$$

**For Cluster 1:**

$$J_1 = (2-3)^2 + (10-7.67)^2 + (2-3)^2 + (5-7.67)^2 + (5-3)^2 + (8-7.67)^2$$
$$J_1 = 1 + 5.43 + 1 + 7.13 + 4 + 0.11 = 18.67$$

**For Cluster 2:**

$$J_2 = (8-7)^2 + (4-4.33)^2 + (7-7)^2 + (5-4.33)^2 + (6-7)^2 + (4-4.33)^2$$
$$J_2 = 1 + 0.11 + 0 + 0.45 + 1 + 0.11 = 2.67$$

**Total WCSS:**

$$J = 18.67 + 2.67 = 21.34$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final WCSS = 21.34** — This is the inertia value. A lower WCSS indicates tighter, more compact clusters. To compare different values of $K$, use the **Elbow Method** to plot $J$ vs $K$ and find the "bend" point.

---

## 3.5 Choosing Optimal K (Elbow Method)

Compute $J$ for $K = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, 10$ and plot. The optimal $K$ is the elbow point where the marginal drop in $J$ sharply decreases.

> [!TIP]
> **Better Metric:** The **Silhouette Score** $s \in [-1, +1]$ measures how similar a point is to its own cluster vs. other clusters. Values near $+1$ indicate well-clustered points.

$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), \; b(i)\}}$$

where $a(i)$ = mean intra-cluster distance, $b(i)$ = mean nearest-cluster distance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — K-Means Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([START]) --> input[/Input: Dataset D, Number of K/]
    input --> initStep[Choose K Initial Centroids using Forgy or KMeans Plus Plus]
    initStep --> assignStep{ASSIGNMENT STEP: For every point x in D}
    assignStep --> distCalc[Compute Euclidean Distance d between x and all K centroids]
    distCalc --> assignCluster[Assign x to Cluster of nearest centroid]
    assignCluster --> morePoints{More Points in D?}
    morePoints -- Yes --> assignStep
    morePoints -- No --> updateStep[UPDATE STEP: Recompute Centroid of each cluster as arithmetic mean of its members]
    updateStep --> check{Convergence Check: Centroids unchanged or WCSS change less than epsilon}
    check -- No --> assignStep
    check -- Yes --> output[/Output: Final Clusters and Centroids/]
    output --> stop([END])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        A1[Raw Dataset] --> A2[Data Preprocessing]
        A2 --> A3[Feature Scaling via StandardScaler]
    end

    subgraph KMEANS_CORE
        B1[K Selection via Elbow Method] --> B2[Centroid Initialization KMeans Plus Plus]
        B2 --> B3[Distance Computation Engine]
        B3 --> B4[Cluster Assignment Module]
        B4 --> B5[Centroid Update Module]
        B5 --> B6[Convergence Detector]
        B6 -->|Not Converged| B3
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT
        C1[Cluster Labels] --> C2[Cluster Centroids]
        C2 --> C3[WCSS Inertia Score]
        C3 --> C4[Visualization via PCA or tSNE]
    end

    A3 --> B1
    B6 -->|Converged| C1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual Map — K-Means vs Variants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Partitioning Clustering Family] --> km[K Means: uses mean and Euclidean distance]
    root --> kmed[K Medoids: uses actual data point as center robust to outliers]
    root --> kmodes[K Modes: uses mode for categorical data]
    root --> kmedians[K Medians: uses median robust to outliers]

    km --> pros1[Fast O N K d I]
    km --> cons1[Sensitive to outliers and initialization]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define K-Means clustering. State its objective function.*

**Model Answer:**

K-Means is a partitional, centroid-based clustering algorithm that divides a dataset of $N$ objects into $K$ non-overlapping clusters, where each object belongs to the cluster with the nearest centroid (mean).

The objective is to minimize the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**:

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$$

where $C_i$ is the $i^{th}$ cluster and $\mu_i$ is its centroid. (3 marks)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Why is K-Means clustering sensitive to initial centroid selection? Suggest a solution.*

**Model Answer:**

K-Means converges to a **local minimum** of the objective function, not the global minimum. Poor initial centroid placement (e.g., two centroids in the same cluster) can lead to:
- Empty clusters.
- Suboptimal final partitioning.
- Slow convergence.

**Solution:** Use the **K-Means++ initialization** algorithm, which probabilistically chooses the next centroid proportional to its squared distance from the nearest existing centroid. This spreads initial centroids far apart and dramatically improves both the quality and reproducibility of results. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option I**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the steps of the **K-Means clustering algorithm** with a neat flowchart. Discuss the role of the objective function in its convergence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply K-Means clustering to the following 5 data points with **K = 2**. Use Euclidean distance. Initial centroids are $C_1 = (2, 2)$ and $C_2 = (5, 5)$. Show at least 2 iterations.

| Point | $x$ | $y$ |
|---|---|---|
| $A$ | 1 | 4 |
| $B$ | 2 | 3 |
| $C$ | 4 | 6 |
| $D$ | 5 | 7 |
| $E$ | 3 | 5 |

---

### **Model Solution to Question A (a):**

**Algorithm Steps (5 marks):**

1. **Input:** Dataset $D = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ and value of $K$.
2. **Initialize:** Choose $K$ initial centroids $\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_K$ (randomly or via K-Means++).
3. **Repeat until convergence:**
   - **Assignment Step:** Assign each $x_j$ to the cluster with the nearest centroid:
     $$C_i = \{x_j : \Vert x_j - \mu_i \Vert^2 \leq \Vert x_j - \mu_p \Vert^2 \; \forall p\}$$
   - **Update Step:** Recalculate each centroid as the mean of its assigned points:
     $$\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x_j \in C_i} x_j$$
4. **Output:** Final clusters and centroids.

**Role of Objective Function (2 marks):**

The WCSS function $J = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$ monotonically decreases (or stays constant) at every iteration. Since $J$ is bounded below by 0, the algorithm is guaranteed to converge. However, it converges only to a *local minimum*, sensitive to initialization. The update rule uses the **arithmetic mean** because it is the unique point that minimizes the sum of squared Euclidean distances.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**Iteration 1:** Centroids $C_1 = (2, 2)$ and $C_2 = (5, 5)$.

| Point | Coords | $d(\cdot, C_1)$ | $d(\cdot, C_2)$ | Assigned |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A(1,4)$ | $(1,4)$ | $\sqrt{1+4} = 2.24$ | $\sqrt{16+1} = 4.12$ | $C_1$ |
| $B(2,3)$ | $(2,3)$ | $\sqrt{0+1} = 1.00$ | $\sqrt{9+4} = 3.61$ | $C_1$ |
| $C(4,6)$ | $(4,6)$ | $\sqrt{4+16} = 4.47$ | $\sqrt{1+1} = 1.41$ | $C_2$ |
| $D(5,7)$ | $(5,7)$ | $\sqrt{9+25} = 5.83$ | $\sqrt{0+4} = 2.00$ | $C_2$ |
| $E(3,5)$ | $(3,5)$ | $\sqrt{1+9} = 3.16$ | $\sqrt{4+0} = 2.00$ | $C_2$ |

**Cluster 1:** $\{A, B\}$; **Cluster 2:** $\{C, D, E\}$.

**Update centroids:**

$$C_1^{\text{new}} = \left(\frac{1+2}{2}, \frac{4+3}{2}\right) = (1.5, 3.5)$$
$$C_2^{\text{new}} = \left(\frac{4+5+3}{3}, \frac{6+7+5}{3}\right) = (4.0, 6.0)$$

**Iteration 2:** New centroids $C_1 = (1.5, 3.5)$ and $C_2 = (4.0, 6.0)$.

| Point | $d(\cdot, C_1)$ | $d(\cdot, C_2)$ | Assigned |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A(1,4)$ | $\sqrt{0.25+0.25} = 0.71$ | $\sqrt{9+4} = 3.61$ | $C_1$ |
| $B(2,3)$ | $\sqrt{0.25+0.25} = 0.71$ | $\sqrt{4+9} = 3.61$ | $C_1$ |
| $C(4,6)$ | $\sqrt{6.25+6.25} = 3.54$ | $\sqrt{0+0} = 0.00$ | $C_2$ |
| $D(5,7)$ | $\sqrt{12.25+12.25} = 4.95$ | $\sqrt{1+1} = 1.41$ | $C_2$ |
| $E(3,5)$ | $\sqrt{2.25+2.25} = 2.12$ | $\sqrt{1+1} = 1.41$ | $C_2$ |

Clusters unchanged. Centroids recomputed will be identical → **CONVERGED.**

**Final:**
- **Cluster 1:** $\{A, B\}$, Centroid $(1.5, 3.5)$.
- **Cluster 2:** $\{C, D, E\}$, Centroid $(4.0, 6.0)$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option II**
`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of K-Means clustering. How is the value of $K$ determined in K-Means? Explain the **Elbow Method**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **K-Means, K-Medoids, and K-Modes** clustering algorithms. When would you prefer one over the other? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B (a):**

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. **Simple and easy to implement** with linear complexity $O(NKdI)$.
2. **Efficient on large datasets** — scales gracefully.
3. **Guaranteed convergence** to a local minimum of WCSS.
4. **Generalizable** — forms the basis for K-Medoids, K-Modes, Fuzzy C-Means.
5. Produces **tight, non-overlapping clusters** when data is well-separated and spherical.

**Limitations (3 marks):**
1. **Must pre-specify $K$** — no native method to determine it.
2. **Sensitive to initialization** — different starts yield different results.
3. **Sensitive to outliers** — a single outlier can drag the mean far from the true cluster center.
4. **Assumes spherical, equal-sized clusters** — fails on elongated, irregular, or varying-density shapes.
5. **Euclidean distance only** with raw data — biased by scale of features.
6. Converges to **local optimum**, not global.

**Determining $K$ (1 mark):**

**Elbow Method** — Plot WCSS ($J$) versus $K = 1, 2, \ldots, 10$. As $K$ increases, $J$ always decreases. The "elbow" — the $K$ value after which the marginal reduction in $J$ sharply diminishes — is considered the optimal number of clusters. Beyond this point, additional clusters do not provide meaningful information.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B (b):**

| Aspect | K-Means | K-Medoids (PAM) | K-Modes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cluster Center** | Mean (synthetic, may not be a real point) | Medoid (actual data point closest to center) | Mode (most frequent category) |
| **Distance Metric** | Euclidean (continuous) | Any distance (e.g., Manhattan, Cosine) | Simple Matching Dissimilarity (categorical) |
| **Data Type** | Numerical/continuous | Numerical/continuous | Categorical |
| **Outlier Sensitivity** | High (mean is dragged) | Low (medoid is robust) | Medium |
| **Complexity** | $O(NKdI)$ | $O(K(N-K)^2 I)$ — slower | $O(NKdI)$ |
| **Algorithm Origin** | Lloyd / MacQueen | Kaufman & Rousseeuw (1987, PAM) | Huang (1998) |
| **Typical Use** | Image compression, vector quantization | Bioinformatics, robust applications | Market segmentation, survey analysis |

**When to prefer (1 mark):**
- **Use K-Means** when data is continuous, clusters are roughly spherical, and speed is essential.
- **Use K-Medoids** when data is noisy or contains outliers and you need a representative actual data point as the cluster center.
- **Use K-Modes** when data is purely categorical (e.g., gender, color, region).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to show BOTH iterations** in numerical problems → at least 2 iterations must be shown to demonstrate convergence (3–4 marks lost if missing).
> 2. **Computing mean incorrectly** — always take mean of $x$-coordinates and $y$-coordinates separately, not the centroid of the bounding box.
> 3. **Not specifying convergence criterion** → always end with "centroids unchanged → algorithm converged."
> 4. **Forgetting to mention K-Means++** in limitations/advantages → examiners specifically test this in 14-mark questions.
> 5. **Using raw, unscaled data** in real-world application questions → always state "first apply `StandardScaler` to normalize features."
> 6. **Confusing "between-cluster variance" with "within-cluster variance"** → WCSS goes *down*; BCSS goes *up* with good clustering.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 High-Yield Last-Minute Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **K-Means** is a **partitional, unsupervised, centroid-based** clustering algorithm.
- ✅ **Goal:** Minimize **WCSS / Inertia**: $J = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$.
- ✅ **Two iterative steps:** (1) **Assignment** to nearest centroid, (2) **Update** centroid as the arithmetic mean.
- ✅ **Centroid formula:** $\mu_i = \dfrac{1}{n_i} \sum_{x \in C_i} x$ — the mean minimizes sum of squared distances.
- ✅ **Distance metric:** Euclidean (default), Manhattan, Cosine (alternatives).
- ✅ **Complexity:** $O(N \cdot K \cdot d \cdot I)$ — linear in $N$ and efficient for big data.
- ✅ **Initialization methods:** Forgy, Random Partition, **K-Means++** (best practice).
- ✅ **Choosing K:** Elbow Method, Silhouette Score, Gap Statistic.
- ✅ **Convergence:** Centroids stop changing OR WCSS change < $\epsilon$ OR max iterations reached.
- ✅ **Sensitivities:** Initialization, outliers, feature scaling, choice of $K$, cluster shape.
- ✅ **Variants:** K-Medoids (robust), K-Modes (categorical), K-Medians, Fuzzy C-Means (soft).
- ✅ **Pre-processing is mandatory:** Standardize / Normalize features before clustering.
- ✅ **Quality metrics:** WCSS (lower = better), Silhouette Score ($\in [-1, +1]$, higher = better), Davies-Bouldin Index (lower = better).
- ✅ **Convergence is to a LOCAL minimum only** — run multiple random restarts to find a better solution.
- ✅ **The mean is the UNIQUE point that minimizes sum of squared Euclidean distances** — this is why K-Means uses the mean as centroid.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hierarchical Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST525 - DATA MINING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hierarchical Clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Hierarchical Clustering** is a *unsupervised machine learning technique* that organizes a set of data points into a tree-like hierarchy of nested clusters, denoted as a **dendrogram**. Unlike partitional methods (such as K-Means) that produce a single flat partition, hierarchical clustering generates a multi-level grouping where clusters at one level are formed by merging or splitting clusters at the next level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Hierarchical clustering is a method of cluster analysis which seeks to build a hierarchy of clusters. It produces a sequence of partitions, with each partition nested inside the next partition in the sequence. Two primary strategies exist — **Agglomerative** (bottom-up) and **Divisive** (top-down).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Family Tree of Species

Imagine a **biological taxonomy**: all living beings start as one super-cluster "Life on Earth." Scientists split it into "Plants" and "Animals." Animals are further split into "Vertebrates" and "Invertebrates," and so on down to individual species.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Explained:** Hierarchical clustering works exactly like building a family tree for your data. Start with each data point as a separate "individual" (leaf of the tree). At each step, the two most *similar* (closest) individuals are "married off" into a small family. Then the closest families merge into larger ones, and so on, until everyone belongs to one big "ancestor" cluster at the root.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Board-Exam Vocabulary)

- **Dendrogram** — Tree-like diagram that records the sequences of merges/splits and the distance at which they occur.
- **Agglomerative Coefficient (AC)** — A scalar value in $[0, 1]$ that measures the clustering structure strength; values closer to 1 indicate stronger structure.
- **Linkage Criterion** — Rule used to compute the distance between two clusters (e.g., *single*, *complete*, *average*, *Ward's*).
- **Cophenetic Distance** — The dendrogram-derived distance between two points (height of their lowest common ancestor in the tree).
- **Proximity Matrix** — Initial $N \times N$ matrix of pairwise distances used as input.
- **Monotonicity** — Property of a proper hierarchical clustering: the dendrogram distances never decrease as we move up the tree.

## 1.4 Formal Mathematical Setup

Given a dataset $D = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$, hierarchical clustering produces a sequence of partitions $P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_{n-1}$ where:
- $P_0 = \{\{x_1\}, \{x_2\}, \ldots, \{x_n\}\}$ (each point in its own cluster — *singleton* partition).
- $P_{n-1} = \{D\}$ (single cluster containing all points).
- Each $P_{t+1}$ is obtained from $P_t$ by either merging two clusters (agglomerative) or splitting one cluster (divisive).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Paradigms of Hierarchical Clustering

### (A) Agglomerative (Bottom-Up) — *The KTU-Favorite*

1. Start with $n$ singleton clusters $C_i = \{x_i\}$.
2. Compute the $n \times n$ **proximity matrix** $M$ of pairwise distances.
3. Repeat until a single cluster remains:
    - Find the pair $(C_i, C_j)$ with the smallest inter-cluster distance.
    - Merge them into $C_{ij} = C_i \cup C_j$.
    - Update the proximity matrix by computing the new distances from $C_{ij}$ to all remaining clusters using a chosen **linkage criterion**.
4. Record the merge index, the merging distance, and the cluster sizes to build a dendrogram.

> [!TIP]
> **Time complexity:** $O(n^3)$ in the naive form. Using a priority queue reduces it to $O(n^2 \log n)$. **Space complexity:** $O(n^2)$ for the proximity matrix.

### (B) Divisive (Top-Down)

1. Start with one cluster containing all points.
2. At each step, select the cluster with the largest diameter.
3. Split it into two sub-clusters (typically using a flat clustering method like K-Means with $k=2$).
4. Repeat until each cluster is a singleton.

> [!WARNING]
> Divisive clustering is **computationally expensive ($O(2^n)$)** because we must consider all possible binary splits. It is rarely used in practice for large datasets.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every formula a KTU 2024 board examiner can ask under "Hierarchical Clustering." **Memorize the linkage parameters** in the Lance-Williams row.

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Notes / Boundary Conditions** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Euclidean Distance | $d(i,j) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{d}(x_{ik} - x_{jk})^{2}}$ | Most common metric; sensitive to scale |
| 2 | Manhattan Distance | $d(i,j) = \sum_{k=1}^{d} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert$ | Robust to outliers; uses $\vert \cdot \vert$ for absolute value |
| 3 | Minkowski Distance (Generalized) | $d(i,j) = \left(\sum_{k=1}^{d} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert^{p}\right)^{1/p}$ | $p=1 \Rightarrow$ Manhattan; $p=2 \Rightarrow$ Euclidean; $p \to \infty \Rightarrow$ Chebyshev |
| 4 | Single Linkage (MIN) | $d(C_i, C_j) = \min_{x \in C_i,\, y \in C_j} d(x, y)$ | Tends to produce **chaining** — long, straggly clusters |
| 5 | Complete Linkage (MAX) | $d(C_i, C_j) = \max_{x \in C_i,\, y \in C_j} d(x, y)$ | Produces **compact, tight** clusters; sensitive to outliers |
| 6 | Average Linkage (UPGMA) | $d(C_i, C_j) = \dfrac{1}{\vert C_i \vert \cdot \vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x \in C_i, y \in C_j} d(x, y)$ | Compromise between single and complete |
| 7 | Centroid Linkage (UPGMC) | $d(C_i, C_j) = d(\mu_{C_i}, \mu_{C_j})$ where $\mu_C = \dfrac{1}{\vert C \vert}\sum_{x \in C} x$ | Distance between cluster centroids |
| 8 | Ward's Method (Minimum Variance) | $\Delta(A,B) = \dfrac{\vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} \cdot d^{2}(\mu_A, \mu_B)$ | Minimizes within-cluster sum of squares; **most popular in KTU exams** |
| 9 | Lance–Williams Update Formula | $d(C_{ij}, C_k) = \alpha_i \, d(C_i, C_k) + \alpha_j \, d(C_j, C_k) + \beta \, d(C_i, C_j) + \gamma \cdot \vert d(C_i, C_k) - d(C_j, C_k) \vert$ | Unified recurrence; $\alpha_i, \alpha_j, \beta, \gamma$ define the linkage (see table below) |
| 10 | Agglomerative Coefficient (AC) | $AC = \dfrac{1}{n(n-1)/2} \sum_{i<j} (1 - c_{ij})$ where $c_{ij}$ = cophenetic similarity | Measures clustering strength; range $[0,1]$ |

### 2.3 Lance-Williams Parameters (CHEAT-SHEET — High Weightage!)

| **Linkage Method** | $\alpha_i$ | $\alpha_j$ | $\beta$ | $\gamma$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single | $1/2$ | $1/2$ | $0$ | $-1/2$ |
| Complete | $1/2$ | $1/2$ | $0$ | $+1/2$ |
| Average (UPGMA) | $\dfrac{\vert C_i \vert}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert}$ | $\dfrac{\vert C_j \vert}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert}$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| Centroid (UPGMC) | $\dfrac{\vert C_i \vert}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert}$ | $\dfrac{\vert C_j \vert}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert}$ | $\dfrac{-\vert C_i \vert \cdot \vert C_j \vert}{(\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert)^{2}}$ | $0$ |
| Ward | $\dfrac{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_k \vert}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert + \vert C_k \vert}$ | $\dfrac{\vert C_j \vert + \vert C_k \vert}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert + \vert C_k \vert}$ | $\dfrac{-\vert C_k \vert}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert + \vert C_k \vert}$ | $0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Hierarchical clustering is used in **phylogenetic tree construction** (biology), **customer segmentation** (marketing), **document/topic hierarchies** (NLP), **gene expression analysis** (bioinformatics), and **image segmentation** (computer vision). Unlike K-Means, it does not require pre-specifying $k$ and produces a *full taxonomy* of the data.

## 2.4 Comparison: Agglomerative vs Divisive

| **Property** | **Agglomerative (AGNES)** | **Divisive (DIANA)** |
|---|---|---|
| Strategy | Bottom-up | Top-down |
| Starting state | $n$ singletons | 1 mega-cluster |
| Complexity | $O(n^3)$ (naive) | $O(2^n)$ |
| Used in practice? | **Yes, very common** | Rarely |
| Decision needed at each step | Which two to **merge** | Which cluster to **split** and how |
| Tie-breaking | Choose min distance pair | Choose cluster with max diameter |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering (Single Linkage, Euclidean)

### 3.1.1 The Dataset

Consider four 2-D data points:

$$A = (1, 1), \quad B = (1, 2), \quad C = (4, 1), \quad D = (4, 5)$$

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Compute the Pairwise Euclidean Distance Matrix

We compute all $d(i, j) = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(A, B) &= \sqrt{(1-1)^2 + (1-2)^2} = \sqrt{0 + 1} = 1 \\
d(A, C) &= \sqrt{(1-4)^2 + (1-1)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 0} = 3 \\
d(A, D) &= \sqrt{(1-4)^2 + (1-5)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \\
d(B, C) &= \sqrt{(1-4)^2 + (2-1)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 1} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.16 \\
d(B, D) &= \sqrt{(1-4)^2 + (2-5)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 9} = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.24 \\
d(C, D) &= \sqrt{(4-4)^2 + (1-5)^2} = \sqrt{0 + 16} = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

The initial proximity matrix $M_0$ is:

$$M_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 3 & 5 \\ 1 & 0 & 3.16 & 4.24 \\ 3 & 3.16 & 0 & 4 \\ 5 & 4.24 & 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — First Merge (Iteration 1)

Find the minimum off-diagonal entry: $\min(M_0) = 1$, corresponding to the pair $(A, B)$.

**Action:** Merge $A$ and $B$ into cluster $C_1 = \{A, B\}$ at height $h_1 = 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> The *merge height* $h_1$ is the distance at which the two clusters joined — it becomes the y-coordinate of the merge node in the dendrogram.

Update the distance matrix using **single linkage** (minimum distance rule):

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(C_1, C) &= \min\bigl(d(A, C),\, d(B, C)\bigr) = \min(3, 3.16) = 3 \\
d(C_1, D) &= \min\bigl(d(A, D),\, d(B, D)\bigr) = \min(5, 4.24) = 4.24 \\
d(C, D) &= 4 \quad \text{(unchanged)}
\end{aligned}
$$

New matrix $M_1$ is $3 \times 3$:

$$M_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & 4.24 \\ 3 & 0 & 4 \\ 4.24 & 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(clusters: } C_1, C, D\text{)}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Second Merge (Iteration 2)

Minimum of $M_1$ is $3$ between $C_1$ and $C$.

**Action:** Merge $C_1 = \{A, B\}$ with $C$ into $C_2 = \{A, B, C\}$ at height $h_2 = 3$.

Update:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(C_2, D) &= \min\bigl(d(A, D),\, d(B, D),\, d(C, D)\bigr) = \min(5, 4.24, 4) = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

New matrix $M_2$ is $2 \times 2$:

$$M_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(clusters: } C_2, D\text{)}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Final Merge (Iteration 3)

Only one entry remains: $4$. Merge $C_2$ and $D$ into $C_3 = \{A, B, C, D\}$ at height $h_3 = 4$.

> [!TIP]
> The sequence of merge heights $\{1, 3, 4\}$ is read bottom-up in the dendrogram. A **horizontal cut** at any height $h$ produces $k$ clusters, where $k$ is the number of vertical lines the cut intersects.

### 3.1.6 The Resulting Dendrogram (Sketch)

```
Height
  4 ┤        ┌─────────────┐
    │        │             │
  3 ┤   ┌────┤             │
    │   │    │             │
  1 ┤ A─┤B   │             │
    │   │    │             │
    │   │   C              D
    └───┴─────────────────────
        1    2     3   4   5
```

Two natural clusters emerge if we cut at $h = 3.5$: $C_2 = \{A, B, C\}$ and $\{D\}$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Lance-Williams Update Formula

### 3.2.1 Setup

Suppose clusters $C_i$ and $C_j$ have just been merged into $C_{ij} = C_i \cup C_j$. We need an efficient way to recompute the distance from $C_{ij}$ to any other cluster $C_k$ without scanning all $\vert C_i \vert \cdot \vert C_j \vert \cdot \vert C_k \vert$ point pairs.

### 3.2.2 The Recurrence (General Form)

The Lance-Williams formula states that any valid hierarchical linkage can be expressed as a linear combination of the *pre-merge* distances:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(C_{ij}, C_k) &= \alpha_i \, d(C_i, C_k) + \alpha_j \, d(C_j, C_k) \\
&\quad + \beta \, d(C_i, C_j) \\
&\quad + \gamma \cdot \bigl\vert d(C_i, C_k) - d(C_j, C_k) \bigr\vert
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\alpha_i, \alpha_j, \beta, \gamma$ are constants (depending on cluster sizes and the chosen linkage).

### 3.2.3 Derivation for Single Linkage

For single linkage: $d(C_{ij}, C_k) = \min\bigl(d(C_i, C_k), d(C_j, C_k)\bigr)$.

Recall the algebraic identity:

$$
\min(a, b) = \tfrac{1}{2}(a + b) - \tfrac{1}{2}\vert a - b \vert
$$

Substituting $a = d(C_i, C_k)$ and $b = d(C_j, C_k)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(C_{ij}, C_k) &= \min\bigl(d(C_i, C_k), d(C_j, C_k)\bigr) \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} d(C_i, C_k) + \tfrac{1}{2} d(C_j, C_k) - \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl\vert d(C_i, C_k) - d(C_j, C_k) \bigr\vert
\end{aligned}
$$

Comparing with the general form, we read off:

$$
\alpha_i = \tfrac{1}{2}, \quad \alpha_j = \tfrac{1}{2}, \quad \beta = 0, \quad \gamma = -\tfrac{1}{2}
$$

### 3.2.4 Derivation for Complete Linkage

For complete linkage: $d(C_{ij}, C_k) = \max\bigl(d(C_i, C_k), d(C_j, C_k)\bigr)$.

Using the identity $\max(a, b) = \tfrac{1}{2}(a + b) + \tfrac{1}{2}\vert a - b \vert$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(C_{ij}, C_k) &= \tfrac{1}{2} d(C_i, C_k) + \tfrac{1}{2} d(C_j, C_k) + \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl\vert d(C_i, C_k) - d(C_j, C_k) \bigr\vert
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $\alpha_i = \alpha_j = \tfrac{1}{2},\, \beta = 0,\, \gamma = +\tfrac{1}{2}$.

### 3.2.5 Why Lance-Williams is Powerful

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Once the initial $n \times n$ distance matrix is computed, the Lance-Williams recurrence lets us update the matrix in $O(n)$ per merge — yielding the celebrated $O(n^2)$ **nearest-neighbor-chain / priority-queue algorithms** (e.g., the *SLINK* algorithm for single linkage). It is the **computational heart** of every library implementing hierarchical clustering (e.g., `scipy.cluster.hierarchy`, `R's hclust()`).

## 3.3 Agglomerative Coefficient (AC) — Measuring Cluster Quality

Given $n$ points, the *cophenetic distance* $c(x_i, x_j)$ is the height at which $x_i$ and $x_j$ first join in the dendrogram. The AC is the average *agreement* between the original distances and the cophenetic distances:

$$
AC = \frac{1}{\binom{n}{2}} \sum_{1 \le i < j \le n} \left(1 - \frac{\vert d(x_i, x_j) - c(x_i, x_j) \vert}{\max_{u < v} d(x_u, x_v)}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $AC \to 1$ means the dendrogram faithfully preserves the original pairwise distances (strong cluster structure). $AC \to 0$ implies a near-random hierarchy. The KTU board often asks: *"Given a dendrogram and distance matrix, compute the AC."*

## 3.4 Choosing the Number of Clusters $k$

Although hierarchical clustering does not require $k$ in advance, $k$ must still be chosen *post-hoc*. The standard methods are:

1. **Dendrogram Cut:** Draw a horizontal line that crosses the **largest vertical gap** between successive merge heights. The number of intersected vertical lines = $k$.
2. **Elbow Method on Merge Heights:** Plot merge heights $h_1 \le h_2 \le \dots$ vs. iteration number. The "elbow" point indicates optimal $k$.
3. **Inconsistency Coefficient:** For each merge, compute the inconsistency — the average of all links below it weighted by their variance. High-inconsistency merges are likely cluster boundaries.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering — Algorithmic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([START N DATA POINTS])
    initNode["INITIALIZE each point as a singleton cluster"]
    distNode["COMPUTE N by N proximity matrix using Euclidean or other metric"]
    findNode["FIND minimum inter-cluster distance pair"]
    linkNode{"CHOOSE linkage criterion SINGLE COMPLETE AVERAGE WARD"}
    mergeNode["MERGE the chosen pair into a new cluster"]
    updateNode["UPDATE distance matrix using LANCE WILLIAMS recurrence"]
    checkNode{"MORE THAN ONE CLUSTER REMAINS"}
    dendroNode["RECORD merge sequence and heights into a DENDROGRAM"]
    cutNode["CUT dendrogram at chosen height to obtain K clusters"]
    endNode([STOP])
    
    startNode --> initNode --> distNode --> findNode --> linkNode
    linkNode --> mergeNode --> updateNode --> checkNode
    checkNode -- YES --> findNode
    checkNode -- NO --> dendroNode --> cutNode --> endNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The loop (find → merge → update) executes exactly $N-1$ times, reducing the cluster count from $N$ down to $1$. The Lance-Williams update inside the loop is the *only* place where the linkage criterion enters the algorithm — making it the single most important implementation detail.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Hierarchical Clustering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawData["RAW DATA MATRIX N TIMES D"]
    normMod["PREPROCESSING MODULE Scaling Encoding Imputation"]
    distMod["DISTANCE COMPUTATION MODULE Euclidean Manhattan Cosine"]
    linkMod["LINKAGE STRATEGY MODULE Single Complete Average Ward"]
    updateMod["LANCE WILLIAMS UPDATE MODULE Alpha Beta Gamma Coefficients"]
    mergeLog["MERGE LOG STRUCTURE Cluster ID Parent ID Height Size"]
    dendroMod["DENDROGRAM RENDERER Tree Visualizer"]
    evalMod["EVALUATION MODULE Agglomerative Coefficient Silhouette Gap Statistic"]
    outClust["FINAL K CLUSTERS"]
    
    rawData --> normMod --> distMod --> linkMod
    linkMod --> updateMod --> mergeLog
    mergeLog --> dendroMod --> evalMod --> outClust
    distMod -.feeds back.-> linkMod
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering insight:** The **Merge Log** is the *de-facto* output of hierarchical clustering. From it, the dendrogram, the cluster labels at any $k$, and the agglomerative coefficient can all be derived *after the fact*. This is why hierarchical clustering is called a *post-partitioning* technique — you can change $k$ without re-running the algorithm.

## 4.3 Conceptual Dendrogram Mapping (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootNode["ROOT ALL POINTS HEIGHT 4"]
    leftInt["INTERNAL NODE ABC HEIGHT 3"]
    rightLeaf["LEAF D HEIGHT 0"]
    leftIntLow["INTERNAL NODE AB HEIGHT 1"]
    leafA["LEAF A HEIGHT 0"]
    leafB["LEAF B HEIGHT 0"]
    leafC["LEAF C HEIGHT 0"]
    
    rootNode --> leftInt
    rootNode --> rightLeaf
    leftInt --> leftIntLow
    leftInt --> leafC
    leftIntLow --> leafA
    leftIntLow --> leafB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cophenetic distance** between any two leaves = height of their **lowest common ancestor (LCA)**. For example, $c(A, C) = 3$ and $c(A, D) = 4$. These cophenetic distances are the building blocks of the Agglomerative Coefficient.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> Question pattern follows KTU 2024 Scheme: Part A (2-mark conceptual + 3-mark short answer) and Part B (14-mark descriptive with **internal choice**). Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define hierarchical clustering. Differentiate between agglomerative and divisive hierarchical clustering.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Hierarchical clustering is an unsupervised clustering technique that builds a hierarchy of clusters represented as a dendrogram, producing a nested sequence of partitions.

**Differentiation (2 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Agglomerative** | **Divisive** |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | Bottom-up (merge) | Top-down (split) |
| Start | $N$ singleton clusters | One cluster with all points |
| Complexity | $O(N^3)$ (naive) | $O(2^N)$ |

---

### Q2. What is a dendrogram? How is the number of clusters chosen from it?
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **dendrogram (1 mark)** is a tree-like visual representation of the hierarchical clustering process, where the vertical axis represents the distance (merge height) at which clusters are joined, and the horizontal axis represents the data points/clusters.

**Choosing $k$ (2 marks):** Draw a horizontal line across the dendrogram at a height where a **largest vertical gap** exists between two successive merges. The number of vertical lines the cut intersects equals the desired number of clusters $k$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Question A OR Question B must be answered (not both). Both carry 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### 📘 Question A — *Lance-Williams and Linkage Methods*

**(a) Explain the four major linkage methods used in hierarchical clustering. For each, state the Lance-Williams parameters $\alpha_i, \alpha_j, \beta, \gamma$.** **[7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(b) Given five 2-D points $P_1(0,0), P_2(1,0), P_3(0,2), P_4(6,6), P_5(7,7)$, perform agglomerative hierarchical clustering using single linkage. Draw the dendrogram and identify the clusters at $k = 2$.** **[7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### ✅ Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**1. Single Linkage (MIN) — 2 marks**

The distance between two clusters is the *minimum* distance between any pair of points, one from each cluster:

$$d(C_i, C_j) = \min_{x \in C_i, y \in C_j} d(x, y)$$

- **Lance-Williams parameters:** $\alpha_i = \alpha_j = \tfrac{1}{2},\, \beta = 0,\, \gamma = -\tfrac{1}{2}$
- **Tendency:** Produces **chaining** — long, straggly clusters.
- **Pros:** Handles non-elliptical shapes. **Cons:** Sensitive to noise bridges.

**2. Complete Linkage (MAX) — 2 marks**

The distance is the *maximum* distance between any pair of points:

$$d(C_i, C_j) = \max_{x \in C_i, y \in C_j} d(x, y)$$

- **Lance-Williams parameters:** $\alpha_i = \alpha_j = \tfrac{1}{2},\, \beta = 0,\, \gamma = +\tfrac{1}{2}$
- **Tendency:** Produces **compact, spherical** clusters.
- **Pros:** Less sensitive to outliers than single. **Cons:** Biased toward equal-sized clusters.

**3. Average Linkage (UPGMA) — 1.5 marks**

The distance is the *average* over all pairs:

$$d(C_i, C_j) = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert \cdot \vert C_j \vert}\sum_{x \in C_i}\sum_{y \in C_j} d(x, y)$$

- **Lance-Williams parameters:** $\alpha_i = \dfrac{\vert C_i \vert}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert},\; \alpha_j = \dfrac{\vert C_j \vert}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert},\; \beta = 0,\; \gamma = 0$
- **Tendency:** Compromise between single and complete.

**4. Ward's Method — 1.5 marks**

Merges the pair of clusters that leads to the **minimum increase in total within-cluster variance**:

$$\Delta(C_i, C_j) = \frac{\vert C_i \vert \cdot \vert C_j \vert}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert} \cdot \lVert \mu_{C_i} - \mu_{C_j} \rVert^{2}$$

- **Lance-Williams parameters:**

$$
\alpha_i = \frac{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_k \vert}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert + \vert C_k \vert}, \quad
\alpha_j = \frac{\vert C_j \vert + \vert C_k \vert}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert + \vert C_k \vert}, \quad
\beta = \frac{-\vert C_k \vert}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert + \vert C_k \vert}, \quad
\gamma = 0
$$

- **Tendency:** Produces **equal-volume, compact** clusters; widely used in practice.

#### ✅ Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute the distance matrix (2 marks)**

Using Euclidean distance $d = \sqrt{(x_1-x_2)^2 + (y_1-y_2)^2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(P_1, P_2) &= \sqrt{1^2 + 0^2} = 1 \\
d(P_1, P_3) &= \sqrt{0^2 + 2^2} = 2 \\
d(P_1, P_4) &= \sqrt{6^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{72} \approx 8.49 \\
d(P_1, P_5) &= \sqrt{7^2 + 7^2} = \sqrt{98} \approx 9.90 \\
d(P_2, P_3) &= \sqrt{1^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24 \\
d(P_2, P_4) &= \sqrt{5^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{61} \approx 7.81 \\
d(P_2, P_5) &= \sqrt{6^2 + 7^2} = \sqrt{85} \approx 9.22 \\
d(P_3, P_4) &= \sqrt{6^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.21 \\
d(P_3, P_5) &= \sqrt{7^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{74} \approx 8.60 \\
d(P_4, P_5) &= \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41
\end{aligned}
$$

**Initial matrix $M_0$ (1 mark):**

$$M_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 2 & 8.49 & 9.90 \\ 1 & 0 & 2.24 & 7.81 & 9.22 \\ 2 & 2.24 & 0 & 7.21 & 8.60 \\ 8.49 & 7.81 & 7.21 & 0 & 1.41 \\ 9.90 & 9.22 & 8.60 & 1.41 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: First merge (1.5 marks)**

Min value = $1$ between $P_1$ and $P_2$. **Merge $C_1 = \{P_1, P_2\}$ at $h_1 = 1$.**

Update using single linkage (MIN):

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(C_1, P_3) &= \min(2, 2.24) = 2 \\
d(C_1, P_4) &= \min(8.49, 7.81) = 7.81 \\
d(C_1, P_5) &= \min(9.90, 9.22) = 9.22
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Second merge (1.5 marks)**

New matrix min = $1.41$ between $P_4$ and $P_5$. **Merge $C_2 = \{P_4, P_5\}$ at $h_2 = 1.41$.**

Update: $d(C_1, C_2) = \min(7.81, 9.22, 7.21, 8.60) = 7.21$

$d(P_3, C_2) = \min(7.21, 8.60) = 7.21$

**Step 4: Third merge (1 mark)**

$d(P_3, C_1) = 2$ and $d(P_3, C_2) = 7.21$. Min = 2. **Merge $C_3 = \{P_1, P_2, P_3\}$ at $h_3 = 2$.**

**Step 5: Final merge (1 mark)**

$d(C_3, C_2) = \min(7.81, 9.22, 7.21, 8.60) = 7.21$. **Merge all at $h_4 = 7.21$.**

**Merge sequence (in dendrogram order):**

| **Iteration** | **Merge** | **Height** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\{P_1, P_2\}$ | $1.00$ |
| 2 | $\{P_4, P_5\}$ | $1.41$ |
| 3 | $\{P_1, P_2\} \cup \{P_3\}$ | $2.00$ |
| 4 | $\{P_1, P_2, P_3\} \cup \{P_4, P_5\}$ | $7.21$ |

**Clusters at $k=2$:** $C_3 = \{P_1, P_2, P_3\}$ and $C_2 = \{P_4, P_5\}$.

---

### 📗 Question B — *Dendrograms, Monotonicity & AC*

**(a) What is a dendrogram? Explain its components and how the number of clusters is determined from it. State and prove the monotonicity property of hierarchical clustering.** **[7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(b) Given the following cophenetic similarity matrix $C$ and original distance matrix $D$ for 4 points, compute the Agglomerative Coefficient (AC):**
$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 & 5 & 7 \\ 2 & 0 & 5 & 7 \\ 5 & 5 & 0 & 3 \\ 7 & 7 & 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
D = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & 4 & 8 \\ 3 & 0 & 6 & 9 \\ 4 & 6 & 0 & 5 \\ 8 & 9 & 5 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$
**Use the formula $AC = \dfrac{1}{\binom{n}{2}}\sum_{i<j}\left(1 - \dfrac{\vert d_{ij} - c_{ij} \vert}{\max d_{ij}}\right)$.** **[7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

#### ✅ Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):** A **dendrogram** is a tree diagram that visualizes the arrangement of clusters produced by a hierarchical clustering algorithm. The vertical axis represents the *dissimilarity* (distance) at which clusters merge, and the horizontal axis represents the data points.

**Components (2 marks):**
- **Leaves (terminal nodes):** Individual data points at height 0.
- **Internal nodes:** Merge points, where two sub-clusters are joined.
- **Vertical edges (branches):** Lengths represent the distance between merges.
- **Root:** The single cluster containing all data, at the top of the tree.

**Determining $k$ (2 marks):**
1. Compute successive merge heights $h_1 \le h_2 \le \dots \le h_{n-1}$.
2. Identify the **largest gap** $h_{k+1} - h_k$.
3. Cut the dendrogram at any height $h$ where $h_k < h < h_{k+1}$. The number of intersected vertical lines gives $k$.

**Monotonicity Property (2 marks):**

> **Statement:** The heights of the merges in a valid hierarchical clustering form a *monotonically non-decreasing* sequence: $h_1 \le h_2 \le \dots \le h_{n-1}$.

**Proof sketch:** By construction, agglomerative clustering always merges the *currently nearest* pair. Since the minimum inter-cluster distance cannot decrease as clusters grow (merging can only create points that are at least as far apart as the current merge distance, because distances are non-negative), the sequence of merge heights is monotonically non-decreasing. The root (final merge) is the maximum possible distance. Hence the dendrogram is well-formed.

#### ✅ Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Extract unique pairs (1 mark)**

For $n=4$ points, there are $\binom{4}{2} = 6$ pairs: $(1,2), (1,3), (1,4), (2,3), (2,4), (3,4)$.

**Step 2: Tabulate $d_{ij}$ and $c_{ij}$ (1.5 marks)**

| **Pair** | $d_{ij}$ | $c_{ij}$ | $\vert d - c \vert$ | $\max d = 9$ | $1 - \frac{\vert d - c \vert}{9}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $(1,2)$ | 3 | 2 | 1 | 9 | $1 - 1/9 = 8/9$ |
| $(1,3)$ | 4 | 5 | 1 | 9 | $1 - 1/9 = 8/9$ |
| $(1,4)$ | 8 | 7 | 1 | 9 | $1 - 1/9 = 8/9$ |
| $(2,3)$ | 6 | 5 | 1 | 9 | $1 - 1/9 = 8/9$ |
| $(2,4)$ | 9 | 7 | 2 | 9 | $1 - 2/9 = 7/9$ |
| $(3,4)$ | 5 | 3 | 2 | 9 | $1 - 2/9 = 7/9$ |

**Step 3: Sum the agreement terms (2 marks)**

$$
\sum_{i<j} \left(1 - \frac{\vert d_{ij} - c_{ij} \vert}{9}\right) = 4 \times \frac{8}{9} + 2 \times \frac{7}{9} = \frac{32 + 14}{9} = \frac{46}{9}
$$

**Step 4: Compute AC (2.5 marks)**

$$
AC = \frac{1}{6} \times \frac{46}{9} = \frac{46}{54} = \frac{23}{27} \approx 0.852
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** $AC \approx 0.85$ is *very high*, indicating the dendrogram is a faithful representation of the original distance structure. Values above $0.75$ are considered strong cluster structures in practice.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks (Repeated Every Year!)**
> 1. **Forgetting to update the distance matrix** after each merge (most common: 2–3 marks lost).
> 2. **Confusing single and complete linkage formulas.** Single uses MIN, complete uses MAX — write them explicitly on the answer sheet.
> 3. **Wrong Lance-Williams parameters.** Students often write $\alpha_i = \alpha_j = 1$ (incorrect); the correct values for single/complete are $1/2$ each.
> 4. **Not drawing the dendrogram** in numerical problems. KTU examiners allocate at least 2 marks for the dendrogram sketch with merge heights.
> 5. **Ignoring the linkage's bias:** Single → chaining; Complete → compact; Ward → minimum variance. KTU loves to ask *"Which linkage is best for noisy data?"* (Answer: Average or Complete).
> 6. **Mistaking hierarchical clustering for K-Means.** Hierarchical does NOT need $k$ in advance; K-Means does.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam**

- ✅ **Two paradigms:** Agglomerative (bottom-up, common) and Divisive (top-down, rare).
- ✅ **Algorithm loop:** Compute distances → Find min pair → Merge → Update matrix → Repeat $N-1$ times.
- ✅ **Four key linkages:** Single (MIN, chains), Complete (MAX, compact), Average (UPGMA, balanced), Ward (variance-minimizing, gold standard).
- ✅ **Lance-Williams recurrence:** $d(C_{ij}, C_k) = \alpha_i d(C_i, C_k) + \alpha_j d(C_j, C_k) + \beta d(C_i, C_j) + \gamma \vert d(C_i, C_k) - d(C_j, C_k) \vert$.
- ✅ **Lance-Williams parameters for single:** $\alpha_i = \alpha_j = 1/2, \beta = 0, \gamma = -1/2$.
- ✅ **Lance-Williams parameters for complete:** $\alpha_i = \alpha_j = 1/2, \beta = 0, \gamma = +1/2$.
- ✅ **Dendrogram =** tree diagram; cut at the *largest vertical gap* to find $k$.
- ✅ **Monotonicity:** merge heights are non-decreasing; the dendrogram never inverts.
- ✅ **AC range:** $[0, 1]$; higher = stronger cluster structure preserved.
- ✅ **Complexity:** Agglomerative = $O(N^3)$ naive, $O(N^2 \log N)$ with heap; Divisive = $O(2^N)$.
- ✅ **No $k$ needed upfront** (unlike K-Means), but $k$ must be chosen post-hoc.
- ✅ **Distance metrics:** Euclidean (most common), Manhattan (robust), Minkowski (generalized), Cosine (for text/vectors).
- ✅ **Sensitive to scale** — always **standardize features** before clustering.
- ✅ **Cophenetic distance $c(x_i, x_j)$** = height of LCA in the dendrogram — used to compute AC.
- ✅ **Ward linkage is preferred** for most numerical KTU problems because it produces interpretable, equal-sized clusters.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DBSCAN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST525 - DATA MINING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DBSCAN

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# DBSCAN — Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic belongs to **Module 3 — Cluster Analysis**, specifically under the heading *"Density-based methods"*. It is a **high-weightage** topic for both Continuous Assessment (CA) and End Semester Examinations (ESE).

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)** is a **density-based clustering algorithm** proposed by *Ester, Kriegel, Sander, and Xu (1996)*. Unlike partitioning methods (e.g., K-Means) or hierarchical methods, DBSCAN does not require the user to specify the number of clusters in advance. Instead, it grows clusters by grouping together points that are **densely packed together**, marking as outliers those points that lie in **low-density regions**.

The algorithm discovers clusters of **arbitrary shape** (globular, elongated, ring-shaped) in spatial databases and is **robust to noise/outliers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** A cluster is a **dense region of points** separated from other dense regions by **low-density (sparse) regions**. Outliers are points that do not belong to any dense region.

## 1.2 The Two Critical Input Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Neighbourhood Radius** | $\varepsilon$ (Eps) | Maximum distance to consider two points as neighbours |
| **Minimum Points** | **MinPts** | Minimum number of points required to form a dense region |

## 1.3 Types of Points in DBSCAN

> [!NOTE]
> DBSCAN classifies every data point into **exactly one** of the following three categories.

| Point Type | Definition | Visual Cue |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Point** | A point $p$ whose $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood contains **at least MinPts** points (including $p$ itself). | A point inside a dense crowd. |
| **Border Point** | A point that is **not a core point**, but falls within the $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood of some core point. | A point on the edge of a dense crowd. |
| **Noise Point (Outlier)** | A point that is neither a core point nor a border point. | A lonely point in a sparse region. |

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The City Party Analogy"

> [!TIP]
> Imagine you are hosting a party in a **big empty field**. People arrive and stand in groups:
> - **Core People** = Anyone who has at least **MinPts** people (including themselves) standing within an **Eps**-radius circle. They form the **"social heart"** of a group.
> - **Border People** = Those who are **near** a core person, but they themselves don't have enough neighbours. They "hug the edge" of a group.
> - **Lone Wanderers** = People who stand so far away from everyone that no one is within their Eps circle. They are the **noise/outliers**.

Just like DBSCAN would say *"all the people standing close enough to a core group belong to the same party, and the loners are noise"*, the algorithm groups dense regions and isolates outliers.

## 1.5 Density-Reachability and Density-Connectivity

These two concepts are the **backbone** of cluster formation in DBSCAN and frequently appear in KTU exams as short-answer questions.

- **Directly Density-Reachable:** A point $q$ is **directly density-reachable** from $p$ if $p$ is a **core point** and $q \in N_{\varepsilon}(p)$.
- **Density-Reachable:** $q$ is density-reachable from $p$ if there exists a **chain** of points $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$ such that $p_1 = p$, $p_n = q$, and each $p_{i+1}$ is directly density-reachable from $p_i$.
- **Density-Connected:** Two points $p$ and $q$ are density-connected if there exists a point $o$ such that both $p$ and $q$ are **density-reachable from $o$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A cluster in DBSCAN is formally defined as the set of all points that are mutually density-connected.** Two points can belong to the same cluster only if they are density-connected (not merely density-reachable, because density-reachability is asymmetric).

## 1.6 Why DBSCAN? — Strengths Over K-Means

| Feature | K-Means | DBSCAN |
|---|---|---|
| Number of clusters $K$ | **Must be specified** | **Automatically determined** |
| Cluster shape | **Spherical/globular only** | **Arbitrary shapes** |
| Handles noise | **Sensitive to outliers** | **Robustly detects outliers** |
| Parameter sensitivity | Moderate | Eps and MinPts selection is critical |
| Time complexity | $O(n \cdot k \cdot t)$ | $O(n \log n)$ with spatial index |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eps-Neighbourhood — Mathematical Foundation

The $\varepsilon$-**neighbourhood** of a point $p$ is the set of all points within radius $\varepsilon$ centred at $p$.

$$
N_{\varepsilon}(p) = \{\, q \in D \mid \mathrm{dist}(p, q) \leq \varepsilon \,\}
$$

where $D$ is the database of points and $\mathrm{dist}(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a chosen distance metric (typically **Euclidean**, sometimes Manhattan or Minkowski).

> [!NOTE]
> **Notice the inequality is $\leq \varepsilon$ (not $< \varepsilon$).** This means a point is *always* in its own neighbourhood. This is why MinPts counts **include the point itself**.

## 2.2 Formal Definition of Point Types

Let $|N_{\varepsilon}(p)|$ denote the **cardinality** (size) of the $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood of $p$.

- **Core Point:** $|N_{\varepsilon}(p)| \geq \text{MinPts}$
- **Border Point:** $|N_{\varepsilon}(p)| < \text{MinPts}$ **AND** $p \in N_{\varepsilon}(q)$ for some core point $q$
- **Noise Point:** $|N_{\varepsilon}(p)| < \text{MinPts}$ **AND** $p$ is not in the $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood of any core point

## 2.3 Core Algorithm — Operational Logic

The DBSCAN algorithm operates as follows:

1. **Initialise:** Mark all points as **unvisited**. Set cluster ID $C = 0$.
2. **Iterate:** Pick any **unvisited** point $p$ from the database.
3. **Mark** $p$ as **visited**.
4. **Compute** the $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood $N_{\varepsilon}(p)$.
   - **If** $|N_{\varepsilon}(p)| < \text{MinPts}$: Temporarily mark $p$ as **noise**. Move to step 2.
   - **Else** (i.e., $p$ is a **core point**):
     - Increment cluster ID: $C = C + 1$.
     - Assign $p$ to cluster $C$.
     - Initialise a **seed set** $S = N_{\varepsilon}(p) \setminus \{p\}$.
     - **Expand cluster:** While $S$ is not empty:
       - Pop a point $q$ from $S$.
       - If $q$ was unvisited: mark visited; compute $N_{\varepsilon}(q)$.
         - If $|N_{\varepsilon}(q)| \geq \text{MinPts}$, add those neighbours to $S$.
       - If $q$ is **not yet assigned** to any cluster, assign $q$ to cluster $C$.
5. **Repeat** from step 2 until all points are visited.
6. **Final noise labelling:** Any point still marked as "noise" but assigned to a cluster (via border-point logic) is confirmed. Remaining noise points are **outliers**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key observation:** A point initially labelled as "noise" in step 4a can later be promoted to a **border point** if it is later found inside a core point's neighbourhood. Only points left as "noise" at the end are true outliers.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table.** All of these expressions are potential short-answer or 7-mark derivation questions in KTU ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood of $p$ | $N_{\varepsilon}(p) = \{\, q \mid \mathrm{dist}(p, q) \leq \varepsilon \,\}$ | $p \in N_{\varepsilon}(p)$ always |
| 2 | Core point condition | $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$ | Includes $p$ itself |
| 3 | Direct density-reachability | $p$ is core $\wedge$ $q \in N_{\varepsilon}(p)$ $\Rightarrow$ $q$ is **directly density-reachable** from $p$ | **Asymmetric** relation |
| 4 | Density-reachability | Chain of directly density-reachable points | **Asymmetric** & **transitive** |
| 5 | Density-connectivity | $\exists\, o : p \leftarrow_{\text{d-reach}} o \rightarrow_{\text{d-reach}} q$ | **Symmetric** relation |
| 6 | Euclidean distance (2D) | $d(p, q) = \sqrt{(x_p - x_q)^2 + (y_p - y_q)^2}$ | Most common in DBSCAN |
| 7 | Manhattan distance | $d(p, q) = \vert x_p - x_q \vert + \vert y_p - y_q \vert$ | Used for high-dim. data |
| 8 | Time complexity (naive) | $O(n^2)$ | Without spatial index |
| 9 | Time complexity (with KD-tree/R-tree) | $O(n \log n)$ | Recommended for large datasets |
| 10 | Space complexity | $O(n)$ | Cluster labels + auxiliary sets |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where DBSCAN is Used

- **Anomaly / Fraud Detection** in banking transactions (e.g., credit-card fraud clusters).
- **Geo-spatial analysis** — identifying hotspots of crime, disease outbreaks, or accident zones.
- **Astronomy** — finding clusters of stars or galaxies amid cosmic noise.
- **Image segmentation** — separating foreground objects from background.
- **Network intrusion detection** — flagging abnormal traffic patterns.
- **Retail analytics** — discovering customer segments of irregular shape in market-basket data.

## 2.6 Choosing Eps and MinPts — Heuristic Rules

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"How do you select the parameters Eps and MinPts in DBSCAN?"*

- **MinPts:** A common rule is $\text{MinPts} \geq d + 1$, where $d$ is the dimensionality. For 2D data, $\text{MinPts} = 4$ is a typical starting point. For noisy data, use **larger** MinPts.
- **Eps:** Plot the **k-distance graph** (distance to the $k$-th nearest neighbour for every point, sorted in descending order). The point of **maximum curvature (the "elbow")** gives a good Eps value, where $k = \text{MinPts}$.

# Step-by-Step Worked Example & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Sample Dataset

Consider the following 2D dataset of 9 points. We will run DBSCAN with $\varepsilon = 1.5$ and $\text{MinPts} = 3$.

| Point | Coordinates $(x, y)$ |
|---|---|
| $A$ | $(1, 1)$ |
| $B$ | $(1, 2)$ |
| $C$ | $(2, 1)$ |
| $D$ | $(2, 2)$ |
| $E$ | $(3, 1)$ |
| $F$ | $(8, 8)$ |
| $G$ | $(8, 9)$ |
| $H$ | $(9, 8)$ |
| $I$ | $(15, 15)$ |

## 3.2 Step 1 — Compute Pairwise Euclidean Distances

Using $d(p, q) = \sqrt{(x_p - x_q)^2 + (y_p - y_q)^2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(A, B) &= \sqrt{(1-1)^2 + (1-2)^2} = 1.00 \\
d(A, C) &= \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (1-1)^2} = 1.00 \\
d(A, D) &= \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (1-2)^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \\
d(A, E) &= \sqrt{(1-3)^2 + (1-1)^2} = 2.00 \\
d(B, C) &= \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (2-1)^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \\
d(B, D) &= \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (2-2)^2} = 1.00 \\
d(B, E) &= \sqrt{(1-3)^2 + (2-1)^2} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \\
d(C, D) &= \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (1-2)^2} = 1.00 \\
d(C, E) &= \sqrt{(2-3)^2 + (1-1)^2} = 1.00 \\
d(D, E) &= \sqrt{(2-3)^2 + (2-1)^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Step 2 — Determine $\varepsilon$-Neighbourhoods

For $\varepsilon = 1.5$, we collect all points (including self) within distance $\leq 1.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{1.5}(A) &= \{A, B, C, D\} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert N_{1.5}(A) \vert &= 4 \\
N_{1.5}(B) &= \{A, B, C, D\} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert N_{1.5}(B) \vert &= 4 \\
N_{1.5}(C) &= \{A, B, C, D, E\} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert N_{1.5}(C) \vert &= 5 \\
N_{1.5}(D) &= \{A, B, C, D, E\} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert N_{1.5}(D) \vert &= 5 \\
N_{1.5}(E) &= \{C, D, E\} &\quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert N_{1.5}(E) \vert &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Step 3 — Classify Each Point (MinPts = 3)

Since all five points $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ satisfy $\vert N_{1.5}(p) \vert \geq 3$, **all five are Core Points**.

Now consider the second group $\{F, G, H\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(F, G) &= 1.00, \quad d(F, H) = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414, \quad d(G, H) = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
N_{1.5}(F) = \{F, G, H\}, \quad N_{1.5}(G) = \{F, G, H\}, \quad N_{1.5}(H) = \{F, G, H\}
$$

All three have neighbourhood size $3 \geq \text{MinPts}$, hence **all are Core Points**.

For the lone point $I = (15, 15)$:

$$
d(F, I) = \sqrt{(8-15)^2 + (8-15)^2} = \sqrt{98} \approx 9.90 > 1.5
$$

Since no point is within $1.5$ of $I$, we get:

$$
N_{1.5}(I) = \{I\}, \quad \vert N_{1.5}(I) \vert = 1 < 3
$$

Hence $I$ is **not a core point** and is not within any core point's neighbourhood. Therefore, $I$ is a **Noise Point (Outlier)**.

## 3.5 Step 4 — Cluster Expansion & Final Labelling

**Cluster 1 (C1):** Starting from $A$ (a core point), the algorithm expands the cluster by recursively including all density-reachable points. Since $A \leftrightarrow B \leftrightarrow C \leftrightarrow D \leftrightarrow E$ are all mutually density-connected, the cluster becomes:

$$
C_1 = \{A, B, C, D, E\}
$$

**Cluster 2 (C2):** Starting from $F$ (a core point), expanding similarly:

$$
C_2 = \{F, G, H\}
$$

**Noise:** $I$ remains unassigned.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final DBSCAN Output:** 2 clusters $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ and $\{F, G, H\}$ + 1 noise point $\{I\}$.

## 3.6 Complete Analytical Matrix (Summary)

| Point | $N_{1.5}(p)$ | $\vert N_{1.5}(p) \vert$ | $\geq$ MinPts ? | Type | Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A(1,1)$ | $\{A, B, C, D\}$ | 4 | Yes | Core | C1 |
| $B(1,2)$ | $\{A, B, C, D\}$ | 4 | Yes | Core | C1 |
| $C(2,1)$ | $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ | 5 | Yes | Core | C1 |
| $D(2,2)$ | $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ | 5 | Yes | Core | C1 |
| $E(3,1)$ | $\{C, D, E\}$ | 3 | Yes | Core | C1 |
| $F(8,8)$ | $\{F, G, H\}$ | 3 | Yes | Core | C2 |
| $G(8,9)$ | $\{F, G, H\}$ | 3 | Yes | Core | C2 |
| $H(9,8)$ | $\{F, G, H\}$ | 3 | Yes | Core | C2 |
| $I(15,15)$ | $\{I\}$ | 1 | No | Noise | — |

## 3.7 A Counter-Example — When Border Points Appear

> [!TIP]
> To see how a **border point** emerges, add a point $J = (3, 2)$ to the dataset. With $\varepsilon = 1.5$, $\text{MinPts} = 3$:
>
> $N_{1.5}(J) = \{D, E, J\}$ — only 3 points, so $J$ is a **core point** (borderline). If instead $\text{MinPts} = 4$, then $\vert N_{1.5}(J) \vert = 3 < 4$, so $J$ would be a **border point** (it lies inside the neighbourhood of $D$ and $E$, which are core). This illustrates how changing MinPts converts core points to border points.

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DBSCAN Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start DBSCAN]) --> initA[Mark all points as UNVISITED]
    initA --> loopA{Any unvisited point p?}
    loopA -- No --> endA([End: Output clusters + noise])
    loopA -- Yes --> visitA[Mark p as VISITED]
    visitA --> neighA[Compute N_eps of p]
    neighA --> checkA{N_eighborhood size >= MinPts?}
    checkA -- No --> noiseMarkA[Temporarily mark p as NOISE]
    noiseMarkA --> loopA
    checkA -- Yes --> newClustA[Increment cluster ID: C = C + 1]
    newClustA --> assignA[Assign p to Cluster C]
    assignA --> seedA[Initialize seed set S = N_eps minus p]
    seedA --> seedLoopA{Is S empty?}
    seedLoopA -- Yes --> loopA
    seedLoopA -- No --> popA[Pop point q from S]
    popA --> checkQA{Was q unvisited?}
    checkQA -- Yes --> markQA[Mark q as VISITED]
    markQA --> neighQA[Compute N_eps of q]
    neighQA --> checkCoreA{N_eps of q size >= MinPts?}
    checkCoreA -- Yes --> addSeedA[Add N_eps of q to seed set S]
    checkCoreA -- No --> skipA[Skip adding neighbours]
    addSeedA --> checkAssignA
    skipA --> checkAssignA
    markQA --> checkAssignA
    checkQA -- No --> checkAssignA{q already assigned to a cluster?}
    checkAssignA -- No --> assignQA[Assign q to Cluster C]
    checkAssignA -- Yes --> nextA[Leave q unchanged]
    assignQA --> seedLoopA
    nextA --> seedLoopA
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — DBSCAN Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputA["INPUT LAYER"]
        rawA["Raw 2D / Multi-D Point Cloud"]
        epsA["Parameter: Eps (radius)"]
        minA["Parameter: MinPts (threshold)"]
    end

    subgraph processA["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        neighComputeA["Eps-Neighbourhood Engine"]
        pointTypeA["Point Type Classifier"]
        subgraph classifyA["Classifier Sub-Units"]
            coreA["Core Point Detector"]
            borderA["Border Point Detector"]
            noiseA["Noise / Outlier Detector"]
        end
        expandA["Cluster Expansion Module"]
    end

    subgraph outputA["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        clusterListA["Set of Clusters: C1, C2, ..., Ck"]
        noiseListA["Set of Noise Points"]
    end

    rawA --> neighComputeA
    epsA --> neighComputeA
    minA --> pointTypeA
    neighComputeA --> pointTypeA
    pointTypeA --> coreA
    pointTypeA --> borderA
    pointTypeA --> noiseA
    coreA --> expandA
    borderA --> expandA
    expandA --> clusterListA
    noiseA --> noiseListA
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the terms **Core Point**, **Border Point**, and **Noise Point** in DBSCAN. Use the parameters $\varepsilon$ and MinPts in your definitions.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Core Point:** A point $p$ is a *core point* if its $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood contains **at least MinPts** points, i.e., $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$. Core points lie in the interior of a dense region. **(1 mark)**
- **Border Point:** A point $p$ is a *border point* if it is **not** a core point ($\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert < \text{MinPts}$), but it lies **within the $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood of some core point**. Border points form the outer edge of a cluster. **(1 mark)**
- **Noise Point (Outlier):** A point that is **neither a core point nor a border point** — meaning it has fewer than MinPts points in its $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood and is not in the neighbourhood of any core point. **(1 mark)**

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **direct density-reachability** and **density-connectivity** in DBSCAN. Why is the latter required for cluster definition?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Direct Density-Reachability:** A point $q$ is *directly density-reachable* from $p$ if $p$ is a **core point** **and** $q \in N_{\varepsilon}(p)$. This relation is **asymmetric** (i.e., if $q$ is directly density-reachable from $p$, it does **not** imply $p$ is directly density-reachable from $q$ unless $q$ is also a core point). **(1.5 marks)**
- **Density-Connectivity:** Two points $p$ and $q$ are *density-connected* if there exists a point $o$ such that both $p$ and $q$ are **density-reachable** from $o$. This relation is **symmetric** and **reflexive**. **(1 mark)**
- **Why it matters:** Density-reachability alone is asymmetric, so it cannot be used directly to say *"both points belong to the same cluster"*. **A DBSCAN cluster is the maximal set of density-connected points**, ensuring the relation is symmetric and well-defined. **(0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice)

> Each question choice contains two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

### 5.2.1 Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q.A.** **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Adapted]** *(a)* With a suitable diagram, explain the architecture and working of the **DBSCAN clustering algorithm**. Discuss the role of the parameters $\varepsilon$ and MinPts. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* For the dataset below, apply DBSCAN with $\varepsilon = 1.5$ and $\text{MinPts} = 3$. Show the **neighbourhood computation**, classify each point as core / border / noise, and state the final clusters. *(7 marks)*

| Point | $x$ | $y$ |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 1 | 1 |
| P2 | 1 | 2 |
| P3 | 2 | 2 |
| P4 | 8 | 8 |
| P5 | 8 | 9 |
| P6 | 9 | 8 |
| P7 | 15 | 15 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture & Working of DBSCAN — 7 marks**

DBSCAN is a **density-based** clustering algorithm. Its key principle is that **a cluster is a dense region of points separated by sparse regions**.

**Architecture (Block-Level):**

1. **Input Module** — Receives the dataset $D$ and the two parameters $\varepsilon$ and MinPts.
2. **Neighbourhood Computation Engine** — For each point, computes the set of points within $\varepsilon$ distance (using a spatial index such as KD-tree for efficiency).
3. **Point Classifier** — Classifies every point as **core**, **border**, or **noise** based on the size of its neighbourhood relative to MinPts.
4. **Cluster Expansion Module** — Starts from a core point and iteratively adds all density-reachable points to the same cluster.
5. **Output Module** — Returns the set of clusters and the set of noise points.

**Working (Algorithm Steps):**

1. Mark all points as unvisited.
2. Pick an arbitrary unvisited point $p$.
3. Mark $p$ as visited and compute $N_{\varepsilon}(p)$.
4. If $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert < \text{MinPts}$: mark $p$ as noise (temporarily).
5. Else: create a new cluster, add $p$ to it. Let $S = N_{\varepsilon}(p) \setminus \{p\}$.
6. While $S$ is not empty: pop $q$ from $S$. If $q$ is unvisited, mark and compute $N_{\varepsilon}(q)$. If $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(q) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$, add its neighbours to $S$. Assign $q$ to current cluster if unassigned.
7. Repeat from step 2 until all points visited.
8. Any point still marked as "noise" is a final outlier.

**Role of Parameters:**

- **$\varepsilon$ (Eps):** Defines the **radius of the neighbourhood**. Too small → many small clusters and noise. Too large → everything merges into one cluster.
- **MinPts:** Defines the **minimum cluster density**. Higher MinPts → more robust to noise but may miss smaller clusters. A common rule is $\text{MinPts} \geq d + 1$ where $d$ is the dimensionality.

**Diagram (to be drawn in exam):** Draw 2D scatter showing core points (filled circles), border points (hollow circles) inside their $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood, and noise points (crosses) far away, with $\varepsilon$-circles drawn around core points.

**(b) Worked Example — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Pairwise Distance Computation (using Euclidean distance)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(P_1, P_2) &= \sqrt{(1-1)^2 + (1-2)^2} = 1.00 \\
d(P_1, P_3) &= \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (1-2)^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41 \\
d(P_2, P_3) &= \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (2-2)^2} = 1.00 \\
d(P_4, P_5) &= 1.00, \quad d(P_4, P_6) = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41, \quad d(P_5, P_6) = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41 \\
d(P_4, P_7) &= \sqrt{98} \approx 9.90 \\
d(P_1, P_4) &= \sqrt{98} \approx 9.90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: $\varepsilon$-Neighbourhoods (with $\varepsilon = 1.5$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{1.5}(P_1) &= \{P_1, P_2, P_3\}, \quad \vert N_{1.5}(P_1) \vert = 3 \\
N_{1.5}(P_2) &= \{P_1, P_2, P_3\}, \quad \vert N_{1.5}(P_2) \vert = 3 \\
N_{1.5}(P_3) &= \{P_1, P_2, P_3\}, \quad \vert N_{1.5}(P_3) \vert = 3 \\
N_{1.5}(P_4) &= \{P_4, P_5, P_6\}, \quad \vert N_{1.5}(P_4) \vert = 3 \\
N_{1.5}(P_5) &= \{P_4, P_5, P_6\}, \quad \vert N_{1.5}(P_5) \vert = 3 \\
N_{1.5}(P_6) &= \{P_4, P_5, P_6\}, \quad \vert N_{1.5}(P_6) \vert = 3 \\
N_{1.5}(P_7) &= \{P_7\}, \quad \vert N_{1.5}(P_7) \vert = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Classification (MinPts = 3)**

| Point | $\vert N_{1.5}(p) \vert$ | $\geq 3$? | Type | Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 3 | Yes | Core | C1 |
| P2 | 3 | Yes | Core | C1 |
| P3 | 3 | Yes | Core | C1 |
| P4 | 3 | Yes | Core | C2 |
| P5 | 3 | Yes | Core | C2 |
| P6 | 3 | Yes | Core | C2 |
| P7 | 1 | No | Noise | — |

**Step 4: Final Output**

$$
\text{Cluster 1 } (C_1) = \{P_1, P_2, P_3\}, \quad \text{Cluster 2 } (C_2) = \{P_4, P_5, P_6\}, \quad \text{Noise} = \{P_7\}
$$

### 5.2.2 Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

> **Q.B.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]** *(a)* Explain the concepts of **density-reachability** and **density-connectivity** in DBSCAN with proper definitions. Why is density-connectivity preferred for cluster definition? *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Compare and contrast **DBSCAN** and **K-Means** clustering algorithms under the heads: (i) number of clusters, (ii) cluster shape, (iii) handling of noise, (iv) parameter requirement, (v) scalability, (vi) typical use case. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Density-Reachability and Density-Connectivity — 7 marks**

**Definition 1 — Direct Density-Reachability:** A point $q$ is **directly density-reachable** from a point $p$ if:
1. $p$ is a **core point**, **and**
2. $q \in N_{\varepsilon}(p)$, i.e., $q$ lies within the $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood of $p$.

**Definition 2 — Density-Reachability:** A point $q$ is **density-reachable** from $p$ if there exists a finite chain of points $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$ where $p_1 = p$, $p_n = q$, and each $p_{i+1}$ is **directly density-reachable** from $p_i$. *(3 marks)*

**Definition 3 — Density-Connectivity:** A point $q$ is **density-connected** to $p$ if there exists a point $o$ such that both $p$ and $q$ are **density-reachable** from $o$. *(2 marks)*

**Why Density-Connectivity is Preferred for Cluster Definition:** Density-reachability is an **asymmetric** relation (a border point can be density-reachable from a core point, but not vice-versa). Therefore, two points $p$ and $q$ in the same cluster may not be density-reachable from one another. To make cluster membership a **symmetric** relation (so that "if $p$ is in cluster $C$ and $q$ is in cluster $C$, they are in the same cluster"), DBSCAN defines a cluster as the **set of all points mutually density-connected**. *(2 marks)*

**(b) Comparison of DBSCAN and K-Means — 7 marks**

| # | Criterion | K-Means | DBSCAN |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Number of clusters** | Must be pre-specified ($K$) | Automatically determined by the algorithm |
| 2 | **Cluster shape** | Assumes spherical / globular clusters of similar size | Detects clusters of **arbitrary shape** |
| 3 | **Handling of noise** | Sensitive to outliers; outliers distort centroids | **Explicitly identifies and labels noise** |
| 4 | **Parameter requirement** | $K$ (number of clusters), initial centroids, distance metric | $\varepsilon$ (Eps) and MinPts |
| 5 | **Scalability** | $O(n \cdot k \cdot t)$ — efficient for large $n$ | $O(n^2)$ naive; $O(n \log n)$ with spatial indexing |
| 6 | **Typical use case** | Customer segmentation with known $K$, vector quantization | Anomaly detection, spatial data, irregular cluster shapes |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in DBSCAN questions (leading to mark deductions):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include the point itself in its $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood.** The relation is $\mathrm{dist}(p, p) = 0 \leq \varepsilon$, so $p \in N_{\varepsilon}(p)$. Always count the point itself. *(−1 mark if missed)*
> 2. **Using strict inequality $<$ instead of $\leq$ in the neighbourhood definition.** The correct condition is $\mathrm{dist}(p, q) \leq \varepsilon$, not $< \varepsilon$. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Confusing density-reachability with density-connectivity.** Density-reachability is **asymmetric**; density-connectivity is **symmetric**. Clusters are defined by the latter, not the former. *(−2 marks if swapped in long answer)*
> 4. **Failing to convert a temporarily-marked "noise" point to a border point** if it is later found inside a core point's neighbourhood during cluster expansion. *(−1 mark)*
> 5. **Not specifying the distance metric** (Euclidean vs. Manhattan) when computing neighbourhoods — KTU examiners expect an explicit mention.
> 6. **Writing $K$-Means answers for DBSCAN** or vice versa in the comparison question. Keep the columns strictly to the algorithm being discussed.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DBSCAN (Module 3, PECST525)**

- **DBSCAN = Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise.** Density-based, deterministic (given parameter order), single-pass.
- **Two parameters:** $\varepsilon$ (Eps — radius) and MinPts (minimum neighbour count).
- **$\varepsilon$-Neighbourhood:** $N_{\varepsilon}(p) = \{\, q \in D \mid \mathrm{dist}(p, q) \leq \varepsilon \,\}$ — includes $p$ itself.
- **Core Point:** $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$.
- **Border Point:** $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert < \text{MinPts}$ but $p$ lies in the $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood of a core point.
- **Noise Point:** Neither core nor border — the final outliers.
- **Direct Density-Reachability** is **asymmetric**; requires the source to be a **core** point.
- **Density-Connectivity** is **symmetric**; defined as two points both density-reachable from a common point.
- **A cluster in DBSCAN = maximal set of density-connected points.**
- **DBSCAN advantages:** Arbitrary-shaped clusters, automatic $K$, identifies noise, deterministic.
- **DBSCAN disadvantages:** Sensitive to $\varepsilon$ and MinPts; struggles with **varying densities** across clusters; performance degrades in very high dimensions.
- **Time complexity:** $O(n^2)$ naive; $O(n \log n)$ with KD-tree / R-tree spatial index.
- **Parameter selection heuristics:** $\text{MinPts} \geq d + 1$ (where $d$ = dimensionality); use the **k-distance graph elbow method** to pick $\varepsilon$.
- **Comparison mantra:** K-Means needs $K$ & is spherical; DBSCAN needs $\varepsilon$/MinPts & is shape-agnostic.
- **Typical distance metric:** Euclidean (Minkowski $p=2$); alternative is Manhattan for sparse high-dim data.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Association Rule Mining - Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST525 - DATA MINING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Association Rule Mining - Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Association Rule Mining - Concepts

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Association Rule Mining (ARM)** is a fundamental unsupervised data mining technique that uncovers interesting, hidden relationships, correlations, frequent patterns, or causal structures among sets of items (or variables) in large transactional or relational databases. Formally introduced by Agrawal, Imieliński, and Swami (1993) in the context of market basket analysis, ARM identifies strong rules discovered in databases using different measures of *interestingness*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):** Association rule mining is the process of finding all co-occurrence relationships, called associations, among items in a dataset. It is a rule-based machine learning method used for discovering interesting relations between variables in large databases.

An **Association Rule** is formally expressed as an implication of the form:

$$ X \Rightarrow Y $$

where $X$ and $Y$ are disjoint non-empty item sets (i.e., $X \cap Y = \emptyset$), and $X$ is called the *antecedent* (Left-Hand Side, LHS) while $Y$ is the *consequent* (Right-Hand Side, RHS). The rule essentially states: *"If a transaction contains items in $X$, it is also likely to contain items in $Y$."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a supermarket manager who wants to know the purchasing behavior of customers:

> 🍎 **Intuitive Analogy: The Grocery Store Story**
> 
> A supermarket manager observes that customers who buy **bread** and **butter** often also buy **jam** together. By analyzing millions of receipts (transactions), the manager can deduce:
> - "If a customer puts bread in their cart, they are 80% likely to also buy butter."
> - "On weekends, when people buy beer, they also tend to buy diapers (the famous 'beer and diapers' story)."
> 
> **ARM is the automated, mathematical tool** that scans all such transactions to find *all* such patterns, ranks them by strength, and presents them as actionable rules.

**Transaction Database ($D$):** A dataset of $N$ transactions, where each transaction is a set of items purchased together by one customer.

**Itemset ($I$):** A collection of one or more items. A $k$-itemset contains exactly $k$ items.

**Itemset Frequency (Count):** The number of transactions in $D$ that contain a particular itemset.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Frequency)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Itemset** | A set containing one or more items |
| **k-Itemset** | An itemset containing exactly $k$ items |
| **Support Count ($\sigma$)** | Number of transactions containing the itemset |
| **Frequent Itemset** | An itemset whose support is ≥ a user-defined `min_sup` threshold |
| **Strong Rule** | A rule whose support and confidence satisfy `min_sup` and `min_conf` |
| **Antecedent (LHS)** | The "if" part of the rule ($X$) |
| **Consequent (RHS)** | The "then" part of the rule ($Y$) |
| **Transactional Database** | Collection of transactions, each a set of items |

> [!NOTE]
