# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q. Define Pattern Analysis. Why is it considered a necessary post-mining step? List any two limitations of the support–confidence framework.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU key style):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Pattern Analysis is the process of evaluating, filtering, compressing, and applying patterns produced by a data-mining algorithm to extract those that are truly *interesting, non-redundant, useful, and actionable* for the end user.
>
> **Necessity (1 mark):** Raw mining produces an overwhelming number of patterns, many of which are *frequent yet uninteresting* (the Brin et al. counter-example shows that a 66.7% confidence rule can be negatively correlated). Pattern Analysis separates signal from noise using correlation, novelty, and constraint-based lenses.
>
> **Two Limitations of Support–Confidence (1 mark):**
> 1. **Confidence is not correlation-invariant** — high confidence can arise from negatively correlated items.
> 2. **Rare items are systematically ignored** — a low-support pattern may still be highly profitable (e.g., diamond rings) and is discarded by the support threshold.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q. Differentiate between a *closed frequent itemset* and a *maximal frequent itemset* with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Closed Frequent Itemset | Maximal Frequent Itemset |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A frequent itemset with no proper super-itemset having the same support. | A frequent itemset with no proper super-itemset that is frequent. |
| **Support Information** | Lossless — subset supports can be reconstructed. | Lossy — only the itemset's own support is known. |
| **Cardinality** | Larger than maximal set. | Smallest possible compressed representation. |
| **Example** | $\{A, B, C\}$ with $\text{sup} = 30$, and no super-set of $\{A,B,C\}$ has support 30. | $\{A, B, C\}$ with $\text{sup} = 30$ is *maximal* if no 4-itemset superset is frequent. |

> If $\{A, B, C\}$ is closed and $\{A, B, C, D\}$ does not exist or is not frequent, then $\{A, B, C\}$ is **both** closed and maximal.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the *limitation of confidence* as an interestingness measure. With a suitable example, show how a high-confidence rule can be misleading. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define *Lift, Conviction, Leverage, and Kulczynski* measures. Using the contingency table below (database of $N = 1000$ transactions), compute all four measures for the rule $A \Rightarrow B$ and comment on the correlation. **(7 marks)**

|  | $B$ | $\neg B$ | Row Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | 400 | 100 | 500 |
| $\neg A$ | 200 | 300 | 500 |
| **Col Total** | 600 | 400 | 1000 |

#### Model Solution — Part (a) **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — The Limitation of Confidence:**
Confidence is a **directional** and **non-correlation-aware** measure. It only checks whether $B$ appears frequently when $A$ is present. It does *not* check whether $B$ appears *less often* when $A$ is present than under statistical independence. Hence, a rule with confidence 66.7% can still exhibit **negative correlation**.

**Step 2 (3 marks) — The Brin et al. Counter-Example:**
Consider 10,000 transactions.
- $\text{sup}(A) = 6000$, $\text{sup}(B) = 7500$, $\text{sup}(A \cup B) = 4000$.
- Confidence$(A \Rightarrow B) = 4000/6000 = 66.7\%$.
- Expected support under independence: $\dfrac{6000 \times 7500}{10000} = 4500$.
- Observed 4000 < expected 4500 ⇒ **negative correlation**.

The rule "sounds" strong (66.7%) but actually $A$ and $B$ *avoid each other* compared to chance. The retailer would *mis-stock* by trusting this rule.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Conclusion:**
Confidence must always be supplemented with a **symmetric correlation measure** (Lift, $\chi^2$, Conviction, Kulczynski). High confidence $\neq$ high interest.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) **(7 marks)**

Given: $N = 1000$, $\text{sup}(A) = 500$, $\text{sup}(B) = 600$, $\text{sup}(A \cup B) = 400$.

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Lift:**

$$ \text{Lift} = \frac{N \cdot \text{sup}(A \cup B)}{\text{sup}(A) \cdot \text{sup}(B)} = \frac{1000 \cdot 400}{500 \cdot 600} = \frac{400{,}000}{300{,}000} = 1.333 $$

> Lift $= 1.33 > 1$ ⇒ positively correlated.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Conviction:**

$$ \text{sup}(\neg B) = 1000 - 600 = 400 $$

$$ \text{Conviction} = \frac{\text{sup}(A) \cdot \text{sup}(\neg B)}{\text{sup}(A) - \text{sup}(A \cup B)} = \frac{500 \cdot 400}{500 - 400} = \frac{200{,}000}{100} = 2000 $$

> Conviction $= 2000 \gg 1$ ⇒ extremely strong rule.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Leverage:**

$$ \text{Leverage} = \text{sup}(A \cup B) - \frac{\text{sup}(A) \cdot \text{sup}(B)}{N} = 400 - \frac{500 \cdot 600}{1000} = 400 - 300 = 100 $$

> Leverage $= +100$ (max possible is 250). Positive ⇒ positive correlation.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Kulczynski:**

$$ \text{Kulc} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\text{sup}(A \cup B)}{\text{sup}(A)} + \frac{\text{sup}(A \cup B)}{\text{sup}(B)}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{400}{500} + \frac{400}{600}\right) $$

$$ \text{Kulc} = \frac{1}{2}(0.800 + 0.667) = \frac{1}{2}(1.467) = 0.733 $$

> Kulczynski $= 0.733 > 0.5$ ⇒ strong positive correlation.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Final Comment:**
All four measures unanimously say $A$ and $B$ are *positively correlated*. The rule $A \Rightarrow B$ is **interesting** and should be retained for downstream use. Note that Kulczynski is the *only* one bounded in $[0,1]$; Conviction is unbounded and depends heavily on $\text{sup}(A)$.

---

### **Question B (Alternative for Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **five-stage pattern analysis funnel**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are **anti-monotone, monotone, succinct, and convertible** constraints? Show, with an example, how an *anti-monotone constraint* can be pushed deep into the Apriori algorithm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) **(7 marks)**

**Stage 1 (1.5 marks) — Raw Pattern Generation:** Mining algorithms (Apriori, FP-Growth) produce a candidate set $\mathcal{C}$ of size $10^4$–$10^7$. This set is the *input* to Pattern Analysis.

**Stage 2 (1.5 marks) — Objective Filtering:** Apply support, lift, $\chi^2$ to prune statistically uninteresting patterns. The Support–Confidence trap is avoided by using correlation-aware measures.

**Stage 3 (1.5 marks) — Constraint Pushing:** Apply user-specified constraints (price ranges, taxonomy levels, regions). Anti-monotone constraints are pushed *before* candidate generation to prune the search space exponentially.

**Stage 4 (1.5 marks) — Compression:** Replace the pattern set by its **closed** or **maximal** representatives. Closed patterns preserve subset support losslessly; maximal patterns provide the smallest representation.

**Stage 5 (1 mark) — Subjective Filtering & Downstream Use:** Apply novelty, actionability, and utility measures. Surviving patterns feed classification (CBA, CMAR) or clustering (subspace, co-clustering) systems.

> **Diagram (refer Section 4.1):** The funnel starts with $\sim 10^6$ candidates and ends with $\sim 10^1$–$10^2$ truly interesting patterns.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) **(7 marks)**

**Constraint Taxonomy (3 marks):**

| Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Anti-monotone** | If a set $S$ violates it, every superset $T \supseteq S$ also violates it. | $\text{avg}(S.\text{price}) \le 100$ |
| **Monotone** | If $S$ satisfies it, every superset also satisfies it. | $\text{sum}(S.\text{price}) \ge 500$ |
| **Succinct** | The constraint can be expressed as a selection on the item domain. | $\text{price} \in [100, 500]$ |
| **Convertible** | A constraint that becomes anti-monotone (or monotone) after a *value-reordering* of items. | $\text{sum}(S.\text{price}) \le 500$ when items are sorted ascending. |

**Pushing Anti-Monotone Constraints (4 marks):**

Consider the constraint $\text{avg}(S.\text{price}) \le 100$. This is **anti-monotone**: if a set $S$ has average price $> 100$, then any superset $T \supseteq S$ also has average price $> 100$ (since adding items can only keep or raise the average).

**Modified Apriori — Gen-Candidates Procedure:**

```
Input: Frequent (k-1)-itemsets L(k-1), anti-monotone constraint C
Output: Candidate k-itemsets Ck
1. For each pair (X, Y) in L(k-1):
2.     Z = X ∪ Y   (Apriori join)
3.     If avg( Z.price ) <= 100:    // PUSH C HERE
4.         Add Z to Ck
5.     Else:
6.         Discard Z and ALL its supersets (prune)
7. For each Z in Ck:
8.     If any (k-1)-subset of Z is NOT in L(k-1):
9.         Remove Z from Ck     (Apriori property prune)
10. Return Ck
```

**Effect (1 mark):** The early `If avg(...) <= 100` check (line 3) prunes *the entire upper lattice* rooted at $Z$ before any subset generation. This is called **constraint-pushing-deep** and reduces the candidate set by orders of magnitude.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B Pattern Analysis**
> 1. **Do not** write "Lift = 0 means no pattern". Lift = 0 means the two items *never co-occur*. The correct interpretation of *no correlation* is **Lift = 1**.
> 2. **Do not** confuse Conviction's range with Confidence's range. Conviction is **unbounded above**; Confidence is bounded in $[0,1]$.
> 3. **Do not** state that *Confidence $\Rightarrow$ Lift* or vice versa — they are *independent* measures. The Brin counter-example proves this.
> 4. **Do not** forget to multiply by $N$ in the Lift formula. A common error is writing $\text{Lift} = \text{sup}(A \cup B)/(\text{sup}(A)\,\text{sup}(B))$ without the $N$ factor.
> 5. **Do not** mark an itemset as "maximal" merely because it is closed. Every maximal frequent itemset is closed, but not every closed itemset is maximal.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Pattern Analysis** is a *post-mining* intelligence layer, not a re-run of mining.
- ✅ The **Support–Confidence framework is insufficient**; always pair it with a **symmetric correlation measure** (Lift, $\chi^2$, Kulczynski, Cosine).
- ✅ **Lift > 1** ⇒ positive correlation; **Lift = 1** ⇒ independence; **Lift < 1** ⇒ negative correlation.
- ✅ **Confidence** is **directional** (rule $A \Rightarrow B$); **Lift and Kulczynski** are **symmetric** (the *pair* $A,B$).
- ✅ **Conviction is unbounded** and tends to infinity for a perfect rule; **Confidence is bounded** in $[0,1]$.
- ✅ **$\chi^2 > 3.841$** (df = 1, $\alpha = 0.05$) ⇒ reject independence. The 3.841 threshold is a KTU favourite.
- ✅ **Closed itemset** = no super-set has the same support (lossless compression).
- ✅ **Maximal itemset** = no super-set is frequent (lossy compression, smallest).
- ✅ **Anti-monotone constraints** can be pushed *deep* into Apriori at candidate-generation time for exponential speed-up.
- ✅ **Succinct constraints** eliminate candidate generation entirely (selection-only).
- ✅ **Pattern-Based Classification** uses discriminative frequent patterns as features (CBA, CMAR, CPAR algorithms).
- ✅ **Pattern-Based Clustering** uses subspace co-occurrence patterns to group similar objects.
- ✅ **Subjective measures** (novelty, actionability, utility) are user-dependent and cannot be computed from data alone.
- ✅ **Rare patterns** are often more valuable than frequent ones (fraud, rare diseases, luxury items) and require special mining techniques.
- ✅ **Leverage range** is $[-0.25, +0.25]$ — not $[0,1]$. A common KTU pitfall.
- ✅ **Cosine measure** is the *geometric* mean of the two directional confidences (Kulczynski is the *arithmetic* mean).
- ✅ **All-confidence $\ge$ Max-confidence** $\ge$ Kulczynski $\ge$ Cosine is **NOT** a valid inequality — the four measures are *unrelated* in general.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Text Mining - Text Data Analysis and Information Retrieval
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST525 - DATA MINING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Text Mining - Text Data Analysis and Information Retrieval

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Text Mining - Text Data Analysis and Information Retrieval

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Text Mining (TM)
**Text Mining** is the automated process of deriving high-quality, non-trivial, and previously unknown information from unstructured textual data. It is a sub-field of data mining that applies Natural Language Processing (NLP), statistical analysis, and machine learning techniques to extract patterns, trends, and relationships from large volumes of text.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Text Mining is the process of distilling actionable knowledge from unstructured text. It is analogous to data mining but operates on free-form linguistic data rather than structured databases."

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a librarian who has to read **one million books** overnight to find every mention of "climate change solutions." A human cannot do this, but text mining acts like a **super-powered speed-reader with a highlighter and a notebook** — it reads everything, highlights relevant phrases, summarizes them, and organizes findings into a clean report by morning.

---

### 1.2 Text Data Analysis (TDA)
**Text Data Analysis** is the systematic computational examination of textual content to identify linguistic patterns, frequency distributions, sentiment, topics, and semantic relationships. It encompasses preprocessing (tokenization, stop-word removal, stemming), feature extraction (bag-of-words, n-grams), and statistical analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Sub-Tasks of Text Data Analysis:**
> - **Tokenization** — splitting text into words/sentences
> - **Stop-word Removal** — discarding common words (the, is, at)
> - **Stemming / Lemmatization** — reducing words to root form
> - **Term Frequency (TF) Analysis** — counting word importance
> - **Sentiment Analysis** — detecting positive/negative tone

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of analyzing a pile of 10,000 customer reviews for a restaurant. Text Data Analysis is like a **detective scanning the pile** to count how many times "rude staff" or "delicious food" appears, tracking word patterns, and ultimately writing a summary report of customer sentiment.

---

### 1.3 Information Retrieval (IR)
**Information Retrieval (IR)** is the science of searching for information within a large repository of documents, databases, or the web. It involves representing, storing, and retrieving textual data based on user queries. The most common example is a **search engine** like Google.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Manning, Raghavan, Schütze lineage):**
> "Information Retrieval (IR) is finding material (usually documents) of an unstructured nature (usually text) that satisfies an information need from within large collections."

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a vast library with **millions of unlabelled books**. Information Retrieval is the act of running to the librarian and asking, "Give me everything on quantum physics written after 2015" — and the librarian instantly produces the exact shelf, books, and pages. IR systems are that **digital librarian**.

---

### 1.4 Relationship between Text Mining, TDA, and IR

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Relationship:**
> - **Information Retrieval (IR)** → *finds relevant documents* (input gathering)
> - **Text Data Analysis (TDA)** → *processes and analyzes text features* (intermediate step)
> - **Text Mining (TM)** → *discovers hidden patterns and knowledge* (final output)

**Key Distinction Table:**

| Aspect | Information Retrieval | Text Mining |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Locate relevant documents | Discover new knowledge/patterns |
| **Input** | User query | Whole document collection |
| **Output** | Ranked list of documents | Patterns, summaries, trends |
| **Complexity** | Comparatively simpler | Computationally intensive |
| **Example** | Google Search Engine | Predicting disease outbreaks from tweets |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Text Mining Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

Text mining follows a structured pipeline. Each step transforms the text into progressively more meaningful representations.

### Stage 1: Text Gathering (Corpus Creation)
- Collect raw documents from sources (web, databases, social media).
- The collection is called a **Corpus**.

### Stage 2: Text Preprocessing
This stage cleans the raw text. Operations include:
- **Tokenization:** Splitting text into individual words (tokens).
- **Normalization:** Converting to lowercase, removing punctuation.
- **Stop-word Removal:** Eliminating common words (e.g., "the", "a", "is").
- **Stemming:** Chopping word ends to get root form (e.g., "running" → "run").
- **Lemmatization:** Context-aware root form (e.g., "better" → "good").

### Stage 3: Feature Extraction (Text Representation)
Text must be converted into numerical vectors for algorithms. Common models:
- **Bag-of-Words (BoW):** Counts word occurrences, ignoring order.
- **TF-IDF (Term Frequency – Inverse Document Frequency):** Weights words by importance.
- **Word Embeddings:** Dense vector representations (Word2Vec, GloVe).

### Stage 4: Pattern Discovery
- Apply statistical, ML, or NLP methods (clustering, classification, topic modeling).

### Stage 5: Evaluation & Interpretation
- Measure accuracy, precision, recall, and interpret the discovered knowledge.

---

## 2.2 Information Retrieval Models

### A. Boolean Retrieval Model
- Documents and queries represented as **sets of terms**.
- Uses logical operators: **AND, OR, NOT**.
- Result: A document either matches or does not (binary relevance).
- **Limitation:** No ranking — relevant and irrelevant documents treated equally.

### B. Vector Space Model (VSM)
- Documents and queries represented as **vectors in a high-dimensional space**.
- Each dimension corresponds to a unique term.
- Relevance determined by **cosine similarity** between query and document vectors.
- **Advantage:** Provides ranked results.

### C. Probabilistic Model
- Ranks documents by **probability of relevance** to the query.
- Based on Bayes' Theorem.
- **Example:** BM25 ranking function.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes every formula tested in KTU 2024 ESE for this topic. Memorize these rigorously.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Term Frequency (TF)** | $TF(t,d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$ | Frequency of term *t* in doc *d* normalized by doc length |
| 2 | **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)** | $IDF(t) = \log\left(\frac{N}{df(t)}\right)$ | *N* = total docs; *df(t)* = docs containing term *t* |
| 3 | **TF-IDF Weight** | $W_{t,d} = TF(t,d) \times IDF(t)$ | Combined importance score |
| 4 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\cos(\theta) = \frac{A \cdot B}{\vert\vert A\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert B\vert\vert} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i B_i}{\sqrt{\sum A_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum B_i^2}}$ | Measures angle between two vectors |
| 5 | **Precision (P)** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Fraction of retrieved docs that are relevant |
| 6 | **Recall (R)** | $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Fraction of relevant docs that are retrieved |
| 7 | **F-Measure (F1 Score)** | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall |
| 8 | **Jaccard Similarity** | $J(A,B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B\vert}{\vert A \cup B\vert}$ | Set-based similarity coefficient |
| 9 | **BM25 Score** (Probabilistic) | $score(D,Q) = \sum_{i} IDF(q_i) \cdot \frac{f(q_i,D) \cdot (k_1 + 1)}{f(q_i,D) + k_1 \cdot (1 - b + b \cdot \frac{\vert D\vert}{avgdl})}$ | Modern IR ranking function |
| 10 | **Bag-of-Words Vector** | $V_d = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)$ | Document as a vector of term counts |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these systems are deployed in industry:**
> - **Search Engines** (Google, Bing) — Vector Space Model + BM25
> - **E-Commerce Recommendations** (Amazon) — TF-IDF + Cosine Similarity
> - **Healthcare NLP** — Text Mining for clinical records and drug discovery
> - **Legal Document Discovery** — Boolean + Vector IR for case-law search
> - **Social Media Monitoring** — Text Mining for brand sentiment analysis
> - **Spam Filters** — Probabilistic text classification

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Complete TF-IDF Computation + Cosine Similarity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a HIGH-PROBABILITY KTU numerical problem.** Practice this step-by-step derivation.

### Given:
A corpus of **3 documents**:
- **D1:** "the cat sat on the mat"
- **D2:** "the dog sat on the log"
- **D3:** "cat and dog are friends"

**Query (Q):** "cat dog"

---

### Step 1: Tokenization & Preprocessing

Lowercase, remove stop-words ("the", "on", "are", "and"):
- **D1:** [cat, sat, mat]
- **D2:** [dog, sat, log]
- **D3:** [cat, dog, friends]

**Unique Vocabulary (V):** {cat, sat, mat, dog, log, friends} → **6 terms**

---

### Step 2: Compute Term Frequency (TF)

Using the normalized formula $TF(t,d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\text{total terms in } d}$:

**D1 (3 terms):**
- TF(cat, D1) = 1/3 ≈ 0.333
- TF(sat, D1) = 1/3 ≈ 0.333
- TF(mat, D1) = 1/3 ≈ 0.333
- TF(dog, D1) = 0/3 = 0
- TF(log, D1) = 0
- TF(friends, D1) = 0

**D2 (3 terms):**
- TF(cat, D2) = 0
- TF(sat, D2) = 1/3 ≈ 0.333
- TF(mat, D2) = 0
- TF(dog, D2) = 1/3 ≈ 0.333
- TF(log, D2) = 1/3 ≈ 0.333
- TF(friends, D2) = 0

**D3 (3 terms):**
- TF(cat, D3) = 1/3 ≈ 0.333
- TF(sat, D3) = 0
- TF(mat, D3) = 0
- TF(dog, D3) = 1/3 ≈ 0.333
- TF(log, D3) = 0
- TF(friends, D3) = 1/3 ≈ 0.333

---

### Step 3: Compute Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)

Formula: $IDF(t) = \log\left(\frac{N}{df(t)}\right)$ where $N = 3$.

Document frequency $df(t)$ = number of documents containing term *t*:
- **cat** appears in D1, D3 → df = 2
- **sat** appears in D1, D2 → df = 2
- **mat** appears in D1 → df = 1
- **dog** appears in D2, D3 → df = 2
- **log** appears in D2 → df = 1
- **friends** appears in D3 → df = 1

$$
\begin{aligned}
IDF(\text{cat}) &= \log(3/2) = \log(1.5) \approx 0.176 \\
IDF(\text{sat}) &= \log(3/2) = \log(1.5) \approx 0.176 \\
IDF(\text{mat}) &= \log(3/1) = \log(3) \approx 0.477 \\
IDF(\text{dog}) &= \log(3/2) = \log(1.5) \approx 0.176 \\
IDF(\text{log}) &= \log(3/1) = \log(3) \approx 0.477 \\
IDF(\text{friends}) &= \log(3/1) = \log(3) \approx 0.477
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Rare words (e.g., "log", "friends" appearing in only 1 doc) get **higher** IDF, which is the intended semantic behavior.

---

### Step 4: Compute TF-IDF Weight $W_{t,d} = TF(t,d) \times IDF(t)$

**Document Vectors (TF-IDF):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D1 &= [0.333 \times 0.176,\ 0.333 \times 0.176,\ 0.333 \times 0.477,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0] \\
   &= [0.0587,\ 0.0587,\ 0.1590,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0] \\
\\
D2 &= [0,\ 0.0587,\ 0,\ 0.0587,\ 0.1590,\ 0] \\
   &= [0,\ 0.0587,\ 0,\ 0.0587,\ 0.1590,\ 0] \\
\\
D3 &= [0.0587,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0.0587,\ 0,\ 0.1590] \\
   &= [0.0587,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0.0587,\ 0,\ 0.1590]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Query Vector (Q = "cat dog"):**
- TF(cat, Q) = 1/2 = 0.5, TF(dog, Q) = 1/2 = 0.5, others = 0.
- $Q_{tfidf} = [0.5 \times 0.176,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0.5 \times 0.176,\ 0,\ 0] = [0.088,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0.088,\ 0,\ 0]$

---

### Step 5: Compute Cosine Similarity between Q and each D

Formula: $\cos(\theta) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i B_i}{\sqrt{\sum A_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum B_i^2}}$

**Similarity(Q, D1):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= (0.088 \times 0.0587) + (0.088 \times 0) + \cdots = 0.0052 \\
\text{Denom}_Q &= \sqrt{0.088^2 + 0.088^2} = \sqrt{0.0155} \approx 0.1244 \\
\text{Denom}_{D1} &= \sqrt{0.0587^2 + 0.0587^2 + 0.1590^2} = \sqrt{0.0339} \approx 0.1842 \\
\cos(Q,D1) &= \frac{0.0052}{0.1244 \times 0.1842} = \frac{0.0052}{0.0229} \approx 0.227
\end{aligned}
$$

**Similarity(Q, D2):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 0.088 \times 0 + 0.088 \times 0.0587 = 0.0052 \\
\cos(Q,D2) &= \frac{0.0052}{0.1244 \times 0.1842} \approx 0.227
\end{aligned}
$$

**Similarity(Q, D3):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 0.088 \times 0.0587 + 0.088 \times 0.0587 = 0.0052 + 0.0052 = 0.0104 \\
\text{Denom}_{D3} &= \sqrt{0.0587^2 + 0.0587^2 + 0.1590^2} \approx 0.1842 \\
\cos(Q,D3) &= \frac{0.0104}{0.1244 \times 0.1842} = \frac{0.0104}{0.0229} \approx 0.454
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 6: Final Ranking

| Rank | Document | Cosine Similarity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **D3** | 0.454 |
| 2 | D1 | 0.227 (tied) |
| 2 | D2 | 0.227 (tied) |

> [!NOTE]
> **D3 ranks highest** because it contains **both** query terms ("cat" AND "dog") — a logical retrieval outcome.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Confusion Matrix for IR Evaluation

| | **Relevant (Actual)** | **Not Relevant (Actual)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Retrieved** | True Positive (TP) | False Positive (FP) |
| **Not Retrieved** | False Negative (FN) | True Negative (TN) |

**Worked Example (Precision/Recall):**
- Suppose a search returns 50 documents.
- Of these, 40 are relevant (TP=40, FP=10).
- The corpus contains 100 relevant documents total (FN=60).
- Then $P = \frac{40}{50} = 0.80$ (80%), $R = \frac{40}{100} = 0.40$ (40%).
- $F_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.80 \times 0.40}{0.80 + 0.40} = \frac{0.64}{1.20} \approx 0.533$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Text Mining Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Corpus] --> B[Text Gathering]
    B --> C[Tokenization]
    C --> D[Stop-word Removal]
    D --> E[Stemming and Lemmatization]
    E --> F[Feature Extraction]
    F --> F1[Bag of Words]
    F --> F2[TF IDF Vectors]
    F --> F3[Word Embeddings]
    F1 --> G[Pattern Discovery]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> G1[Classification]
    G --> G2[Clustering]
    G --> G3[Topic Modeling]
    G1 --> H[Knowledge Output]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Evaluation Metrics]
    I --> J[Precision Recall F1]
```

**Explanation of Nodes:**
- **A → B:** Raw unstructured text is collected into a corpus.
- **C → E:** The cleaning/preprocessing chain.
- **F → F1/F2/F3:** Branching into three feature representation methods.
- **G → G1/G2/G3:** Three primary pattern discovery paradigms.
- **H → I → J:** Output evaluation using standard IR metrics.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Information Retrieval System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[User Query] --> QP[Query Processor]
    QP --> QR[Query Representation Vector]
    DC[Document Collection] --> DI[Document Indexer]
    DI --> DR[Document Representation Vectors]
    QR --> SM[Similarity Matching Engine]
    DR --> SM
    SM --> RL[Ranked Document List]
    RL --> RES[Results to User]
    SM -.-> FB[Relevance Feedback]
    FB -.-> QP
```

**Explanation of Nodes:**
- **U → QP → QR:** The user's query is parsed and converted into a numerical vector.
- **DC → DI → DR:** Documents in the collection are pre-indexed into vectors offline.
- **QR + DR → SM:** Vectors are compared via cosine similarity or BM25.
- **SM → RL → RES:** Results are ranked and returned.
- **FB (dashed line):** Optional relevance feedback loop for query refinement.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: IR Model Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IR Models] --> B[Boolean Model]
    A --> C[Vector Space Model]
    A --> D[Probabilistic Model]
    B --> B1[Uses AND OR NOT]
    B --> B2[Binary Relevance]
    B --> B3[No Ranking]
    C --> C1[Document as Vector]
    C --> C2[Cosine Similarity]
    C --> C3[Ranked Output]
    D --> D1[Bayes Theorem]
    D --> D2[Probability of Relevance]
    D --> D3[BM25 Algorithm]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Text Mining. List any four major applications of text mining.**

**Model Answer:**
**Text Mining** is the computational process of extracting high-quality, meaningful, and previously unknown information from unstructured textual data using techniques from Natural Language Processing (NLP), statistics, and machine learning.

**Four Major Applications:**
1. **Sentiment Analysis** — detecting opinions in social media, reviews.
2. **Spam Detection** — classifying emails as spam/legitimate.
3. **Document Summarization** — generating concise summaries of long documents.
4. **Biomedical Text Mining** — extracting drug-disease relationships from medical literature.
5. *(Bonus)* **Search Engines** — Google, Bing query-document matching.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Information Retrieval (IR) and Text Mining (TM).**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Information Retrieval | Text Mining |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Locate relevant documents for a query | Discover hidden patterns and knowledge in text |
| **Input** | User query + document collection | Whole text collection (no query needed) |
| **Output** | Ranked list of relevant documents | Patterns, trends, summaries, associations |
| **Methodology** | Indexing, ranking (VSM, Boolean, BM25) | NLP, ML, clustering, classification |
| **Example** | Google search results | Predicting stock market from news sentiment |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Module 4 provides an internal choice. Both Question A and Question B are presented; the student answers **one**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**Consider a document collection with 5 documents containing the following terms:**

- D1: "data mining techniques"
- D2: "machine learning models"
- D3: "data science and mining"
- D4: "deep learning networks"
- D5: "data analysis and learning"

**For the query Q = "data mining", compute:**

#### (a) **Compute the TF-IDF vectors for all 5 documents and the query.** [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Preprocess — lowercase, no stop-words here (none present).**

**Step 2: Build vocabulary:** {data, mining, techniques, machine, learning, models, science, and, deep, networks, analysis}
→ 11 unique terms.

**Step 3: TF values (raw counts per document):**

| Term | D1 | D2 | D3 | D4 | D5 | df |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| data | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 3 |
| mining | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 2 |
| techniques | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| machine | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| learning | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 3 |
| models | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| science | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| and | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| deep | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| networks | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| analysis | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |

**Step 4: IDF = log(N/df), N = 5:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IDF(\text{data}) &= \log(5/3) \approx 0.222 \\
IDF(\text{mining}) &= \log(5/2) \approx 0.398 \\
IDF(\text{techniques}) &= \log(5/1) \approx 0.699 \\
IDF(\text{machine}) &= \log(5/1) \approx 0.699 \\
IDF(\text{learning}) &= \log(5/3) \approx 0.222 \\
IDF(\text{models}) &= \log(5/1) \approx 0.699 \\
IDF(\text{science}) &= \log(5/1) \approx 0.699 \\
IDF(\text{and}) &= \log(5/2) \approx 0.398 \\
IDF(\text{deep}) &= \log(5/1) \approx 0.699 \\
IDF(\text{networks}) &= \log(5/1) \approx 0.699 \\
IDF(\text{analysis}) &= \log(5/1) \approx 0.699
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: TF-IDF for each document** (raw TF × IDF, only nonzero entries shown):

- **D1:** [data=0.222, mining=0.398, techniques=0.699, others=0]
- **D2:** [machine=0.699, learning=0.222, models=0.699, others=0]
- **D3:** [data=0.222, mining=0.398, science=0.699, and=0.398, others=0]
- **D4:** [learning=0.222, deep=0.699, networks=0.699, others=0]
- **D5:** [data=0.222, learning=0.222, and=0.398, analysis=0.699, others=0]

**Step 6: Query Q = "data mining":**
- $TF(\text{data},Q) = 0.5$, $TF(\text{mining},Q) = 0.5$
- $Q = [\text{data}=0.111, \text{mining}=0.199, \text{others}=0]$

#### (b) **Using the Vector Space Model, compute the cosine similarity between the query Q and each document. Rank the documents.** [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Magnitudes**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert\vert D1\vert\vert &= \sqrt{0.222^2 + 0.398^2 + 0.699^2} = \sqrt{0.049 + 0.158 + 0.489} = \sqrt{0.696} \approx 0.834 \\
\vert\vert D3\vert\vert &= \sqrt{0.222^2 + 0.398^2 + 0.699^2 + 0.398^2} = \sqrt{0.696 + 0.158} = \sqrt{0.854} \approx 0.924 \\
\vert\vert D5\vert\vert &= \sqrt{0.222^2 + 0.222^2 + 0.398^2 + 0.699^2} = \sqrt{0.049 + 0.049 + 0.158 + 0.489} = \sqrt{0.745} \approx 0.863 \\
\vert\vert Q\vert\vert &= \sqrt{0.111^2 + 0.199^2} = \sqrt{0.0123 + 0.0396} = \sqrt{0.0519} \approx 0.228
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Cosine Similarity** = $\frac{D \cdot Q}{\vert\vert D\vert\vert \times \vert\vert Q\vert\vert}$

- $\cos(Q, D1) = \frac{(0.222 \times 0.111) + (0.398 \times 0.199)}{0.834 \times 0.228} = \frac{0.0246 + 0.0792}{0.1902} = \frac{0.1038}{0.1902} \approx 0.546$
- $\cos(Q, D3) = \frac{(0.222 \times 0.111) + (0.398 \times 0.199)}{0.924 \times 0.228} = \frac{0.1038}{0.2107} \approx 0.493$
- $\cos(Q, D5) = \frac{(0.222 \times 0.111)}{0.863 \times 0.228} = \frac{0.0246}{0.1968} \approx 0.125$
- $\cos(Q, D2) = 0$, $\cos(Q, D4) = 0$ (no overlap)

**Step 3: Final Ranking**

| Rank | Document | Cosine Similarity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **D1** | 0.546 |
| 2 | D3 | 0.493 |
| 3 | D5 | 0.125 |
| 4 | D2 | 0.000 |
| 4 | D4 | 0.000 |

> [!NOTE]
> **D1 ranks highest** — it contains **both** query terms ("data" + "mining") and has a smaller magnitude penalty compared to D3.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**(a) Explain the Vector Space Model for Information Retrieval with a neat diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations.** [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

The **Vector Space Model (VSM)** represents documents and queries as vectors in a multi-dimensional space, where each dimension corresponds to a unique term in the corpus.

**Working Principle:**
1. Each document $D$ is represented as $D = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)$ where $w_i$ is the weight (e.g., TF-IDF) of term $i$.
2. Each query $Q$ is represented similarly.
3. The relevance of $D$ to $Q$ is the **cosine similarity** between their vectors.
4. Documents are ranked in descending order of similarity.

**Diagram (Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2):**
The VSM workflow involves: User Query → Query Processor → Vector Representation → Similarity Matching Engine → Ranked Document List.

**Advantages:**
- **Partial matching** — documents with some matching terms are still retrieved.
- **Ranked output** — provides a relevance ordering, unlike Boolean model.
- **Mathematical rigor** — well-defined similarity measures.
- **Weighting flexibility** — supports TF-IDF and other schemes.

**Limitations:**
- **Assumes term independence** — ignores semantic relationships (e.g., "car" vs "automobile").
- **High dimensionality** — sparse vectors for large vocabularies.
- **No phrase/word-order sensitivity** — "data mining" vs "mining data" treated identically.
- **Scalability issues** — cosine similarity is O(n) per comparison.

---

**(b) Suppose an IR system retrieves 80 documents for a query. Of these, 60 are relevant. The corpus contains 100 relevant documents in total. Compute Precision, Recall, and F-Measure.** [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Retrieved = 80, Relevant Retrieved = 60 → **TP = 60**, **FP = 80 − 60 = 20**
- Total relevant in corpus = 100 → **FN = 100 − 60 = 40**

**Step 1: Precision**
$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{60}{60 + 20} = \frac{60}{80} = 0.75 \quad (75\%)
$$

**Step 2: Recall**
$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{60}{60 + 40} = \frac{60}{100} = 0.60 \quad (60\%)
$$

**Step 3: F-Measure (F1 Score)**
$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.75 \times 0.60}{0.75 + 0.60} = \frac{0.90}{1.35} \approx 0.667
$$

**Final Result:** $P = 0.75$, $R = 0.60$, $F_1 \approx 0.667$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The system is good at precision (75% of retrieved docs are relevant) but misses 40% of relevant documents — recall could be improved.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Confusing TF and TF-IDF** — TF is a *raw count or normalized frequency*; TF-IDF multiplies TF by IDF. Examiners check if you used the right formula.
> 2. **Forgetting to normalize** in cosine similarity — always divide by $\vert\vert A\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert B\vert\vert$, not by raw sums.
> 3. **Sign errors in IDF** — if $N < df(t)$, IDF becomes negative, which is mathematically invalid; ensure $N \geq df(t)$.
> 4. **Mixing up Precision and Recall formulas** — Precision uses $TP + FP$ (denominator), Recall uses $TP + FN$ (denominator). Mnemonic: **P**recision → **P**redicted (retrieved) positives.
> 5. **Skipping steps in numericals** — KTU examiners award **step marks** even if the final answer is wrong. Always show: Preprocessing → TF → IDF → TF-IDF → Similarity.
> 6. **Not drawing the diagram** in VSM questions — a neat block diagram fetches at least 2 marks.
> 7. **Using base-10 log without stating** — specify whether you used natural log ($\ln$) or $\log_{10}$ for IDF (it doesn't matter for ranking, but the numerical values change).
> 8. **Forgetting to lowercase and remove punctuation** in preprocessing — examiners often embed punctuation traps in TF-IDF numericals.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Text Mining & IR (Module 4):**

- **Text Mining** = Knowledge discovery from unstructured text (subfield of data mining).
- **Information Retrieval (IR)** = Finding relevant documents for a query.
- **Core Pipeline:** Corpus → Preprocessing → Feature Extraction → Pattern Discovery → Evaluation.
- **Preprocessing Steps:** Tokenization → Stop-word Removal → Stemming → Lemmatization.
- **Feature Models:** Bag-of-Words (counts), TF-IDF (weighted), Word Embeddings (dense).
- **TF Formula:** $TF(t,d) = f_{t,d} / \text{(sum of all term freqs in } d\text{)}$
- **IDF Formula:** $IDF(t) = \log(N / df(t))$
- **TF-IDF = TF × IDF** (a high TF-IDF means the term is frequent in this document but rare across the corpus).
- **IR Models:** Boolean (AND/OR/NOT, no ranking), Vector Space (cosine similarity, ranked), Probabilistic (BM25, probability of relevance).
- **Cosine Similarity:** $\cos\theta = \frac{A \cdot B}{\vert\vert A\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert B\vert\vert}$ → ranges from 0 (orthogonal/unrelated) to 1 (identical).
- **Precision** = TP / (TP + FP) → accuracy among retrieved.
- **Recall** = TP / (TP + FN) → coverage of relevant items.
- **F1 Score** = Harmonic mean of P and R; **use F1** (not arithmetic mean) because it penalizes extreme imbalances.
- **Confusion Matrix:** Rows = Predicted/Retrieved; Columns = Actual/Relevant.
- **Real-world systems:** Google Search (BM25), Amazon Recommendations (TF-IDF + cosine), Spam Filters (Naive Bayes), Sentiment Analysis (Text Classification).
- **Jaccard Similarity** = $\vert A \cap B\vert / \vert A \cup B\vert$ — useful for set-based comparison.
- **Boolean model limitations:** no partial match, no ranking, returns only exact matches.
- **VSM advantage over Boolean:** supports partial matching and ranked output.
- **Common numerical trap:** always include the zero-weight terms in vectors to keep dimension consistent for cosine similarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Text Retrieval** is the sub-discipline of Information Retrieval (IR) concerned with locating and ranking textual documents (web pages, research papers, news articles, etc.) that are relevant to a user's information need, expressed as a **query**. To evaluate the effectiveness of any text retrieval system, we use a family of **basic measures** — primarily **Precision**, **Recall**, **F-measure**, and **E-measure** — that quantify how well the system balances two competing goals: *returning everything useful* and *avoiding returning junk*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECST525 – Module 4):**
> The basic measures for text retrieval form the evaluation backbone of **Pattern Discovery, Classification, and Clustering** evaluation. The same mathematics of Precision, Recall, and F-measure re-appears in **Confusion Matrix evaluation** of classifiers (Decision Trees, Naïve Bayes, k-NN), making this topic a *cross-cutting evaluative skill*.

### 1.2 The Universe of Documents — Set Theoretic View

Let:
- **R** = the set of **Relevant** documents in the entire collection (the *ground truth*).
- **A** = the set of documents **Answered / Retrieved** by the system.
- **R ∩ A** = the set of documents that are both relevant AND retrieved (a.k.a. **Hits / True Positives**).

The universe of documents partitions into four disjoint regions:

| Region | Meaning | Set Notation | Standard Name |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hit | Relevant AND Retrieved | $R \cap A$ | **True Positive (TP)** |
| Miss | Relevant BUT NOT Retrieved | $R \setminus A$ | **False Negative (FN)** |
| False Alarm | Retrieved BUT NOT Relevant | $A \setminus R$ | **False Positive (FP)** |
| Correct Rejection | Neither Relevant NOR Retrieved | $\overline{R} \cap \overline{A}$ | **True Negative (TN)** |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Fishing Net"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Fishing in a Lake**
> Imagine a lake containing 1000 fish. Only 100 of them are the **Salmon** you want. You cast a net and catch 80 fish. Of these 80, only 60 turn out to be Salmon.
> - **Precision** asks: *"Of the fish I caught, how many are the ones I actually wanted?"* → 60/80 = 75%. (Purity of the catch.)
> - **Recall** asks: *"Of all the Salmon swimming in the lake, how many did I manage to catch?"* → 60/100 = 60%. (Coverage of the catch.)
> A perfect fisherman has **Precision = 1.0** *and* **Recall = 1.0** — every Salmon in the lake, and *only* Salmon, are in the net. In practice, the larger the net, the higher the recall but the lower the precision — this is the famous **Precision-Recall Trade-off**.

### 1.4 Engineering Significance

In real engineering systems, these measures are used to evaluate:
- **Web Search Engines** (Google, Bing) — relevance of top-10 results.
- **Legal E-Discovery Tools** — recall is critical (missing a relevant document is a lawsuit).
- **Medical Information Systems** — both precision and recall are critical (wrong drug info = death).
- **Spam Filters** — high precision preferred (don't drop real mail), moderate recall acceptable.
- **Data Mining Classifiers** — exactly the same TP/FP/FN/FN framework on a test set.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Fundamental Quantities

Given the set partition above, every basic measure is built from the cardinalities of the four regions. We denote:
- $a = \vert R \cap A \vert$ — number of hits (TP)
- $b = \vert A \setminus R \vert$ — number of false alarms (FP)
- $c = \vert R \setminus A \vert$ — number of misses (FN)
- $d = \vert \overline{R} \cap \overline{A} \vert$ — number of correct rejections (TN)

The total collection size is $N = a + b + c + d$.

### 2.2 Precision (P) — The "Purity" Metric

$$\text{Precision} = P = \frac{\text{Number of relevant documents retrieved}}{\text{Total number of documents retrieved}} = \frac{a}{a + b}$$

**Range:** $[0, 1]$. **Goal:** Maximize. **Engineering intuition:** "When my system says YES, how often is it right?"

### 2.3 Recall (R) — The "Coverage" Metric

$$\text{Recall} = R = \frac{\text{Number of relevant documents retrieved}}{\text{Total number of relevant documents in the collection}} = \frac{a}{a + c}$$

**Range:** $[0, 1]$. **Goal:** Maximize. **Engineering intuition:** "Of all the gold in the ground, how much did my detector find?"

> [!NOTE]
> **Why both?**
> A system can trivially achieve **Recall = 1.0** by retrieving *every* document in the collection — but then **Precision → 0**. Conversely, retrieving just the single most confident document can yield **Precision = 1.0** but terrible recall. The art of text retrieval is balancing these two.

### 2.4 F-Measure (F-Score / F1-Score) — The Single-Summary Metric

The **F-measure** is the **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall, giving a single number that rewards systems that do well on *both*.

$$F = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}$$

**Range:** $[0, 1]$. **Goal:** Maximize. **F = 1** only when P = R = 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Harmonic Mean, not Arithmetic Mean?**
> The harmonic mean is **punishing** to imbalanced scores. Example: $P = 1.0, R = 0.1$.
> - Arithmetic mean = 0.55 (looks decent).
> - Harmonic mean = **0.18** (correctly reveals the system is broken).
> This is exactly why KTU examiners and TREC (Text REtrieval Conference) use F-measure.

### 2.5 The Generalized F_β Measure — Asymmetric Importance

In some applications, Precision matters more than Recall (spam filter) or vice-versa (cancer detection). The parameter $\beta$ controls the trade-off:

$$F_{\beta} = \frac{(1 + \beta^{2}) \cdot P \cdot R}{\beta^{2} \cdot P + R}$$

- $\beta = 1$ → equal weight → **F1** (standard F-measure).
- $\beta > 1$ → **Recall is weighted more** (e.g., $\beta = 2$ emphasizes recall).
- $\beta < 1$ → **Precision is weighted more** (e.g., $\beta = 0.5$ emphasizes precision).

### 2.6 E-Measure (Van Rijsbergen's Effectiveness Measure)

Proposed by **C. J. van Rijsbergen** in 1979, the **E-measure** is a parameterized complement of F-measure, allowing explicit control over the relative importance $\alpha$ of Precision vs. Recall:

$$E = 1 - F_{\beta} \quad \text{where} \quad \beta^{2} = \frac{1 - \alpha}{\alpha}, \;\; \alpha \in [0, 1]$$

Equivalently:

$$E(\alpha) = 1 - \frac{1}{\frac{\alpha}{P} + \frac{1 - \alpha}{R}}$$

- $\alpha = 0.5$ → E reduces to $1 - F_1$.
- $\alpha \to 1$ → user cares only about Precision.
- $\alpha \to 0$ → user cares only about Recall.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Many KTU question papers ask students to *derive* E from F or to compute E for a given $\alpha$. Always convert $\alpha \to \beta$ first using $\beta^2 = (1-\alpha)/\alpha$.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Formula Card (Print & Memorize):**

| # | Measure | Formula | Range | Goal | Real-world Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Precision (P)** | $\dfrac{a}{a+b}$ | $[0,1]$ | $\uparrow$ Maximize | Search-result quality |
| 2 | **Recall (R)** | $\dfrac{a}{a+c}$ | $[0,1]$ | $\uparrow$ Maximize | Legal/medical completeness |
| 3 | **F-Measure (F1)** | $\dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$ | $[0,1]$ | $\uparrow$ Maximize | Single summary metric |
| 4 | **F$_β$-Measure** | $\dfrac{(1+\beta^{2})PR}{\beta^{2}P+R}$ | $[0,1]$ | $\uparrow$ Maximize | Asymmetric weighting |
| 5 | **E-Measure** | $1 - F_{\beta}$, $\beta^{2}=\frac{1-\alpha}{\alpha}$ | $[0,1]$ | $\downarrow$ Minimize | Van Rijsbergen's metric |
| 6 | **Accuracy** | $\dfrac{a+d}{a+b+c+d}$ | $[0,1]$ | $\uparrow$ Maximize | Classifier correctness |
| 7 | **Error Rate** | $1 - \text{Accuracy}$ | $[0,1]$ | $\downarrow$ Minimize | Classifier mistakes |
| 8 | **Avg Precision (AP)** | $\dfrac{1}{R}\sum_{k=1}^{N} P(k) \cdot \text{rel}(k)$ | $[0,1]$ | $\uparrow$ Maximize | Ranked retrieval |
| 9 | **Mean Avg Precision (MAP)** | $\dfrac{1}{\vert Q \vert}\sum_{q=1}^{\vert Q \vert} AP(q)$ | $[0,1]$ | $\uparrow$ Maximize | TREC benchmark |

### 2.8 The Precision-Recall Trade-off Curve

When a retrieval system returns a **ranked list** of documents (e.g., by TF-IDF score), we can plot **Precision on the y-axis** vs. **Recall on the x-axis** as we walk down the ranked list. A perfect system hugs the **top-right corner** (P = 1, R = 1). The **area under this curve (AUC-PR)** is a widely-used single-number summary of system quality.

**Interpolated Precision** at recall level $r$ is defined as:

$$P_{\text{interp}}(r) = \max \{ P(r') : r' \ge r \}$$

This smooths the curve and is the basis of the **11-point interpolated precision** used in TREC.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: F-Measure as the Special Case of E-Measure

**Claim:** $F_1 = 1 - E$ when $\alpha = 0.5$.

**Step 1.** Start from the E-measure definition:

$$E(\alpha) = 1 - \frac{1}{\frac{\alpha}{P} + \frac{1-\alpha}{R}}$$

**Step 2.** Substitute $\alpha = 0.5$:

$$E(0.5) = 1 - \frac{1}{\frac{0.5}{P} + \frac{0.5}{R}} = 1 - \frac{1}{0.5 \left( \frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R} \right)}$$

**Step 3.** Simplify the denominator:

$$E(0.5) = 1 - \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}} = 1 - \frac{2PR}{R + P}$$

**Step 4.** Recognize the F-measure formula:

$$\frac{2PR}{P + R} = F_1 \quad \therefore \quad E(0.5) = 1 - F_1$$

**Conclusion:** F-measure is mathematically the *complement* of E-measure at the symmetric point $\alpha = 0.5$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: F_β as Weighted Harmonic Mean

**Claim:** $F_\beta$ weights Recall $\beta$ times more heavily than Precision.

**Step 1.** The general weighted harmonic mean of two positive numbers $x, y$ with weights $w_x, w_y$ (summing to 1) is:

$$H = \frac{1}{\frac{w_x}{x} + \frac{w_y}{y}}$$

**Step 2.** Choose weights so that Recall gets weight $\beta$ relative to Precision:

$$w_R = \frac{\beta}{1+\beta}, \qquad w_P = \frac{1}{1+\beta}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute into harmonic mean with $x = P, y = R$:

$$F = \frac{1}{\frac{w_P}{P} + \frac{w_R}{R}} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{(1+\beta)P} + \frac{\beta}{(1+\beta)R}}$$

**Step 4.** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1+\beta)PR$:

$$F = \frac{(1+\beta)PR}{R + \beta P} = \frac{(1+\beta)}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{\beta}{R}} = \frac{(1+\beta)PR}{\beta P + R}$$

**Step 5.** Square the weight factor to match the standard F_β convention (gives nice symmetry):

$$F_{\beta} = \frac{(1+\beta^{2})PR}{\beta^{2}P + R}$$

**Conclusion:** The exponent 2 on $\beta$ is *purely a convention* chosen so that $F_1$ recovers the standard F1. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Full KTU-Style Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A text retrieval system is queried about the topic *"renewable energy"*. The collection contains **120 documents** in total, of which **30** are genuinely relevant. The system retrieves **40 documents**, and an independent human judge confirms that **20** of these 40 are actually relevant. Calculate: (a) Precision, (b) Recall, (c) F1-Measure, (d) F2-Measure, (e) E-measure with $\alpha = 0.25$.

**Step 1 — Identify the four cardinalities.**

We are given: $a + b + c + d = 120$, $a + c = 30$, $a + b = 40$, $a = 20$.

Solving the system:
- $a = 20$ (relevant AND retrieved)
- $b = (a+b) - a = 40 - 20 = 20$ (retrieved but not relevant)
- $c = (a+c) - a = 30 - 20 = 10$ (relevant but not retrieved)
- $d = 120 - (a+b+c) = 120 - 50 = 70$ (neither relevant nor retrieved)

**Step 2 — Compute Precision.**

$$P = \frac{a}{a + b} = \frac{20}{20 + 20} = \frac{20}{40} = 0.50$$

**Step 3 — Compute Recall.**

$$R = \frac{a}{a + c} = \frac{20}{20 + 10} = \frac{20}{30} = 0.6667$$

**Step 4 — Compute F1-Measure.**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 P R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.50 \times 0.6667}{0.50 + 0.6667} = \frac{0.6667}{1.1667} = 0.5714$$

**Step 5 — Compute F2-Measure (Recall-weighted).**

$$F_2 = \frac{(1 + 2^{2}) P R}{2^{2} P + R} = \frac{5 \times 0.50 \times 0.6667}{4 \times 0.50 + 0.6667} = \frac{1.6667}{2.6667} = 0.6250$$

Note that $F_2 > F_1$ because Recall is higher than Precision — the F2 metric rewards that imbalance.

**Step 6 — Compute E-measure with $\alpha = 0.25$.**

First, convert $\alpha$ to $\beta$:

$$\beta^{2} = \frac{1 - \alpha}{\alpha} = \frac{1 - 0.25}{0.25} = \frac{0.75}{0.25} = 3 \quad \therefore \quad \beta = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$$

Then compute:

$$E = 1 - \frac{1}{\frac{\alpha}{P} + \frac{1-\alpha}{R}} = 1 - \frac{1}{\frac{0.25}{0.50} + \frac{0.75}{0.6667}}$$

$$E = 1 - \frac{1}{0.5 + 1.125} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.625} = 1 - 0.6154 = 0.3846$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Since $\alpha = 0.25$ means we care more about Precision (the user gave Precision a quarter weight, Recall three-quarters), and our Precision (0.50) is *lower* than our Recall (0.6667), the E-measure should penalize us. $E = 0.3846$ is indeed high, confirming the penalty.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Measure

| Scenario | Best Measure | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Web search engine top-10 | **Precision@k** | User only looks at first page; false positives are costly. |
| Legal e-discovery (must find *all* evidence) | **Recall** | Missing a document = lawsuit. Precision less critical. |
| Cancer screening test | **Recall** | Missing a tumor is catastrophic. False alarms lead to more tests, not death. |
| Spam filter (don't drop real email) | **Precision** | Better to let some spam through than block a customer's invoice. |
| Single-number benchmark for an algorithm | **F1** or **MAP** | Symmetric or rank-aware summary metric. |
| Asymmetric user preference | **F$_β$** or **E-measure** | Explicit $\beta$ or $\alpha$ encodes preference. |
| Imbalanced classes (e.g., fraud = 0.1%) | **F1** (not Accuracy) | A naive "all-yes" classifier has 99.9% accuracy but 0% recall on fraud. |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Set-Theoretic Foundation of the Four Cardinalities

The following diagram shows the document universe partitioned into the four regions from which Precision and Recall are constructed. Documents in the outer rectangle are the entire collection; the left circle is the *Relevant* set, the right circle is the *Retrieved (Answered)* set.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Collection N documents]:::outerBox
    B[Relevant Set R]:::relevantSet
    C[Retrieved Set A]:::retrievedSet
    D[TP Hit a]:::hit
    E[FP False Alarm b]:::falseAlarm
    F[FN Miss c]:::miss
    G[TN Correct Rejection d]:::correctRejection

    A --> B
    A --> C
    B --- D
    B --- F
    C --- D
    C --- E
    A --- G

    classDef outerBox fill:#E8E8E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef relevantSet fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef retrievedSet fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hit fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef falseAlarm fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef miss fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef correctRejection fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#616161,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The yellow region (Hit / TP) is the **numerator** for *both* Precision and Recall. The red region (FP) is the denominator addition for Precision. The orange region (FN) is the denominator addition for Recall. The grey region (TN) never appears in P or R, but does appear in Accuracy.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Text Retrieval Evaluation Pipeline

The following Mermaid flowchart models the end-to-end logical architecture of how a text retrieval system is evaluated using the basic measures. It maps the data flow from raw query input through ranking, human-judged relevance sets, and finally the metric computation block.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    queryStart[User Query Q]:::input
    corpusIn[Document Collection N]:::input
    retEngine[Retrieval Engine Indexing and Ranking]:::engine
    retOut[Retrieved Set A size a+b]:::output
    relJudge[Human Judge or Ground Truth Trec Qrels]:::engine
    relSet[Relevant Set R size a+c]:::output
    interBlock[Intersection R intersection A yields a Hits]:::core
    metricBlock[Metric Computation Engine]:::core
    pOut[Precision equals a over a+b]:::metric
    rOut[Recall equals a over a+c]:::metric
    fOut[F Measure equals 2PR over P+R]:::metric
    eOut[E Measure equals 1 minus F beta]:::metric
    reportOut[Final Evaluation Report]:::report

    queryStart --> retEngine
    corpusIn --> retEngine
    retEngine --> retOut
    relJudge --> relSet
    retOut --> interBlock
    relSet --> interBlock
    interBlock --> metricBlock
    metricBlock --> pOut
    metricBlock --> rOut
    metricBlock --> fOut
    metricBlock --> eOut
    pOut --> reportOut
    rOut --> reportOut
    fOut --> reportOut
    eOut --> reportOut

    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef engine fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef metric fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef report fill:#CFD8DC,stroke:#263238,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Precision-Recall Trade-off Curve (Conceptual Schematic)

The following diagram is a block-level representation of a Precision-Recall curve as a system slides its retrieval threshold from "return nothing" to "return everything." The curve never increases monotonically — as we retrieve more documents, recall increases but precision typically drops.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P_axis[Precision Axis Y from 0 to 1]:::axis
    R_axis[Recall Axis X from 0 to 1]:::axis
    P1[Point k=1 Highest P low R]:::point
    P2[Point k=N Halfway P Halfway R]:::point
    P3[Point k=All Low P R equals 1]:::point
    Ideal[Ideal Point P=1 R=1 unreachable]:::ideal
    Curve[Descending Stepwise Precision Recall Curve]:::curve

    P_axis --- P1
    P1 --- P2
    P2 --- P3
    R_axis --- P3
    P3 -.-> Curve
    P2 -.-> Curve
    P1 -.-> Curve
    Ideal --- P1

    classDef axis fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef point fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ideal fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef curve fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **area under the Precision-Recall curve (AUC-PR)** is a single scalar quality score. A system whose curve *dominates* another (lies above and to the right) is strictly better at *every* recall level. This dominance ordering is the basis of the **TREC benchmark** used internationally.
